EDCS-26600339.summary: This votive inscription is dedicated to Silvanus Domesticus by Felicissimus, a steward, for the well-being of himself and his family, likely dating to the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-26600340.text_edition: \n Veneri Victrici Augustae sacrum Eburus ex iussu deae\n
EDCS-26600340.translation: Sacred to Venus Victrix Augusta. Eburus, by order of the goddess.
EDCS-26600340.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Venus Victrix Augusta by Eburus, following a divine command, likely dating to the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-26600341.text_edition: \n Signum Victoriae Quintus Gavius Maximus Quintus Tor Priscianus maggistri collegii Genii provinciae Pannoniae superioris Publius Aelius Exuperatus et Titus Aurelius Peculiaris scribae collegii donum dederunt Modesto II et Probo consulibus\n
EDCS-26600341.translation: Quintus Gavius Maximus and Quintus Tor Priscianus, masters of the college of the Genius of the province of Pannonia Superior, and Publius Aelius Exuperatus and Titus Aurelius Peculiaris, scribes of the college, dedicated this statue of Victory in the consulship of Modestus II and Probus.
EDCS-26600341.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a statue of Victory by officials of the college of the Genius of Pannonia Superior, dated to the consulship of Modestus II and Probus (likely 228 CE).

EDCS-26600342.text_edition: \n August-- sacrum Tiberius Claudius Togiorum libertus Eutychus Augustalis\n
EDCS-26600342.translation: Sacred to the Augusti. Tiberius Claudius Eutychus, freedman of Togius, the Augustalis.
EDCS-26600342.summary: This is a dedication to the deified emperors (Augusti) by Tiberius Claudius Eutychus, a freedman and Augustalis, likely dating to the 2nd-3rd century CE.

EDCS-26600344.text_edition: \n ostenso posuit Caius Alfius Luci filius Secundus veteranus legionis XV Apollinaris\n
EDCS-26600344.translation: Caius Alfius Secundus, son of Lucius, a veteran of the XV Apollinaris legion, set up [this monument] after [it was] shown.
EDCS-26600344.summary: This inscription records a veteran of the XV Apollinaris legion, Caius Alfius Secundus, setting up a monument, the exact nature of which is lost but suggests a posthumous dedication or unveiling.

EDCS-26600345.text_edition: \n Liberalis eques legionis XIIII geminae votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-26600345.translation: Liberalis, an equestrian of the XIIII Gemina legion, gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-26600345.summary: This votive inscription records a soldier, Liberalis, from the XIIII Gemina legion fulfilling a vow, likely dating to the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-26600346.text_edition: \n Liberalis veteranus legionis XIIII geminae Antoninianae votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-26600346.translation: Liberalis, a veteran of the XIIII Gemina Antoniniana legion, gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-26600346.summary: This votive inscription records Liberalis, a veteran of the XIIII Gemina Antoniniana legion, fulfilling a vow, likely dating to the 3rd century CE due to the Antoniniana epithet.

EDCS-26600349.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar divi Vespasiani filius [Domitianus] Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate imperator II consul VIII designatus VIIII pater patriae\n
EDCS-26600349.translation: The Emperor Caesar Domitianus Augustus, son of the deified Vespasian, chief priest, holding tribunician power for the [X]th time, imperator for the 2nd time, consul for the 8th time, designated consul for the 9th time, father of the country.
EDCS-26600349.summary: This is a partial titulus of Emperor Domitian, listing his official titles and powers, likely dating to the period between 81 and 96 CE.

EDCS-26600350.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar divi Vespasiani filius [Domitianus Augustus] pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate imperator II consul VIII designatus VIIII pater patriae [[]]\n
EDCS-26600350.translation: The Emperor Caesar Domitianus Augustus, son of the deified Vespasian, chief priest, holding tribunician power for the [X]th time, imperator for the 2nd time, consul for the 8th time, designated consul for the 9th time, father of the country.
EDCS-26600350.summary: This is a partial titulus of Emperor Domitian, listing his official titles and powers, likely dating to the period between 81 and 96 CE, with a lacuna at the end.

EDCS-26600353.text_edition: \n Beatitudine domini nostri Constantis victoris ac triumfatoris semper Augusti provisa copia quae horreis deerat postea quam condendis horrea deesse coeperunt haec Vulcatius Caius Rufinus vir clarissimus praefectus praetorio per se coepta in securitatem perpet<ua=E>m rei annonariae dedicavit \n
EDCS-26600353.translation: In accordance with the abundant provision of our lord Constans, victor and ever triumphant Augustus, which was lacking from the granaries after the granaries began to fail, Vulcatius Caius Rufinus, a most distinguished man, prefect of the praetorium, dedicated these [granaries] which he had begun himself, for the perpetual security of the food supply.
EDCS-26600353.summary: This inscription records the dedication of granaries for the public food supply by Vulcatius Caius Rufinus, prefect of the praetorium, likely during the reign of Emperor Constans.

EDCS-26600354.text_edition: \n Marcus Aemilius Marcellinus vivus fecit sibi et Sen Sisiatae coniugi pientissimae et Aemiliae Ursae filiae pientissimae annorum XX et Aemiliae Dignae annorum et Aemilio Venatori militi legionis XIIII geminae Gaius Iu ANV ORT G I FN OI et PE\n
EDCS-26600354.translation: Marcus Aemilius Marcellinus, while alive, made this for himself and for his most devoted wife Sen Sisiata and for his most devoted daughter Aemilia Ursa, aged 20, and for Aemilia Digna, aged [?], and for Gaius Iu [?], soldier of the 14th Gemina legion, [son] of ANV ORT G I FN OI and PE.
EDCS-26600354.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording a man, Marcus Aemilius Marcellinus, who erected it for himself, his wife, his daughters, and a soldier of the 14th Gemina legion.

EDCS-26600355.text_edition: \n Marcus Aurelius Romanus domo Antiochia veteranus legionis X geminae vivus fecit sibi et Ateriae Sabinae coniugi annorum LX et Marco Aurelio Romaniano filio et Aureliae Valeriae coniugi\n
EDCS-26600355.translation: Marcus Aurelius Romanus, from Antioch, a veteran of the 10th Gemina legion, while alive, made this for himself and for his wife Ateria Sabina, aged 60, and for his son Marcus Aurelius Romanianus, and for his wife Aurelia Valeria.
EDCS-26600355.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording a veteran, Marcus Aurelius Romanus, who erected it for himself, his wife, and his son.

EDCS-26600356.text_edition: \n Titus Cnorius Sabinianus ex praefecto alae contariorum vir egregius et Lucius Valerius Verus decurio coloniae Claudiae Savariensium sacerdotalis provinciae Pannoniae superioris cryptam vi ignis exustam sumptibus suis refecerunt\n
EDCS-26600356.translation: Titus Cnorius Sabinianus, a former prefect of the auxiliary cavalry, an outstanding man, and Lucius Valerius Verus, a decurion of the colony of Claudia Savariensium and priest of the province of Upper Pannonia, restored at their own expense the crypt which had been destroyed by fire.
EDCS-26600356.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a fire-damaged crypt by Titus Cnorius Sabinianus, a former prefect of cavalry, and Lucius Valerius Verus, a decurion and priest.

EDCS-26600357.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Comminia Valagenta annorum viva fecit sibi et Tartonio Secundino legionis XIIII geminae defuncto in expeditione inconparabili sic pietate annorum XXV mater infelicissima et Tartonio Finitiano Daiberi veterano annorum LXX et Com Optato militi legionis XIIII annorum\n
EDCS-26600357.translation: To the departed spirits. Comminia Valagenta, aged [?], while alive, made this for herself and for Tartonius Secundinus, of the 14th Gemina legion, who died on an incomparable expedition, [and] for his most unhappy mother, aged 25, and for Tartonius Finitianus Daiberi, a veteran, aged 70, and for Com Optatus, a soldier of the 14th legion, aged [?].
EDCS-26600357.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by Comminia Valagenta for herself and several deceased individuals, including a soldier of the 14th Gemina legion who died on an expedition and his mother.

EDCS-26600358.inscription_type: carmina, inscriptiones christianae, tituli operum, tituli sacri, tituli sepulcrales
EDCS-26600358.text_edition: \n Flavius Dalmatius vir perfectissimus ex protectore qui vixit annos XL et Aurelia Iulia conpar dulcissima qui sic vixit annos XXXV ideoque hunc titulum scripserunt quisquis heris sic post me dominus Laris huius et horti vicinas mihi carpe rosas mihi lilia pone candedas sic quae viridis dabit hortulus ista beatum Volussius sic et Sabatia liberti posuerunt\n
EDCS-26600358.translation: Flavius Dalmatius, a most accomplished man, from the protectores, who lived 40 years, and Aurelia Iulia, his sweetest companion, who lived 35 years, wrote this inscription for themselves; whoever, after me, becomes the master of this household and garden, let him plant my roses and place my white lilies, so that this little garden will provide these things. Volussius and Sabatia, freedmen, placed [this].
EDCS-26600358.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by freedmen Volussius and Sabatia for their deceased masters, Flavius Dalmatius and Aurelia Iulia, and includes a request for the care of their garden.

EDCS-26600359.inscription_type: inscriptiones christianae, tituli operum, tituli sepulcrales
EDCS-26600359.text_edition: \n Bene memorandae coniugi dulcissimae quae vixit annos XX menses V Flavius Pomentius Aureliae Iustinae maritus et vector nam et protector contra votum memoriam posuit\n
EDCS-26600359.translation: Flavius Pomentius placed this memorial, against his will, for his sweetest wife Aurelia Iustina, who lived 20 years and 5 months; he was her husband and also a protector.
EDCS-26600359.summary: This inscription is a memorial erected by Flavius Pomentius for his wife Aurelia Iustina, noting his roles as husband and protector.

EDCS-26600360.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Cai Iuli Iuliani veterani legionis XIIII geminae annorum LX et Tulliae Florentinae annorum Iulia Flora filia et heres faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-26600360.translation: To the departed spirits. Iulia Flora, daughter and heir, saw to the construction of this [tomb] for Caius Julius Julianus, a veteran of the 14th Gemina legion, aged 60, and for Tullia Florentina, aged [?].
EDCS-26600360.summary: This funerary epitaph records that Iulia Flora, daughter and heir, commissioned the tomb for her father, Caius Julius Julianus, a veteran of the 14th Gemina legion, and her mother Tullia Florentina.

EDCS-26600361.text_edition: \n Lucius Licinius Luci filius Claudia Lepidus natus domo Verona veteranus legionis XV Apollinaris \n
EDCS-26600361.translation: Lucius Licinius Lepidus, son of Lucius, of the Claudia tribe, born in Verona, a veteran of the 15th Apollinaris legion.
EDCS-26600361.summary: This inscription identifies Lucius Licinius Lepidus as a veteran of the 15th Apollinaris legion, originally from Verona.

EDCS-26600362.text_edition: \n Publius Maecius Publi filius Sabinus veteranus legionis XV Apollinaris domo Sergia deductus nnorum LX hic situs est testamento fieri iussit arbitratu Marci Domitiani Quinti fili CAO heredis\n
EDCS-26600362.translation: Publius Maecius Sabinus, son of Publius, of the Sergia tribe, a veteran of the 15th Apollinaris legion, from [?], aged 60, is buried here; he ordered this to be made by his will, by the arbitration of Marcus Domitius, son of Quinti CAO, his heir.
EDCS-26600362.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Publius Maecius Sabinus, a 60-year-old veteran of the 15th Apollinaris legion, who specified its construction in his will.

EDCS-26600363.text_edition: \n Maximine vivas in Deo Aurelia Hernilla coniux fecit Maximi<n=M>o centurioni caro suo bene merito virginio suo maemoriam sic posuet sic anos vixit XLV L \n
EDCS-26600363.translation: May you live in God, Maximinus. Aurelia Hernilla, his wife, made this memorial for her dear, well-deserving husband Maximinus, her kinsman. She lived 45 years.
EDCS-26600363.summary: A tombstone memorializing Aurelia Hernilla's husband, Maximinus, a centurion, who lived 45 years, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600364.text_edition: \n Lucius Valerius Luci filius Claudia Censorinus decurio coloniae Claudiae Savariae item veteranus legionis I adiutricis ex beneficiario consularis annorum vivus fecit sibi et Flaviae Insequentinae coniugi annorum LX et Valeriis Censoriniano annorum Florentino annorum Venerio annorum Veterano annorum et Censorinillae annorum filiis itemque libertis libertabusque suis\n
EDCS-26600364.translation: Lucius Valerius Censorinus, son of Lucius, of the Claudia tribe, decurion of the colony of Savaria Claudius, and a veteran of the I Adiutrix legion, serving under the *beneficiarius consularis*, made this for himself alive, and for his wife Flavia Insequentina, aged 60, and for his children Valerius Censorinianus, aged [?], Florentinus, aged [?], Venerius, aged [?], Veteranus, aged [?], and Censorinilla, aged [?], and likewise for his freedmen and freedwomen.
EDCS-26600364.summary: A funerary monument set up by Lucius Valerius Censorinus, a military veteran and local official in Savaria, for himself, his wife, children, and freedmen, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600367.text_edition: \n Quintus Mullius Sexti filius Claudia Florentinus annorum vivus fecit sibi et Caenatiustae coniugi annorum et Quinto Mullio Marcello seviro coloniae Claudiae Savariae annorum XX et Mulliae Marcellinae filio nepoti\n
EDCS-26600367.translation: Quintus Mullius Florentinus, son of Sextus, of the Claudia tribe, aged [?], made this for himself alive, and for his wife Caenatiusta, aged [?], and for Quintus Mullius Marcellus, aged 20, a *sevirus* of the colony of Savaria Claudius, and for his son and grandson Mullia Marcellina.
EDCS-26600367.summary: A tombstone erected by Quintus Mullius Florentinus for himself, his wife, son Marcellus (a *seviri*), and grandson Marcellinus, from the colony of Savaria Claudius, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600368.summary: A fragmentary inscription identifying a person named Claudius who was a scribe, probably from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600369.text_edition: \n Luci Maximi filius domo Emona annorum vivus fecit sibi et suis et Saloniae Marcellae coniugi pientissimae annorum XXX\n
EDCS-26600369.translation: Lucius Maximinus, son of Lucius, from Emona, aged [?], made this for himself alive, and for his family, and for his most devoted wife Salonia Marcella, aged 30.
EDCS-26600369.summary: A tombstone set up by Lucius Maximinus, from Emona, for himself, his family, and his wife Salonia Marcella, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600370.text_edition: \n Bonae memoriae Aureliae Iustinae nnorum XL coniugi carissimae Aurelius Gaianus et Gaius et Iustinus filii matri pientissimae vvivi ffecerunt et filia Surica Dis Manibus\n
EDCS-26600370.translation: For the good memory of Aurelia Iustina, aged 40, a most beloved wife. Aurelius Gaianus, Gaius, and Iustinus, her sons, and her daughter Surica, made this for their most devoted mother. To the departed spirits.
EDCS-26600370.summary: A funerary inscription honoring Aurelia Iustina, set up by her sons and daughter, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600371.text_edition: \n Caio Caesio Cai filio Claudia Victori annorum LXX et Iuliae Tiberi filiae Proculae coniugi annorum L Caius Caesius Vitulus et Caius Caesius Optatus et Caesia Graeca parentibus optimis\n
EDCS-26600371.translation: Caius Caesius Vitulus and Caius Caesius Optatus and Caesia Graeca [set this up] for their most excellent parents, Gaius Caesius Vitulus, son of Caius, of the Claudia tribe, aged 70, and Iulia Procula, daughter of Tiberius, his wife, aged 50.
EDCS-26600371.summary: A funerary monument dedicated by children to their parents, Caius Caesius Vitulus and Iulia Procula, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600372.text_edition: \n Tiberius Claudius Ursus vivus fecit sibi et Valentinae coniugi annorum LI et Tiberio Claudio Urso annorum XX et Tiberio Claudio Ursino filio et Bononio Crescenti annorum XX\n
EDCS-26600372.translation: Tiberius Claudius Ursus made this for himself alive, and for his wife Valentina, aged 51, and for Tiberius Claudius Ursus, aged 20, and for his son Tiberius Claudius Ursinus, and for [his son?] Bononius Crescens, aged 20.
EDCS-26600372.summary: A tombstone erected by Tiberius Claudius Ursus for himself, his wife Valentina, and his sons Tiberius Claudius Ursus and Tiberius Claudius Ursinus, and possibly another individual Bononius Crescens, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600373.text_edition: \n Firmia Luci filia Scarbantina annorum XXXV hic sita est Quintus Caesernius Iustus filius heres faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-26600373.translation: Firmia, daughter of Lucius, of the Scarbantina tribe, aged 35, lies here. Quintus Caesernius Iustus, her son and heir, saw to the construction.
EDCS-26600373.summary: A funerary inscription recording the burial of Firmia, aged 35, with her son Quintus Caesernius Iustus acting as heir and overseeing the monument, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600374.text_edition: \n Tiberius Iulius Ursus vivus fecit sibi et Iuliae Verinae con(iugi) annorum et Tiberio Iulio o filio annorum et Tiberio Iulio Ino filio annorum et Iuliae Verinae filiae et Semproniae Brigae aviae annorum \n
EDCS-26600374.translation: Tiberius Iulius Ursus made this for himself alive, and for his wife Iulia Verina, aged [?], and for his son Tiberius Iulius [name illegible], aged [?], and for his son Tiberius Iulius Inus, aged [?], and for his daughter Iulia Verina, and for his grandmother Sempronia Bria, aged [?].
EDCS-26600374.summary: A tombstone erected by Tiberius Iulius Ursus for himself, his wife Iulia Verina, two sons, a daughter, and his grandmother Sempronia Bria, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600375.text_edition: \n Rufina eredes Iuli Faustiniani ex testamento Valeriae Iustae \n
EDCS-26600375.translation: Rufina, heir of Iulius Faustinianus, according to the will of Valeria Iusta.
EDCS-26600375.summary: This inscription records the testament of Valeria Iusta, executed by her heir Rufina, likely on a funerary monument.

EDCS-26600376.text_edition: \n AP MN vivus fecit sibi et Iuliae Iuli filiae Silviniae annorum XXXV coniugi et Caiae filiae annorum \n
EDCS-26600376.translation: MN, while alive, made this for himself, for his wife Iulia Silvinia, daughter of Iulius, aged 35, and for his daughter Caia, aged...
EDCS-26600376.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by MN for himself and his deceased wife and daughter, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600377.text_edition: \n Macedo Suri filius vivus fecit sibi et Antoniae Marci filiae Viaticae coniugi pientissimae annorum L\n
EDCS-26600377.translation: Macedo, son of Surus, while alive, made this for himself and for his most dutiful wife Antonia Viatica, daughter of Marcus, aged 50.
EDCS-26600377.summary: This funerary inscription was erected by Macedo for himself and his devoted wife, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600378.text_edition: \n II libertis ponendum curaverunt Mamilia Tertia mater et Publius Postumius Romulus libertus \n
EDCS-26600378.translation: The freedmen had this set up. Mamilia Tertia, the mother, and Publius Postumius Romulus, a freedman, took care of it.
EDCS-26600378.summary: This inscription records that freedmen commissioned a monument, with Mamilia Tertia and Publius Postumius Romulus overseeing its erection, likely in the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600379.text_edition: \n Marciae Marcellinae annorum L Aelia Aventina ex testamento faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-26600379.translation: Aelia Aventina had this made according to the will for Marcia Marcellina, aged 50.
EDCS-26600379.summary: This inscription indicates that Aelia Aventina fulfilled the will of Marcia Marcellina by commissioning this funerary monument.

EDCS-26600380.text_edition: \n Marcia Marcellina annorum viva fecit sibi et Ulpiae Maximinae annorum et Ulpio Sabiniano annorum XXII et Flaviae Sabinae annorum XXX et Ulpio Titiano annorum XXIII et Ulpio Dubitato annorum XXVII filiis piientissimis et Ulpiae Dubitatae sorori annorum LXX et Tito Flavio Leoni annorum XII PARTERVM C\n
EDCS-26600380.translation: Marcia Marcellina, while alive, made this for herself, and for her most dutiful sons Ulpius Maximianus, aged..., Ulpius Sabinianus, aged 22, Flavia Sabina, aged 30, and Ulpius Titianus, aged 23, and Ulpius Dubitatus, aged 27, and for her sister Ulpia Dubitata, aged 70, and for Titus Flavius Leon, aged 12.
EDCS-26600380.summary: This funerary inscription was made by Marcia Marcellina for herself, her children, and her sister, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600381.text_edition: \n Titus Pomponius Cupitus annorum sibi et ae Avitae coniugi carissimae annorum et Pomponiis \n
EDCS-26600381.translation: Titus Pomponius Cupitus, aged..., for himself and for his dearest wife Avita, aged..., and for the Pomponii...
EDCS-26600381.summary: This funerary inscription was erected by Titus Pomponius Cupitus for himself, his wife Avita, and other members of the Pomponii family.

EDCS-26600383.text_edition: \n Salviae Marci filiae Verae annorum XXX et Caio Tullio Cai filio Nigrino annorum XX et Tulliae Cai filiae Marcellinae annorum XVI hic siti sunt Caius Tullius Cai filius Paullinus coniugi et filiis fecit\n
EDCS-26600383.translation: For Salvia Vera, daughter of Marcus, aged 30, and for Gaius Tullius Nigrinus, son of Gaius, aged 20, and for Tullia Marcellina, daughter of Gaius, aged 16. Gaius Tullius Paullinus, son of Gaius, made this for his wife and sons.
EDCS-26600383.summary: This funerary inscription records that Gaius Tullius Paullinus set up a tomb for his wife Salvia Vera and his sons Nigrinus and Marcellinus.

EDCS-26600385.text_edition: \n Tullius Florentinus et Marcia Agrippina et Tullia Florentina vivi fecerunt sibi et Tullio Floriano annorum IX et Marcio Saturnino annorum XVIII\n
EDCS-26600385.translation: Tullius Florentinus and Marcia Agrippina and Tullia Florentina, while alive, made this for themselves and for Tullius Florianus, aged 9, and Marcius Saturninus, aged 18.
EDCS-26600385.summary: This funerary inscription was jointly created by Tullius Florentinus, Marcia Agrippina, and Tullia Florentina for themselves and their children.

EDCS-26600386.text_edition: \n Ticinius us sibi et Ticinae niae coniugi imae annorum\n
EDCS-26600386.translation: Ticinius ... for himself and for his wife Ticinia Niae, aged...
EDCS-26600386.summary: This funerary inscription was made by Ticinius for himself and his wife, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600389.text_edition: \n In domino bonae memoriae Aeliae Kalendinae fideli matri dulcissimae qui sic vixit annos numero LX Aelii Mucianus Bassus Paulinianus et Paulus filii pientissimi memoriam posuerunt\n
EDCS-26600389.translation: To Aelia Kalendina, mistress of good memory, a most sweet and faithful mother, who lived for 60 years, Aelii Mucianus Bassus Paulinianus and Paulus, her most dutiful sons, set up this memorial.
EDCS-26600389.summary: A funerary monument set up by two sons for their mother, Aelia Kalendina, who lived for 60 years.

EDCS-26600390.text_edition: \n Bonae memoriae in Deo vivatis Aurreliorum Flavini et Leonis conlactanei fideles et innocentes Aurelii Flavianus et Nemesius parentes memoriam fecerunt\n
EDCS-26600390.translation: To Aurelii Flavius and Leos, faithful and innocent foster-brothers, of good memory, living in God, Aurelii Flavianus and Nemesius, their parents, made this memorial.
EDCS-26600390.summary: A memorial set up by parents for their two foster-sons, Aurelii Flavius and Leos, described as faithful and innocent.

EDCS-26600391.text_edition: \n Aspaliae filiae carissimae fideli nnorum XXV Gaudentius veteranus ex praeposito silvarum dominicarum et Crescentia parentes fideles vivi fecerunt et sibi\n
EDCS-26600391.translation: To Aspalia, their most dear and faithful daughter, aged 25, Gaudentius, a veteran and former overseer of the imperial forests, and Crescentia, her faithful parents, made this memorial for themselves while alive.
EDCS-26600391.summary: A funerary monument set up by parents for their deceased daughter Aspalia, aged 25, who also includes themselves.

EDCS-26600392.text_edition: \n Bonae memoriae in Deo vivas Aurelius Iodorus civis Graecus ex regione Laodicea qui vixit annos L et Aurelio <F=E>rontoni annorum II et Aureliae Celsinae mensium VIIII Aurelia Domnica uxor coniugi carissimo et filiis dulcissimis memoriam viva fecit \n
EDCS-26600392.translation: To Aurelius Iodorus, a Greek citizen from the region of Laodicea, of good memory, living in God, who lived for 50 years, and to Aurelius Fronton, aged 2 years, and Aurelia Celsina, aged 9 months, Aurelia Domnica, his wife, made this memorial for her dearest husband and most sweet children while alive.
EDCS-26600392.summary: A memorial set up by a wife for her husband Aurelius Iodorus (aged 50) and their two children, Aurelius Fronton (aged 2) and Aurelia Celsina (aged 9 months).

EDCS-26600393.text_edition: \n Hic positus est Florentinus in<f=E>ans qui vixit annos septem et requiem adcepit in Deo patre nostro et Christo eius \n
EDCS-26600393.translation: Here lies Florentinus, who lived for seven years and received rest in God our Father and His Christ.
EDCS-26600393.summary: A tombstone recording the burial of Florentinus, who died at the age of seven.

EDCS-26600394.text_edition: \n Memoriam pictoribus duobis sic pelegrinis sic Launioni vixit annis L et dies sic XI et Secundinus vixit annis XXV et dies VII fecerunt collegas sic eorum\n
EDCS-26600394.translation: Memorial for two traveling painters, Launio, who lived for 50 years and 11 days, and Secundinus, who lived for 25 years and 7 days. Their colleagues set this up.
EDCS-26600394.summary: A memorial set up by colleagues for two deceased traveling painters, Launio and Secundinus.

EDCS-26600396.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marco Cocceio Superiano |(centurioni) legionis X geminae nnorum XXX mensium VIIII et Valerio Luciliano militi cohortium praetorianarum annorum XL mensium III Septimia Lucilla mater pientissima et Cocceius us signifer legionis X \n
EDCS-26600396.translation: To the Divine Shades. For Marcus Cocceius Superianus, centurion of the Legio X Gemina, aged 30 years and 9 months, and Valerius Lucilianus, soldier of the Praetorian cohorts, aged 40 years and 3 months. Septimia Lucilla, his most dutiful mother, and Cocceius Us, standard-bearer of the Legio X.
EDCS-26600396.summary: A funerary monument honoring two soldiers: Marcus Cocceius Superianus (centurion) and Valerius Lucilianus (Praetorian soldier), set up by a mother and a standard-bearer.

EDCS-26600397.text_edition: \n Publius Antonius Claudia Favoris liberti filius annorum XXV Claudia Titi filia Dacumena nnorum XXXXV Faventina heres faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-26600397.translation: Publius Antonius, son of the freedman of Claudia Favoris, aged 25 years. Claudia Titi filia Dacumena, aged 45 years. Faventina, the heir, took care to have this made.
EDCS-26600397.summary: A funerary inscription naming Publius Antonius and Claudia Titi filia Dacumena, with Faventina as the heir responsible for the monument.

EDCS-26600398.text_edition: \n Nymphis Augustis sacrum res publica Poetovionensis mandante Lucio Tullio Tusco legato Auggustorum pro praetore curante Tito <G=C>eminio Rufino procuratore Auggustorum \n
EDCS-26600398.translation: Sacred to the August Nymphs. The Republic of Poetovio dedicated this, under the command of Lucius Tullius Tuscus, imperial legate pro praetore, with Titus Geminius Rufinus, imperial procurator, overseeing it.
EDCS-26600398.summary: A public dedication to the Nymphs by the city of Poetovio, commissioned by a legate and overseen by a procurator.

EDCS-26600399.text_edition: \n Marcus Fabius Fabullus tribunus militum legionis XIII geminae legatus Augusti provinciae Africae pro praetore legatus Augusti legionis XIII geminae sacrum Nymphis\n
EDCS-26600399.translation: Marcus Fabius Fabullus, military tribune of Legio XIII Gemina, legate of the Emperor in the province of Africa pro praetore, legate of the Emperor of Legio XIII Gemina, sacred to the Nymphs.
EDCS-26600399.summary: An inscription recording sacred rites dedicated to the Nymphs by Marcus Fabius Fabullus, who held multiple high military and provincial appointments.

EDCS-26600400.text_edition: \n Nymphis Augustis sacrum Titus Iulius Ianuarius pro Tito Iulio Secundo filio votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-26600400.translation: Sacred to the August Nymphs, Titus Julius Ianuarius willingly and deservedly paid his vow for his son Titus Julius Secundus.
EDCS-26600400.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to the Nymphs by Titus Julius Ianuarius on behalf of his son, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600401.text_edition: \n Polluci pro salute Luci Fabi Cilonis clarissimi viri et fili nepotesque sic eius Menander libertus\n
EDCS-26600401.translation: Menander, freedman of the highly distinguished man Lucius Fabius Cilo, and his grandchildren, [made this] for Pollux for the health of Lucius Fabius Cilo, his master, and his grandchildren.
EDCS-26600401.summary: This is a dedication to Pollux made by a freedman for the health of his master and his master's grandchildren, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600402.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Flavius Valerius Constantinus Pius Felix maximus Augustus Aquas Iasas olim vii sic ignis consumptas cum porticibus et omnibus ornamentis ad pristinam faciem restituit provisione etiam pietatis suae Nundinas die Solis perpeti anno constituit curante Valerio Catullino viro perfectissimo praep(osito provinciae Pannoniae superioris\n
EDCS-26600402.translation: Emperor Caesar Flavius Valerius Constantinus Pius Felix Maximus Augustus restored the Springs of Iasas, formerly seven, which fire had consumed, along with the porticoes and all embellishments, to their former state; furthermore, by his pious provision, he established perpetual Nundinae on the day of the Sun, in the year [date not specified], under the supervision of Valerius Catullinus, a most accomplished man, prefect of the province of Upper Pannonia.
EDCS-26600402.summary: This imperial inscription records Emperor Constantine's restoration of the Baths of Iasas and the establishment of perpetual market days, dated to the period of his reign (early 4th century CE) and overseen by the prefect of Upper Pannonia.

EDCS-26600403.text_edition: \n Vibenus Vanni filius vivus fecit sibi et Marciae Crispinae coniugi et Marcio Vibiano filio annorum VIII\n
EDCS-26600403.translation: Vibenus, son of Vanni, made this while alive for himself, for his wife Marcia Crispinus, and for his son Marcius Vibianus, aged 8.
EDCS-26600403.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by Vibenus Vanni for himself, his wife Marcia Crispinus, and their young son, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600404.text_edition: \n Caius Iulius Severinus veteranus legionis I adiutricis vivus fecit sibi et Straboniae Victorinae coniugi pientissimae nnum XXXX et Caio Ulpio Licinio amico optimo\n
EDCS-26600404.translation: Gaius Julius Severinus, a veteran of the First Legion Adjutrix, made this while alive for himself, for his most devoted wife Strabonia Victorina, aged 40, and for his excellent friend Gaius Ulpius Licinus.
EDCS-26600404.summary: This funerary inscription records a veteran of Legio I Adiutrix dedicating a monument for himself, his wife, and a friend, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600405.text_edition: \n Marcus Magius Marci Pupinia Ingenuus Baeterris veteranus posuit\n
EDCS-26600405.translation: Marcus Magius Ingenuus, son of Marcus Pupinia, from Baeterrae, a veteran, erected this.
EDCS-26600405.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Marcus Magius Ingenuus, a veteran from Baeterrae, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600406.summary: This is an incomplete inscription mentioning Gaius Papirius, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600407.text_edition: \n Neptuno Augusto Iulius Lupus pro se et suis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-26600407.translation: Iulius Lupus willingly and deservedly paid his vow to Neptune Augustus for himself and his family.
EDCS-26600407.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Neptune Augustus by Iulius Lupus for his family, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600408.text_edition: \n Herculi Augusto Sabinianus decurio T VR R P C votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-26600408.translation: Sabinianus, a decurion of the municipal town council, willingly and deservedly paid his vow to Hercules Augustus.
EDCS-26600408.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Hercules Augustus by Sabinianus, a local official, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600411.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo sacrum Tiberius Flavius Severus votum reddidit pro se et suis\n
EDCS-26600411.translation: Tiberius Flavius Severus dedicated this sacred offering to Jupiter Best and Greatest, and paid his vow for himself and his family.
EDCS-26600411.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Tiberius Flavius Severus for his family, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600412.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Flavius Atticianus miles legionis I adiutricis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-26600412.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Flavius Atticianus, a soldier of the I Adiutrix legion, willingly and deservedly paid his vow.
EDCS-26600412.summary: This is a military votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by a soldier of the I Adiutrix legion, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-26600413.text_edition: \n Deo Invicto Mithrae PPPublii Aelii Nepos et Proculus et Firminus pro salute sua suorumque\n
EDCS-26600413.translation: To the Unconquered God Mithras, Publius Aelius Nepos and Proculus and Firminus for their own health and that of their families.
EDCS-26600413.summary: This is a dedicatory inscription to the god Mithras, likely set up by a group of individuals for their well-being, possibly from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-26600415.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Nundinario pro salute Imperatoris Gordiani Augusti Gaius D Quirina Victorinus decurio coloniae Sisciae IIviralis eques Romanus sacerdos provinciae Pannoniae superioris et Gaius D Victorinianus filius decurio coloniae Sisciae eques Romanus et Lucilia Lucilla coniux sacerdotalis Pio et Proculo consulibus\n
EDCS-26600415.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus Nundinarius, for the health of Emperor Gordian Augustus, Gaius Quirinius Victorinus, decurion of the colony of Siscia, twice chief magistrate, a Roman knight, priest of the province of Pannonia Superior, and Gaius Victorinianus, his son, decurion of the colony of Siscia, a Roman knight, and Lucilia Lucilla, his priestly wife, under the consulship of Pius and Proculus.
EDCS-26600415.summary: This is a lengthy official dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus Nundinarius for the emperor's health, made by prominent local officials and their family in Siscia, dated to the consulship of Pius and Proculus (likely 237 CE).

EDCS-26600416.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo sacrum Mercurialis Secundi Augusti nostri Moesiae vilici regionis Sisciae O \n
EDCS-26600416.translation: Sacred to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Mercurialis Secundi, vilicus of our Augustus in Moesia, of the region of Siscia.
EDCS-26600416.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus made by Mercurialis Secundi, an official overseeing administrative and possibly agricultural matters in the Siscia region of Moesia, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-26600417.text_edition: \n Dibus Maioribus Aurelius Secundinus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-26600417.translation: To the Great Gods, Aurelius Secundinus willingly and deservedly paid his vow.
EDCS-26600417.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to the 'Great Gods' by Aurelius Secundinus, probably from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-26600418.text_edition: \n Cereri Augustae sacrum Quintus Iulius Moderatus beneficiarius procuratoris votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-26600418.translation: Sacred to Ceres Augusta, Quintus Iulius Moderatus, an assistant of the procurator, willingly and deservedly paid his vow.
EDCS-26600418.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Ceres Augusta by Quintus Iulius Moderatus, an official serving a procurator, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-26600419.translation: To Isis Augusta, Publius Antonius [paid his vow].
EDCS-26600419.summary: This is a dedication to the goddess Isis Augusta by Publius Antonius, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-26600420.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Caius Iulius Flavus beneficiarius consularis iterum stationem habens votum solvit\n
EDCS-26600420.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Caius Iulius Flavus, an assistant of the consular governor, holding a station for the second time, paid his vow.
EDCS-26600420.summary: This is a votive inscription to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Caius Iulius Flavus, an assistant to a consular governor, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-26600421.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Caius Livius Moderatus beneficiarius consularis votum solvit\n
EDCS-26600421.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Caius Livius Moderatus, an assistant of the consular governor, paid his vow.
EDCS-26600421.summary: This is a votive inscription to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Caius Livius Moderatus, an assistant to a consular governor, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-26600422.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Genio loci pro salute Cai Hei Capitonis et Caeciliae Rufinae coniugis eius suorumque omnium votum libens laetus merito\n
EDCS-26600422.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, for the health of Caius Herennius Capito and Caecilia Rufina, his wife, and all their family, [he] willingly, joyfully, and deservedly paid [his] vow.
EDCS-26600422.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter Optimus Maximus and the Genius Loci for the well-being of a family, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-26600423.text_edition: \n Soli Invicto Mithrae pro salute Imperatoris Caesaris Marci Aureli Antonini Pii Felicis Augusti Aurelius Eutyches ex voto\n
EDCS-26600423.translation: To the Unconquered Sun Mithras, for the safety of the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, Aurelius Eutyches, according to his vow.
EDCS-26600423.summary: A votive inscription dedicated to Sol Invictus Mithras for the emperor's well-being, likely from the reign of Marcus Aurelius.

EDCS-26600424.text_edition: \n Deo Invicto Mithrae Aurelius Heraclides et Agathopus fratres votum solverunt libentes merito\n
EDCS-26600424.translation: To the Unconquered God Mithras, Aurelius Heraclides and Agathopus, brothers, fulfilled their vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-26600424.summary: A votive inscription dedicated to Mithras by two brothers, Aurelius Heraclides and Agathopus, who fulfilled a vow.

EDCS-26600425.summary: A dedication to the god Silvanus by a woman named Longinia Ingenua.

EDCS-26600427.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari divi Traiani Parthici filio divi Nervae nepoti Traiano Hadriano Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate VIII consuli III patri patriae Lucius Titius Proculus\n
EDCS-26600427.translation: To the Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Trajan the Parthian, grandson of the deified Nerva, Trajan Hadrian Augustus, Pontifex Maximus, with tribunician power for the 8th time, consul for the 3rd time, father of the fatherland, Lucius Titius Proculus [dedicated this].
EDCS-26600427.summary: An inscription recording the titles of Emperor Hadrian, likely erected by a magistrate named Lucius Titius Proculus during Hadrian's reign.

EDCS-26600429.text_edition: \n Marcus Aurelius Glabrio veteranus legionis XIIII geminae ex beneficiario consularis vivus sibi et Septimiae Matronae coniugi fecit\n
EDCS-26600429.translation: Marcus Aurelius Glabrio, a veteran of the Fourteenth Gemina legion and a beneficiarius of the consular governor, made this for himself and his wife Septimia Matrona while alive.
EDCS-26600429.summary: A tombstone for a veteran soldier, Marcus Aurelius Glabrio, and his wife Septimia Matrona, indicating it was erected during his lifetime.

EDCS-26600431.text_edition: \n Gaio Sempronio Severo corniculario legionis XIIII geminae XXXXI Iulia Florentina coniugi carissimo viva fecit\n
EDCS-26600431.translation: To Gaius Sempronius Severus, cornicularius of the Fourteenth Gemina legion, aged 41, Julia Florentina made this for her dearest husband while alive.
EDCS-26600431.summary: A tombstone erected by Julia Florentina for her husband, Gaius Sempronius Severus, a soldier of the Fourteenth Gemina legion, during her lifetime.

EDCS-26600432.summary: A very brief inscription possibly identifying a person named Urbicus from the region of Siscia.

EDCS-26600433.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus positus est hic Leburna magister mimariorum qui vicxit sic annos plus minus centum aliquotiens mortuus sum set sic sic nunquam sic opto vos ad superos bene valerae sic\n
EDCS-26600433.translation: To the departed spirits. Here lies Leburnus, master of mimes, who lived more or less one hundred years; I have died indeed, but I wish you who are above to be well. So be it.
EDCS-26600433.summary: A tombstone for a mime actor named Leburnus, who claims to have lived for a century and offers a final wish for the living.

EDCS-26600434.text_edition: \n Domu sic aeternae et perpetuae securitati Caenius ianus vir egregius vivus fecit Flavio Tiberianico qui vixit annos XXX niae Ursae quae vixit annos nae quae vixit annos IIII quae vixit annos \n
EDCS-26600434.translation: For eternal and perpetual security of the household, Caelius Ianus, an excellent man, made this while alive for Flavius Tiberianicus, who lived 30 years, and Ursa, who lived 4 years [and ...].
EDCS-26600434.summary: A funerary monument erected by Caelius Ianus for Flavius Tiberianicus and Ursa, specifying their ages and likely indicating a familial relationship.

EDCS-26600437.text_edition: \n Huic arcae inest Severilla famula XPChristi quae vixit cum viro novem continuis annis cuius post obitum Marcellianus sedem hanc videtur conlocasse maritus\n
EDCS-26600437.translation: Within this tomb lies Severilla, servant of Christ, who lived with her husband for nine full years; after whose death Marcellianus, the husband, seems to have placed this tomb.
EDCS-26600437.summary: A Christian tombstone for Severilla, specifying her marital duration and that it was likely erected by her husband, Marcellianus.

EDCS-26600438.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Dolicheno pro salute Impperatorum Luci Septimi Severi et Marci Aureli Antonini Auggustorum [et Getae Caesaris] Quintus Carmaeus Iulianus |(centurio) legionis VII geminae cum Iulia Atticilla et Carmaeis Secundo et Atticilliano filiis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-26600438.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest Dolichenus, for the safety of the Emperors Lucius Septimius Severus and Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augusti [and Geta Caesar], Quintus Carmaeus Iulianus, a centurion of the VII Gemina legion, with Iulia Atticilla and his sons Carmaeus Secundus and Atticianus, gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-26600438.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter Dolichenus by a centurion and his family for the imperial safety, likely dating to the reign of Septimius Severus and Caracalla (c. 198-211 AD).

EDCS-26600439.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Dolocheno sic Secundius Restutus |(centurio) legionis X geminae pro se et suis posuit\n
EDCS-26600439.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest Dolichenus, thus Secundius Restutus, a centurion of the X Gemina legion, set this up for himself and his family.
EDCS-26600439.summary: This is a dedicatory inscription to Jupiter Dolichenus by a centurion of the X Gemina legion for himself and his family, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-26600440.text_edition: \n Flavio Val militi cohortis praetoriae I Valerio Dig NI veterano Adatilia Ania mater filiis pientissimis fecit\n
EDCS-26600440.translation: Adatilia Ania, mother, made this for her most dutiful sons, Flavius Valerius, a soldier of the I Praetorian cohort, and Valerius Digitus, a veteran.
EDCS-26600440.summary: This is a funerary or commemorative monument set up by a mother for her two sons, one a Praetorian guardsman and the other a veteran, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-26600441.text_edition: \n Lucio Funisulano Luci filio Aniensi Vettoniano tribuno militum legionis VI victricis quaestori provinciae Siciliae tribuno plebis praetori legato legionis IIII Scythicae praefecto aerari Saturni curatori viae Aemiliae consuli VIIviro epulonum legato pro praetore provinciae Delmatiae sic item provinciae Pannoniae item Moesiae superioris donato [ab] [Imperatore Domitiano Augusto Germani][co] bello Dacico coronis IIII murali vallari classica aurea hastis puris IIII vex<il>lis IIII patrono decreto decurionum D(eae) Nemesi Reginae Augustae sacrum Iulius Victorinus veteranus c(ivis) municipii Andautoniensium cum suis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-26600441.translation: To Lucius Funisulanus Vettonianus, son of Lucius, of the Aniensis voting tribe, tribune of the soldiers of the VI Victrix legion, quaestor of the province of Sicily, tribune of the plebs, praetor, legate of the IIII Scythica legion, prefect of the Saturnian treasury, curator of the Aemilia road, consul, member of the college of VII men responsible for public feasts, legate pro praetore of the province of Dalmatia, and similarly of the province of Pannonia, and similarly of Moesia Superior, decorated by [the Emperor Domitian Augustus] with 4 crowns (mural, rampart, naval, and classical), 4 golden spears, and 4 vexilla for his participation in the Dacian War, patron. To the deified Nemesis Regina Augusta, sacred. Iulius Victorinus, a veteran citizen of the municipality of Andautonia, with his family, gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-26600441.summary: This is a long dedicatory inscription by a veteran soldier to the goddess Nemesis, listing the extensive career of Lucius Funisulanus Vettonianus, who served in various military and administrative roles and received numerous honors, likely dating to the late 1st century AD.

EDCS-26600442.text_edition: \n Savo Augusto sacrum Marcus Iuventius Primigenius et socii votum solverunt libentes merito\n
EDCS-26600442.translation: To the deified Augustus, Marcus Iuventius Primigenius and his associates gladly and deservedly fulfilled their vow.
EDCS-26600442.summary: This is a votive inscription to the deified Emperor Augustus by a man and his associates, likely dating to the early imperial period.

EDCS-26600446.text_edition: \n Iovi Depulsori Caius Petronius Chryseros ex visu locus datus decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-26600446.translation: To Jupiter the Repeller, Caius Petronius Chryseros, by vision, was granted this place by decree of the local council.
EDCS-26600446.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a place to Jupiter Depulsor by Caius Petronius Chryseros, based on a divine vision and approved by a local council decree, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-26600447.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo pro salute et victoria Imperatoris Caesaris Luci Septimi Severi Pertinacis Augusti domini indulgentissimi Iunianus libertus adiutor tabulariorum provinciae Pannoniae superioris ex voto\n
EDCS-26600447.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, for the safety and victory of the Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pertinax Augustus, most indulgent lord, Iunianus, a freedman and assistant scribe of the province of Pannonia Superior, [made this] in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-26600447.summary: This is a votive inscription to Jupiter Best and Greatest by a freedman and assistant scribe for the safety and victory of Emperor Septimius Severus, likely dating to his reign (193-211 AD).

EDCS-26600449.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo pro salute et incolumitate Publi Valeri Tiberini Marciani Iunioris Publius Valerius Marcianus miles duplarius legionis X geminae Antoninianae adiutor praef(ecti) et Graecinia Publi filia Priscilla parentes votum solverunt\n
EDCS-26600449.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, for the safety and well-being of Publius Valerius Tiberius Marcianus Iunior, Publius Valerius Marcianus, a double-pay soldier of the X Gemina Antoniniana legion and assistant to the prefect, and Graecinia Priscilla, daughter of Publius, his parents, fulfilled their vow.
EDCS-26600449.summary: This votive inscription to Jupiter Best and Greatest was made by the parents for the safety of their son, Publius Valerius Marcianus Iunior, a soldier in the X Gemina legion, likely dating to the 3rd century AD.

EDCS-26600450.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo et Genio Imperatoris [[]] Augusti Pii Felicis Attonius Rufinus procurator provinciae Pannoniae superioris votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-26600450.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest and to the Genius of the Emperor Augustus Pius Felix, Attonius Rufinus, procurator of the province of Pannonia Superior, gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-26600450.summary: This is a votive inscription to Jupiter Best and Greatest and the Emperor's Genius by the procurator of Pannonia Superior, likely dating to the reign of Emperor Severus Alexander (222-235 AD) or later Severans.

EDCS-26600451.text_edition: \n Praestito Iovi sacrum [[]] tribunus cohortis X praetoriae cultor numinis ipsius proficiscens ad opprimendam factionem Gallicanam iussu principis sui aram istam posuit\n
EDCS-26600451.translation: Sacred to Jupiter Praestitor. [Name of Tribune], tribune of the X Praetorian cohort, a devotee of his divinity, proceeding to suppress the Gallican faction by order of his princeps, erected this altar.
EDCS-26600451.summary: This inscription records the erection of an altar to Jupiter Praestitor by a tribune of the X Praetorian cohort, who was sent to suppress a revolt in Gaul by imperial order, likely dating to the turbulent period of the Gallic Empire (c. 260-274 AD).

EDCS-26600452.text_edition: \n Caius Valerius Tettius Fuscus decurio coloniae Ulpiae Traianae Poetovionensis quaestor aedilis praefectus fabrum IIvir iure dicundo augur loca collegio magno Larum et imaginum domini nostri Caesaris ob honorem tribunatus pecunia sua fecit Caius Valerius Tettius Fuscus decurio coloniae Ulpiae Traianae Poetovionensis quaestor aedilis praefectus fabrum IIvir iure dicundo augur loca collegio magno Larum et imaginum domini nostri Caesaris ob honorem tribunatus pecunia sua fecit\n
EDCS-26600452.translation: Gaius Valerius Tettius Fuscus, decurion of the colony of Ulpia Traiana Poetovio, quaestor, aedile, military tribune, duovir with judicial authority, augur, has made the site for the great college of the Lares and images of our lord Caesar, at his own expense, on the occasion of his tribuneship.
EDCS-26600452.summary: This is a dedication by Gaius Valerius Tettius Fuscus, a prominent local official in Poetovio, for the college of Lares and images of the emperor, funded by himself.

EDCS-26600453.text_edition: \n Serapi Augusto sacrum Epaphroditus Alexandri Augusti dispensatoris tabularius votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-26600453.translation: To Serapis Augustus. Epaphroditus, scribe of Epaphroditus, freedman of the emperor, has fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-26600453.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Serapis by Epaphroditus, a freedman and scribe holding an imperial financial post.

EDCS-26600454.text_edition: \n Pro salute collegii iuventutis et Ulpi Marcellini et Aeli M<a>rcelli praefectorum et Maximi et Ursi patres sic Gellius Marcellinus et Pant Tertius et Aelius Valerius qquaestores collegii supra scripti ex voto posuerunt IIIno Marcell Valerius \n
EDCS-26600454.translation: For the health of the college of youth and of Ulpius Marcellinus and Aelius Marcellus, prefects, and of Maximus and Ursus, fathers, Gellius Marcellinus, Pant Tertius, and Aelius Valerius, quaestors of the aforesaid college, have set this up from a vow, along with Marcellus Valerius.
EDCS-26600454.summary: This is a dedication for the well-being of the college of youth and its leaders by the quaestors of that college.

EDCS-26600455.translation: For the health of the god Fortunus. Fortunatus set this up.
EDCS-26600455.summary: A simple dedication made by Fortunatus for the health of the god Fortunus.

EDCS-26600456.text_edition: \n O Marciae Maximae filiorum votum solvit libens merito Crispino et Aeliano consulibus I\n
EDCS-26600456.translation: For the health of Marcia Maxima. Her sons fulfilled their vow willingly and deservedly, under the consulship of Crispinus and Aelianus.
EDCS-26600456.summary: This inscription records the fulfillment of a vow by the sons of Marcia Maxima, dated by the consuls Crispinus and Aelianus.

EDCS-26600458.text_edition: \n IIIIXXX stipendiorum VII domo Claudia Celeia Caius Betuscius Tertius miles legionis eiusdem fratri pientissimo posuit\n
EDCS-26600458.translation: Served 40 years and 7 months. From Claudia Celeia. Gaius Betuscius Tertius, a soldier of the same legion, set this up for his most devoted brother.
EDCS-26600458.summary: This tombstone commemorates Gaius Betuscius Tertius, a soldier who served 40 years and 7 months, and was dedicated by his brother.

EDCS-26600459.text_edition: \n Caius Cornelius Cai filius Pomptina Dertona Verus veteranus legionis II adiutricis deductus coloniam Ulpiam Traianam Poetovionensem missione agraria II militavit beneficiarius consularis nnum L hic situs est testamento fieri iussit heres Caius Billienius Vitalis faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-26600459.translation: Gaius Cornelius Verus, son of Gaius, of the Pomptina tribe, from Dertona, a veteran of the II Adjutrix legion, settled in the colony of Ulpia Traiana Poetovio with land allotment upon discharge. He served as an assistant to a consular legate for 50 years. He lies here. He ordered this to be made in his will. His heir, Gaius Billienius Vitalis, had it made.
EDCS-26600459.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gaius Cornelius Verus, a veteran of the II Adjutrix legion, who settled in Poetovio and served 50 years, with arrangements made by his heir.

EDCS-26600460.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Tit-- Iuli Firm legionis XIII \n
EDCS-26600460.translation: To the departed spirits. Titus Iulius Firmus of the XIII Legion [died...]
EDCS-26600460.summary: This is a fragmentary tomb inscription for Titus Iulius Firmus, a soldier of the XIII Legion.

EDCS-26600462.text_edition: \n Marcus Petronius Marci filius Arnensis Classicus Marrucinus |(centurio) legionis VIII Augustae hic est crematus ossa relata domi Publius frater et conlega posuit\n
EDCS-26600462.translation: Marcus Petronius Classicus Marrucinus, son of Marcus, of the Arnensis tribe, a centurion of the VIII Augusta legion. He was cremated here. His bones were brought home. His brother and colleague Publius placed [this].
EDCS-26600462.summary: This is a tomb inscription for Marcus Petronius Classicus Marrucinus, a centurion of the VIII Augusta legion, stating his cremation and placement by his brother and colleague.

EDCS-26600463.text_edition: \n Vexillum equitum Caius Rufius Cai filius Oufentina Mediolano miles legionis XIII geminae annorum XXXVI stipendiorum XVI frat<er=RE> posuit hic situs est \n
EDCS-26600463.translation: Gaius Rufius, son of Gaius, of the Oufentina tribe, from Mediolanum, a soldier of the XIII Gemina legion, 36 years old, 16 years of service. His brother placed [this]. He lies here.
EDCS-26600463.summary: This funerary inscription records Gaius Rufius, a soldier of the XIII Gemina legion, who served 16 years and died at 36, placed by his brother.

EDCS-26600464.text_edition: \n Caio Iulio Magno decurioni coloniae Ulpiae Traianae Poetovionensis annorum et Ulpiae Maternae annorum L Iulia Magna OAII \n
EDCS-26600464.translation: To Gaius Julius Magnus, decurion of the colony of Ulpia Traiana Poetovio, aged [XX], and Ulpia Materna, aged 50, Julia Magna [set this up].
EDCS-26600464.summary: This epitaph records a monument set up by Julia Magna for her deceased husband, Gaius Julius Magnus, a decurion, and for Ulpia Materna.

EDCS-26600466.text_edition: \n beneficiarius Iulii Bassi consularis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-26600466.translation: A beneficiary of Julius Bassus, the consular governor, gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-26600466.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by a beneficiary of Julius Bassus, a former consular governor.

EDCS-26600467.text_edition: \n Valerius Valens vivus fecit sibi et Ulpiae Liciniae coniugi carissimae quae vixit annos XXXV et Tito Valerio Valentino et Valeriano Iustino nepotibus\n
EDCS-26600467.translation: Valerius Valens made this while alive for himself and for his dearest wife, Ulpia Licinia, who lived 35 years, and for his grandsons Titus Valerius Valentinus and Valerianus Iustinus.
EDCS-26600467.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by Valerius Valens for himself, his wife Ulpia Licinia, and their grandsons.

EDCS-26600468.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marcus Ulpius Marci filius Cutio vivus fecit sibi et Medulliae Matierae coniugi Medulliae Respectae filiae locus sepulturae in fronte pedes XX in agro pedes XX\n
EDCS-26600468.translation: To the departed spirits. Marcus Ulpius Cutio, son of Marcus, made this while alive for himself and for his wife, Medullia Matiera, and for his daughter, Medullia Respecta. The burial plot is 20 feet in front and 20 feet in area.
EDCS-26600468.summary: This funerary epitaph records a burial plot purchased by Marcus Ulpius Cutio for himself, his wife Medullia Matiera, and his daughter Medullia Respecta.

EDCS-26600469.text_edition: \n Marcus Ulpius Tertullus vivus fecit sibi et Ulpiae Antoniae coniugi annorum XL et Ulpio Tutori filio annorum XXXVI et Ulpius Tertius filius Ulpia Tutorina et Ulpius Tutorinus nepotes\n
EDCS-26600469.translation: Marcus Ulpius Tertullus made this while alive for himself and for his wife Ulpia Antonia, aged 40, and for his son Ulpius Tutor, aged 36, and for his grandchildren Ulpius Tertius, son [of Tutor], Ulpia Tutorina, and Ulpius Tutorinus.
EDCS-26600469.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by Marcus Ulpius Tertullus for himself, his wife Ulpia Antonia, their son Ulpius Tutor, and grandchildren.

EDCS-26600472.translation: The brothers Intimius and Maxsimilianus set this up for Crispinius.
EDCS-26600472.summary: This inscription records a monument set up by the brothers Intimius and Maxsimilianus for Crispinius.

EDCS-26600473.summary: This inscription records a vow set up by Pusinnia.

EDCS-26600474.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aeliae Messorinae quae vixit nnos II menses IIII dies XV Aelius Messius immunis legionis I adiutricis et Aurelia Tacita filiae pientissimae posuerunt\n
EDCS-26600474.translation: To the departed spirits. Aelius Messius, immunis of the I Adiutrix legion, and Aurelia Tacita, [his] most dutiful daughter, set this up for Aelia Messorina, who lived 2 years, 4 months, and 15 days.
EDCS-26600474.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Aelia Messorina and was set up by her parents, Aelius Messius and Aurelia Tacita.

EDCS-26600475.translation: The two brothers set this up.
EDCS-26600475.summary: This inscription records a monument set up by two brothers.

EDCS-26600476.translation: To the departed spirits. Marcus Oclatius Avitus, decurion of the colony of Emona, and Oclatia Avita, [his wife], [set this up] for Iustus, their son, who died aged 16, [the] unhappiest parents made [this] for themselves and for their own.
EDCS-26600476.summary: This funerary epitaph records a monument set up by Marcus Oclatius Avitus, a decurion, and his wife for their deceased son Iustus and themselves.

EDCS-26600477.translation: To the gods of the underworld. Durrius Avitus and Petronia Maximilla, while alive, made this for themselves and for their daughter Maxima, who died at the unfortunate age of seventeen, their parents.
EDCS-26600477.summary: This is a funerary dedication made by parents for themselves and their deceased daughter, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-26600478.translation: To the gods of the underworld. For Lucius Caesernius Primitivus, a quinquennial decurion of the guild of builders, and for Ollia Primilla, his wife, as provided by their testaments. The guild of builders will provide roses for 200 denarii for the parents. Lucius Caesernius Primitivos [sic].
EDCS-26600478.summary: This is a funerary inscription with specific provisions for the upkeep of the tomb by a guild, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-26600479.translation: Tertia, daughter of Rufus, aged 25, lies here. Maximus, son of Rusticus, arranged for the construction of this monument for his wife and for Lascontia, daughter of Tertius, his mother-in-law.
EDCS-26600479.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Tertia and her mother-in-law Lascontia, arranged by Tertia's husband, Maximus, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-26600480.text_edition: \n Savo Augusto sacrum Publius Rufrius Verus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-26600480.translation: Sacred to Savo Augustus. Publius Rufrius Verus gladly and deservedly paid his vow.
EDCS-26600480.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to the deity Savo Augustus by Publius Rufrius Verus, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-26600482.text_edition: \n Caius Fabius Tertius sibi et suis Vaeturiae sic Spuri libertae Faustae uxori Vaeturiae sic Spuri libertae Habenti Vaeturiae sic Spuri libertae Peregrinae Publio Annio Philadi\n
EDCS-26600482.translation: Gaius Fabius Tertius for himself and his family, and for Vaeturia, freedwoman of Spurius, Faustus his wife, Vaeturia, freedwoman of Spurius, Habentis, Vaeturia, freedwoman of Spurius, Peregrina, and for Publius Annius Philades.
EDCS-26600482.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the individuals for whom a tomb was made by Gaius Fabius Tertius, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-26600483.text_edition: \n I(ovi) O(ptimo) M(aximo) diis deabusque omnibus Genio loci sacrum pro salute Imperatoris Alexa[n]dri Augusti Iulius Terentius beneficiarius consularis legionis X geminae Severianae votum solvit libens merito Iuliano et Crispino consulibus pridie Nonas Octobres\n
EDCS-26600483.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, all gods and goddesses, and the spirit of the place. Iulius Terentius, a beneficiary of a consular legate of the Tenth Twin Legion Severiana, gladly and deservedly paid his vow for the safety of Emperor Alexander Augustus, in the consulship of Iulianus and Crispinus, on the day before the Nones of October.
EDCS-26600483.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter, other deities, and the local spirit for the emperor's safety by a soldier of the Tenth Legion, dating to the consulship of Iulianus and Crispimus (likely AD 224).

EDCS-26600484.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Caius Antistius Threptus beneficiarius consularis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-26600484.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest. Gaius Antistius Threptus, a beneficiary of a consular legate, gladly and deservedly paid his vow.
EDCS-26600484.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter by a soldier holding a privileged position as a beneficiary, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-26600485.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Titus Iullius Firminus beneficiarius consularis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-26600485.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest. Titus Iullius Firminus, a beneficiary of a consular legate, gladly and deservedly paid his vow.
EDCS-26600485.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter by a soldier serving as a beneficiary, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-26600486.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Caius Novetius Restitutus veteranus legionis I adiutricis ex beneficiario consularis votum solvit\n
EDCS-26600486.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest. Gaius Novetius Restitutus, a veteran of the First Adjutant Legion, formerly a beneficiary of a consular legate, paid his vow.
EDCS-26600486.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter by a veteran soldier who previously held a beneficiary position, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-26600487.text_edition: \n I(ovi) O(ptimo) M(aximo) ceterisque dis deabusque et Genio loci Gaius Baebius Marcellinus miles legionis X geminae beneficiarius consularis cum Gaio Baebio Marcello iuniore filio votum solvit libens laetus merito Fusco et Dextro consulibus VI Idus Apriles\n
EDCS-26600487.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, and to all other gods and goddesses, and to the spirit of the place. Gaius Baebius Marcellinus, a soldier of the Tenth Twin Legion, a beneficiary of a consular legate, with Gaius Baebius Marcellus the younger, his son, gladly and joyfully paid his vow, deservedly, in the consulship of Fuscus and Dexter, on the sixth day before the Ides of April.
EDCS-26600487.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter, other deities, and the local spirit by a soldier and his son, dating to the consulship of Fuscus and Dexter (likely AD 227).

EDCS-26600488.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo et Genio loci Aurelius Secundianus qui et Itriu beneficiarius consularis legionis X geminae XV Kalendas Iunias votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-26600488.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest and to the Spirit of the Place, Aurelius Secundianus, also called Itriu, a beneficiary of the consular legate of the Tenth Legion Gemina, paid his vow willingly and deservedly fifteen days before the Kalends of June.
EDCS-26600488.summary: This is a votive inscription from Aurelius Secundianus to Jupiter and the Genius Loci, dating to before June 17th and mentioning the Tenth Legion Gemina.

EDCS-26600489.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo et Genio loci Marcus Aurelius Valentinus beneficiarius consularis legionis XIIII geminae votum solvit libens merito Impperatoribus II Philippis Au[ggustis consulibus] Kalendis Novembribus\n
EDCS-26600489.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest and to the Spirit of the Place, Marcus Aurelius Valentinus, a beneficiary of the consular legate of the Fourteenth Legion Gemina, paid his vow willingly and deservedly on the Kalends of November during the consulship of the Emperors the two Augusti Philippi.
EDCS-26600489.summary: This votive inscription from Marcus Aurelius Valentinus to Jupiter and the Genius Loci dates to November 1st, during the consulship of the two Philippi Augusti, and mentions the Fourteenth Legion Gemina.

EDCS-26600490.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo et Genio loci Gaius Iulius Impetratus beneficiarius consularis legionis X geminae O N M L G Idibus O<c=G>tobribus Valeriano IIII et Gallieno III Auggustis consulibus \n
EDCS-26600490.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest and to the Spirit of the Place, Gaius Julius Impetratus, a beneficiary of the consular legate of the Tenth Legion Gemina, on the Ides of October during the consulship of Valerian for the fourth time and Gallienus for the third time Augusti.
EDCS-26600490.summary: This inscription by Gaius Julius Impetratus to Jupiter and the Genius Loci dates to October 13th, during the consulship of Valerian and Gallienus, and mentions the Tenth Legion Gemina.

EDCS-26600491.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo et Genio loci et Numini Augusti pro salute domini nostri Lucius Valerius Faventinus miles legionis X <g=C>(eminae) A<n>toninianae beneficiarius consularis votum solvit libens merito Praesente et Extricato consulibus \n
EDCS-26600491.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, to the Spirit of the Place, and to the Divine Power of the Augustus, Lucius Valerius Faventinus, a soldier of the Tenth Legion Gemina Antoniniana, a beneficiary of the consular legate, paid his vow willingly and deservedly, in the presence of Praesens and Extricatus as consuls.
EDCS-26600491.summary: This votive inscription from Lucius Valerius Faventinus, a soldier and beneficiary of the Tenth Legion Gemina Antoniniana, to Jupiter, Genius Loci, and the Divine Augustus, dates to the consulship of Praesens and Extricatus.

EDCS-26600492.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Dolicheno et Iovi Optimo Maximo Heliopolitano Aurelius Domittius sic qum sic Flavio Castore et Aurelio Maximu sic fratribus ex iusso sic numinis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-26600492.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest Dolichenus and to Jupiter Best and Greatest Heliopolitanus, Aurelius Domittius, as instructed by the divine power, paid his vow willingly and deservedly to Flavius Castor and Aurelius Maximus, brothers.
EDCS-26600492.summary: This votive inscription from Aurelius Domittius to Jupiter Dolichenus and Jupiter Heliopolitanus honors the brothers Flavius Castor and Aurelius Maximus.

EDCS-26600493.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Dolicheno Marcus Aurelius Valentinus beneficiarius consularis legionis XIIII geminae votum solvit libens merito Impperatoribus II Philippis Aug[gustis] consulibus Kalendis Novembribus\n
EDCS-26600493.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest Dolichenus, Marcus Aurelius Valentinus, a beneficiary of the consular legate of the Fourteenth Legion Gemina, paid his vow willingly and deservedly on the Kalends of November during the consulship of the Emperors the two Augusti Philippi.
EDCS-26600493.summary: This votive inscription from Marcus Aurelius Valentinus to Jupiter Dolichenus dates to November 1st, during the consulship of the two Philippi Augusti, and mentions the Fourteenth Legion Gemina.

EDCS-26600494.translation: To the Unconquered Mithras, Publius Aelius Respectus.
EDCS-26600494.summary: This is a dedication from Publius Aelius Respectus to the god Mithras.

EDCS-26600495.text_edition: \n sua Moderatilla et filii eiius sic votum solverunt libentes merito ex iussu dei\n
EDCS-26600495.translation: Moderata and her children willingly and deservedly paid their vow as instructed by the god.
EDCS-26600495.summary: This votive inscription from Moderata and her children thanks a god, likely a healing deity, for fulfilling a vow.

EDCS-26600497.text_edition: \n QRP cohortis IIII Aquitanorum Gaianus alumnus votum solvit libens merito Cilone iterum consule\n
EDCS-26600497.translation: Gaianus, a foster-child of the Fourth Cohort Aquitanorum, paid his vow willingly and deservedly when Cilo was consul for the second time.
EDCS-26600497.summary: This votive inscription from Gaianus of the Fourth Cohort Aquitanorum dates to the second consulship of Cilo.

EDCS-26600498.text_edition: \n Gaius Claudius Romanus nnorum LXX ibse sic fecit sibi et Aureliae Romanae coniugi\n
EDCS-26600498.translation: Gaius Claudius Romanus, aged seventy, made this for himself and for his wife Aurelia Romana.
EDCS-26600498.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gaius Claudius Romanus, aged seventy, and his wife Aurelia Romana.

EDCS-26600499.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo pro salute Imperatoris Caesaris Traiani Hadriani Augusti Aelii Carni cives Romani\n
EDCS-26600499.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, for the safety of the Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrian Augustus, the Roman citizens Aelius Carni.
EDCS-26600499.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Jupiter by Aelius Carni for the health of Emperor Hadrian.

EDCS-26600500.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Lucius Carantius Gratus beneficiarius consularis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-26600500.translation: Lucius Carantius Gratus, a consular beneficiary, gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Jupiter Best and Greatest.
EDCS-26600500.summary: This inscription records Lucius Carantius Gratus fulfilling a vow to Jupiter, likely during the period of his service as a consular beneficiary.

EDCS-26600501.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Marcius Victorinus et Cornelia Ursina heius sic cum Marciis votum solverunt libentes laeti\n
EDCS-26600501.translation: Marcius Victorinus and Cornelia Ursina, with their household (or family), gladly and joyfully fulfilled their vow to Jupiter Best and Greatest.
EDCS-26600501.summary: This inscription records Marcius Victorinus and Cornelia Ursina, along with their family, fulfilling a vow to Jupiter.

EDCS-26600502.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo et Genio loci Aurelius Marcus beneficiarius consularis legionis XIIII geminae pro se et suis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-26600502.translation: Aurelius Marcus, a consular beneficiary of the Legio XIIII Gemina, gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Jupiter Best and Greatest and to the Genius of the place, for himself and his family.
EDCS-26600502.summary: This inscription records Aurelius Marcus of Legio XIIII Gemina dedicating a vow to Jupiter and the local Genius for his family and himself.

EDCS-26600503.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo et Genio municipii Flavii Nevioduni sacrum Lucius Pompeius Ingenuus beneficiarius consularis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-26600503.translation: Lucius Pompeius Ingenuus, a consular beneficiary, gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Jupiter Best and Greatest and to the Genius of the municipium Flavium Neviodunense, making it sacred.
EDCS-26600503.summary: This inscription records Lucius Pompeius Ingenuus fulfilling a vow to Jupiter and the Genius of the Roman municipality of Neviodunum.

EDCS-26600504.text_edition: \n Lunae Augustae sacrum pro salute Speratillae ex voto\n
EDCS-26600504.translation: Sacred to Luna Augusta, in fulfillment of a vow for the safety of Speratilla.
EDCS-26600504.summary: This inscription records a sacred dedication to Luna Augusta made in fulfillment of a vow for the well-being of Speratilla.

EDCS-26600505.translation: To the unconquered god, Charito of Neviodunenses, the highest.
EDCS-26600505.summary: This inscription records a dedication to an unconquered god, identified as Charito of Neviodunenses, likely from the Roman settlement of Neviodunum.

EDCS-26600506.text_edition: \n Sedato Augusto sacrum Publius Paconius aedem et aram donum dedit\n
EDCS-26600506.translation: Sacred to Sedatus Augustus; Publius Paconius gave the temple and altar as a gift.
EDCS-26600506.summary: This inscription records Publius Paconius donating a temple and altar to Sedatus Augustus.

EDCS-26600507.text_edition: \n Silvano Augusto sacrum Titus Eppius Rufinus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-26600507.translation: Titus Eppius Rufinus gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Silvanus Augustus, making it sacred.
EDCS-26600507.summary: This inscription records Titus Eppius Rufinus fulfilling a vow to Silvanus Augustus.

EDCS-26600508.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari divi Nervae filio Nervae Traiano Augusto Germanico pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate II consuli II patri patriae decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-26600508.translation: To the Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Nerva, Nerva Traianus Augustus Germanicus, Pontifex Maximus, holder of tribunician power for the second time, consul for the second time, father of the country, by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-26600508.summary: This inscription is a formal dedication of a monument to Emperor Trajan, listing his titles and honors, as decreed by the local council.

EDCS-26600510.text_edition: \n riae QVN Maximius Mansuetus |(centurio) legionis XII Fulminatae socrui et Iulia Pompeia filia matri dulcissimae faciendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-26600510.translation: To Ria, Maximius Mansuetus, centurion of the Twelfth Legion Fulminata, and his daughter Iulia Pompeia, had this made for their dearest mother.
EDCS-26600510.summary: This funerary inscription records a monument erected by a centurion and his daughter for their mother and mother-in-law respectively, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600511.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Naeviae Luci filiae Ingenuae nnorum XXX Marcus Iulius Vitalis beneficiarius consularis\n
EDCS-26600511.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For Naevia Ingenua, daughter of Lucius, aged 30. Marcus Iulius Vitalis, a beneficiarius consularis, erected this.
EDCS-26600511.summary: This funerary inscription records a monument erected for Naevia Ingenua by Marcus Iulius Vitalis, a beneficiarius consularis, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600512.text_edition: \n Quinto Annaeo Crispo annorum LXX et Vipsaniae Marci filiae Priscillae Quintus Annaeus Verus parentibus et Annaeo Quinti filio Colono fratri annorum XV\n
EDCS-26600512.translation: For Quintus Annaeus Crispus, aged 70, and Vipsania Priscilla, daughter of Marcus. Quintus Annaeus Verus erected this for his parents and for his brother Colonus, son of Annaeus, aged 15.
EDCS-26600512.summary: This funerary inscription records a monument erected by Quintus Annaeus Verus for his parents and brother, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600513.text_edition: \n Iuliae Gai filiae Priscillae nnorum XXXX Caius Rustius Dexter coniugi optimae et Rustiae Gai filiae Priscillae nnorum XXXV- pater infelicissimus\n
EDCS-26600513.translation: For Iulia Priscilla, daughter of Gaius, aged 40, and Rustia Priscilla, daughter of Gaius, aged 35. Caius Rustius Dexter, most unhappy father, erected this for his wife and daughter.
EDCS-26600513.summary: This funerary inscription records a monument erected by Caius Rustius Dexter for his wife and daughter, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600514.text_edition: \n Titius Atilius et Titia Accepta coniux vivi fecerunt sibi [et ------\n
EDCS-26600514.translation: Titius Atilius and his wife Titia Accepta, while alive, made this for themselves and [for others]...
EDCS-26600514.summary: This funerary inscription records a self-funded monument for Titius Atilius and his wife Titia Accepta, with space for other individuals, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600516.translation: Buio Brocci made this himself while alive, for himself and for his wife Maxima, daughter of Eppo, who died aged 60, and for his sons Severus and Bucio, alive, and for Quinctius, son of Severus, who died aged 20, and for Lasonia, daughter of Voltanius, who died aged 1.
EDCS-26600516.summary: This funerary inscription records a monument commissioned by Buio Brocci for himself, his deceased wife, two living sons, a deceased son, and a deceased infant daughter, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600517.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Calvus, son of Severus, died aged 60. Voltia, daughter of Voltupare, died aged 80. And Maxima, died aged 35. And her daughter Maximela, died aged 20.
EDCS-26600517.summary: This funerary inscription records the deaths of four individuals, likely family members, with their ages, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600518.translation: Firmus, son of Voltupare, made this himself while alive, for himself and for his wife Tertia Secunda, daughter of Secundus, who died aged 25.
EDCS-26600518.summary: This funerary inscription records a monument commissioned by Firmus for himself and his deceased wife, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600519.translation: Maximus, son of Vibus, made this for his brother Rusticus, whom enemies killed, aged 40.
