EDCS-26600519.summary: This funerary inscription records a monument erected by Maximus for his brother Rusticus, who was killed in conflict, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600521.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Sabinus, son of Valentis, made this himself while alive, for himself and for his wife Tertia, daughter of Dexter, who died aged 70.
EDCS-26600521.summary: This funerary inscription records a monument commissioned by Sabinus for himself and his deceased wife, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600522.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Surus, son of Lucius, who died at the age of 50, and Quarta, daughter of Firmus Galunus, who died at the age of 70. Sabinus erected this for his parents and himself.
EDCS-26600522.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the death ages of Surus and Quarta, with their son Sabinus as the dedicator, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600523.translation: Tertius, son of Epponius Bolerianus, made this while alive for himself and his buried wife Pusilla, aged 40.
EDCS-26600523.summary: This is a funerary inscription where Tertius, son of Epponius Bolerianus, dedicated a tomb for himself and his wife Pusilla, indicating he made it during his lifetime.

EDCS-26600524.translation: Buctor and Quartio, sons, set this up for their father Volteregius, who died, and for their living wife Eninna.
EDCS-26600524.summary: This is a funerary inscription by sons Buctor and Quartio for their deceased father Volteregius and living mother Eninna.

EDCS-26600525.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Voltrex, son of Plaetor, erected this for Venixama, daughter of Plunco, his wife who died, and for himself.
EDCS-26600525.summary: This is a funerary inscription by Voltrex, son of Plaetor, for his deceased wife Venixama and himself.

EDCS-26600526.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Ursinus, son of Buio, and Maxima, daughter of Quintus, made this while alive for themselves and for their son Buio, who died at the age of 17.
EDCS-26600526.summary: This funerary inscription was made by Ursinus and Maxima for themselves and their deceased son Buio, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600527.translation: Gaius Aemilius Felix willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Aecorna.
EDCS-26600527.summary: This is a votive inscription by Gaius Aemilius Felix dedicated to Aecorna.

EDCS-26600528.translation: Auctus willingly fulfilled his vow to Aecorna.
EDCS-26600528.summary: This is a votive inscription by Auctus dedicated to Aecorna.

EDCS-26600529.translation: Gaius Claudius Priamus willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Aecorna.
EDCS-26600529.summary: This is a votive inscription by Gaius Claudius Priamus dedicated to Aecorna.

EDCS-26600530.translation: Sacred to Aesculapius. Lucius Peticius Technicus, the physician.
EDCS-26600530.summary: This is a dedication to the god Aesculapius by Lucius Peticius Technicus, who identifies himself as a physician.

EDCS-26600531.translation: Sacred to Ceres. Vibius, a supplier of grain from the 15th legion, arranged for this to be made in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-26600531.summary: This is a votive inscription to the goddess Ceres by Vibius, a supplier of grain and a soldier in the 15th legion.

EDCS-26600534.translation: Sacred to Hercules Augustus. Lucius Appuleius Proculus gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-26600534.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Hercules Augustus by Lucius Appuleius Proculus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600535.translation: Sacred to Hercules Augustus. Lucius Clodius Alpinus, son of Gaius, of the Velina tribe, and Gaius Clodius Clemens, son of Lucius, of the Claudia tribe, dedicated this.
EDCS-26600535.summary: This dedication to Hercules Augustus was made by two individuals, Lucius Clodius Alpinus and Gaius Clodius Clemens, likely Romans from the Imperial period.

EDCS-26600536.translation: To the gods of the underworld, the shades. Aurelius Iovinus, veteran of the XIII Gemina legion, a torquatus and duplarius soldier from Upper Moesia, to Aurelia Ursa, his dearest wife.
EDCS-26600536.summary: This epitaph honors Aurelia Ursa, the wife of Aurelius Iovinus, a decorated veteran of the XIII Gemina legion, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600537.translation: To Marcus Titius Titianus, son of Marcus, of the Claudia tribe, decurion of Emona, centurion of the II Adiutrix legion, also of the X Fretensis legion, a hastatus in the first cohort of the II Traiana legion, from the cornicularius of the praetorian prefect. Lartia Vera to her most dutiful son. The place was given by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-26600537.summary: This funerary monument, erected by Lartia Vera for her son Marcus Titius Titianus, details his distinguished military and civil career including service in multiple legions and as a decurion, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600538.translation: To Titus Vario, son of Titus, of the Papiria tribe from Narbonne, veteran of the XV legion, aged 60. Petroniae [---]idae, aged [---].
EDCS-26600538.summary: This is a partial funerary inscription for Titus Vario, a veteran of the XV legion, and possibly his wife or relative, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600539.translation: Gaius Vettennius [---], veteran of the [---] legion, is buried here. This monument [---] to Titus Vettennius, son of Gaius [---], his brother, for his piety [---].
EDCS-26600539.summary: This funerary inscription records the burial of Gaius Vettennius, a veteran, and notes that his brother Titus Vettennius erected this monument for him, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600540.translation: Sacred to the gods of the underworld, the shades. Titus Caesernius Ianuarius, freedman of Titus, a quinquennial duumvir, made this while alive for himself and his family. This monument will not pass to his heir.
EDCS-26600540.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by Titus Caesernius Ianuarius, a freedman and a local official, for himself and his family, with a clause stating it is not hereditary, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600541.translation: Sacred to the gods of the underworld. To Lucius Publilius Afer, freedman and clerk of the public treasury of the Augustales, for free. He made this while alive for himself.
EDCS-26600541.summary: This funerary inscription records that Lucius Publilius Afer, a freedman and clerk for the Augustales, erected this monument for himself, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600543.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription likely related to the Aurelii Seuthii family, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600544.translation: Lucius Cantius Proculus, son of Lucius, of the Velina tribe, made this while alive for himself and for Lucius Cantius Probatus, freedman of Lucius, his father, and for Cantia Cirrata, freedwoman of Lucius, his mother.
EDCS-26600544.summary: This funerary monument was erected by Lucius Cantius Proculus for himself, his freedman father's father, and his freedman mother, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600545.translation: Lucius Claturnius Sabinus, son of Lucius, aged 10, and his mother Notria Maxima, daughter of Caius, aged 28, and her daughter Claturnia Polittancula, aged six months, are buried here. Lucius Claturnius placed [this monument] for his son and comrade and for his daughters.
EDCS-26600545.summary: This funerary inscription records the burial of a father, mother, and daughter, with the father placing the monument for his family.

EDCS-26600547.translation: Fronto, son of Vibius, made [this] for himself while alive, and for his wife Secunda, daughter of Maximu(s), who died at 100 years old, and also for Bugia, daughter of Sextus, who died at 60 years old, and for Fronto, son of Lucius, who died at 60 years old. The monument occupies 15 feet in front.
EDCS-26600547.summary: A man named Fronto erected a tomb for himself, his wife Secunda, another woman named Bugia, and a man also named Fronto, specifying the dimensions of the monument.

EDCS-26600548.translation: For Caius Julius Quadratus, freedman of Caius, and for his wife Devontia, daughter of Uss[---], while alive, and for Urb[---], son(?), Firmus I[---]N made [this] while alive.
EDCS-26600548.summary: A funerary inscription dedicating a monument to Caius Julius Quadratus, his wife Devontia, and a son(?), with the dedicator named Firmus.

EDCS-26600549.translation: Caius Marcius Dignus, son of Lucius, and Marcia Tertulla, daughter of Caius, aged [---], are buried here. Caius Marcius Maximus and Marcia Oppidana(?), daughter of Caius, sons/daughters [---], made [this].
EDCS-26600549.summary: This inscription marks the burial of Caius Marcius Dignus and Marcia Tertulla, and records that Caius Marcius Maximus and Marcia Oppidana erected the monument for them.

EDCS-26600550.translation: Dedicated to the departed spirits. Marcus Octavius Satullus made [this] while alive for himself and his wife Domitia Fortunata.
EDCS-26600550.summary: A funerary inscription recording that Marcus Octavius Satullus erected a tomb for himself and his wife.

EDCS-26600552.summary: This is a fragment of a funerary inscription mentioning Lucius Ruf[ius].

EDCS-26600556.translation: To the departed spirits. Ur() Av() son died at 70 years old.
EDCS-26600556.summary: A funerary inscription recording the death of a man named Ur() Av() at the age of 70.

EDCS-26600567.text_edition: \n Volcano Augusto sacrum cohors II Alpinorum equitata cui praeest Aulus Plautius Fabia Bassianus Roma praefectus\n
EDCS-26600567.translation: Sacred to Volcano Augustus, from the Second Cohort of Alpine Troops, cavalry. Commanded by Aulus Plautius Fabianus Bassianus, prefect from Rome.
EDCS-26600567.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the god Volcano Augustus by a military unit, the Second Cohort of Alpine Troops.

EDCS-26600568.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publio Petronio Urso equiti tubicini cohortis II Alpinorum vivi sic Iulius Posimarus nepoti eius pientissimo\n
EDCS-26600568.translation: To the departed spirits. For Publius Petronius Urus, cavalryman and trumpeter of the Second Cohort of Alpine Troops. Built by his grandson Julius Posimarus, most dutiful.
EDCS-26600568.summary: This funerary inscription honors Publius Petronius Urus, a cavalry trumpeter, and was erected by his grandson.

EDCS-26600571.text_edition: \n Herculi Magno Deo sacrum Martianus et Naso votum solverunt libentes merito\n
EDCS-26600571.translation: Sacred to Hercules Magnus Deus. Martianus and Naso fulfilled their vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-26600571.summary: This inscription records a votive offering to the god Hercules Magnus made by Martianus and Naso.

EDCS-26600574.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus perpetuae securitati Lucio Caesernio Sabino qui vixit annis XXXVIII menses VII Caesernius Paulinus strator consularis fratri pientissimo faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-26600574.translation: To the departed spirits, for the eternal peace of Lucius Caesernius Sabinus, who lived 38 years and 7 months. Caesernius Paulinus, staff officer of a consular governor, arranged this for his most devoted brother.
EDCS-26600574.summary: A funerary inscription dedicated by a brother to Lucius Caesernius Sabinus, who died at 38 years old, likely from the late 2nd or early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-26600575.text_edition: \n Tiberius Claudius Secundus cum Tiberiis Claudiis Marcello et Secundo iuniore \n
EDCS-26600575.translation: Tiberius Claudius Secundus, together with Tiberius Claudius Marcellus and Tiberius Claudius Secundus the younger.
EDCS-26600575.summary: A fragmented inscription that appears to record the names of three individuals, Tiberius Claudius Secundus, Marcellus, and Secundus the younger, possibly part of a larger funerary or commemorative monument.

EDCS-26600576.text_edition: \n Domitiae Resputae qui sic vixit nnos XXI \n
EDCS-26600576.translation: For Domitia Resputa, who lived 21 years.
EDCS-26600576.summary: A brief funerary inscription recording the name and age at death of Domitia Resputa, who lived 21 years.

EDCS-26600577.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Flaviae quondam Valentinae filiae et Flavio quondam Valentino patri et marito suo et Av filiae et R\n
EDCS-26600577.translation: To the departed spirits, for Flavia Valentina, my daughter, and for her father and husband, Flavius Valens, and for Avia, daughter, and R...
EDCS-26600577.summary: A funerary inscription dedicated to Flavia Valentina and her family members, including her father Flavius Valens, indicating a familial bond and loss.

EDCS-26600578.text_edition: \n Fusca Bargathis liberta nnorum XXII hic est sita patronus pietatis causa titulum memoriae posuit\n
EDCS-26600578.translation: Fusca Bargathis, freedwoman, 22 years old. She lies here. Her patron placed this memorial tablet for her out of affection.
EDCS-26600578.summary: A funerary inscription for Fusca Bargathis, a 22-year-old freedwoman, whose patron erected the monument out of affection.

EDCS-26600579.text_edition: \n Valens Marcus Ulpius Valentinus Aurelius Respectus vivus et Sisiuni coniugi vivae et Dervoniae vixit annos LV et Valentino filio annorum XXX et Troucetimarus filius vixit annos XXV et Optimo filio vixit annos XX et Sisiu filia vixit annos XVIII vivus sibi et suis fecit\n
EDCS-26600579.translation: Valens Marcus Ulpius Valentinus Aurelius Respectus while alive, and to his wife Sisiunia while alive, and to Dervonia, who lived 55 years, and to his son Valentinus, 30 years old, and to his son Troucetimarus, who lived 25 years, and to his son Optimo, who lived 20 years, and to his daughter Sisiu, who lived 18 years. While alive, he made this for himself and his family.
EDCS-26600579.summary: A funerary monument self-dedicated by Valens Marcus Ulpius Valentinus Aurelius Respectus for himself and his family, including his wife and four children, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-26600580.text_edition: \n et perpetuae securitati Aurelio Satull miles legionis I adiutricis qui vixit annis XXIIII stipendiorum VI in cursu hostis Daciae decidit et Aurelio Satullo qui vixit annis XIIII Aurelius Acutus miles legionis I adiutricis pater filiis bene merentibus posuit\n
EDCS-26600580.translation: and for eternal peace, Aurelius Satullus, soldier of the I Adjutrix Legion, who lived 24 years and served 6 years, fell in battle in Dacia, and for Aurelius Satullus, who lived 14 years. Aurelius Acutus, soldier of the I Adjutrix Legion, erected this for his deserving sons.
EDCS-26600580.summary: A funerary inscription erected by a father, Aurelius Acutus, a soldier in the I Adjutrix Legion, for his two sons, one of whom, Aurelius Satullus, died in service in Dacia at age 24, and the other at age 14.

EDCS-26600581.summary: A fragmented inscription that appears to be a dedication to a mother named Vivennia, likely part of a larger funerary monument.

EDCS-26600582.text_edition: \n Neptuno et Nymphis pro salute Imperatoris Caesaris Marci Aureli Anto<nini Augusti Anto>nius Iulianus praefectus cohortis V Lucensium posuit\n
EDCS-26600582.translation: To Neptune and the Nymphs, for the safety of the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus. Antoninus Iulianus, prefect of the V Cohort of Lucensians, placed this.
EDCS-26600582.summary: A votive inscription dedicated by Antoninus Iulianus, prefect of the V Cohort of Lucensians, to Neptune and the Nymphs for the safety of Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, likely from the Antonine period (161-180 CE).

EDCS-26600583.text_edition: \n Marcus Terentius Iustus beneficiarius procuratoris Augusti votum solvit libens laetus merito\n
EDCS-26600583.translation: Marcus Terentius Iustus, acting secretary of the imperial procurator, fulfilled his vow willingly, happily, and deservedly.
EDCS-26600583.summary: A votive inscription recording Marcus Terentius Iustus, an official subordinate to the imperial procurator, fulfilling a vow made willingly and gladly.

EDCS-26600584.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari divi Marci Antonini Germanici Sarmatici filio divi Antonini nepoti divi Hadriani pronepoti divi Traiani Parthici abnepoti divi Nervae adnepoti Lucio Septimio Severo Pertinaci Augusto Arabico Adiabenico pontifici Parthico maximo tribunicia potestate VI imperatori XI consuli II proconsuli cohors V Gallaecorum Lucensium Antoniniana\n
EDCS-26600584.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus, son of the deified Marcus Antoninus Germanicus Sarmaticus, grandson of the deified Antoninus Pius, great-grandson of the deified Hadrian, great-great-grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus, great-great-great-grandson of the deified Nerva, with his sixth tribunician power, eleventh acclamation as imperator, second consul, proconsul, the Fifth Cohort of Gallaeci Lucenses Antoniniana.
EDCS-26600584.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Septimius Severus by the Fifth Cohort of Gallaeci Lucenses, likely dating to his sixth tribunician power (c. 197-202 AD).

EDCS-26600585.text_edition: \n Liccaio Vano N B F annorum LV idem annorum XXX D F VISSE P HE DILIGIVS IVCV\n
EDCS-26600585.translation: Liccaia Vano, daughter of N, aged 55; the same, son of D, aged 30. Visse P He Diligius Iucv.
EDCS-26600585.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the ages of two individuals, Liccaia Vano and an unnamed male, with a fragmented personal name or phrase following.

EDCS-26600586.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus et memoriae Anniae Lupae quae vixit annis VIIII mensibus XI diebus XVI Caius nnius Candidianus et Aurelia Lupa parentes filiae carissimae faciendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-26600586.translation: To the spirits of the departed and to the memory of Annia Lupa, who lived 9 years, 11 months, and 16 days. Caius Iunnius Candidianus and Aurelia Lupa, parents, had this made for their most beloved daughter.
EDCS-26600586.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Annia Lupa, detailing her lifespan and erected by her grieving parents, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-26600587.text_edition: \n Dis patriis Manapho et Theandrio pro salute dddominorum nnnostrorum Claudius Victorinus eques cohortis |(quingenariae) Canathenorum et Trachonitarum et Claudius Maximus filius domo Canatha votum solverunt libentes laeti\n
EDCS-26600587.translation: To the ancestral spirits Manapho and Theandrius, for the safety of our lords. Claudius Victorinus, an equestrian soldier of the quinqagenarian cohort of Canathenorum and Trachonitarum, and Claudius Maximus, his son, from the city of Canatha, fulfilled their vow willingly and gladly.
EDCS-26600587.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by Claudius Victorinus, an equestrian soldier, and his son Claudius Maximus, to ancestral spirits for the safety of their emperors, originating from Canatha.

EDCS-26600592.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus C Ingenua Cai filia nnorum LX hic sita est Publius C Adiutor libertus\n
EDCS-26600592.translation: To the spirits of the departed. C. Ingenua, daughter of Caius, aged 60, lies here. Publius C. Adiutor, her freedman.
EDCS-26600592.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for C. Ingenua, stating her age and identifying her freedman, Publius C. Adiutor, as the one associated with her burial.

EDCS-26600593.text_edition: \n Tiberius Iulius Facundus annorum LXXX hic situs est Antonia Octavia annorum patri filia posuit\n
EDCS-26600593.translation: Tiberius Iulius Facundus, aged 80, lies here. Antonia Octavia, his daughter, placed this for her father.
EDCS-26600593.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Tiberius Iulius Facundus, recording his age and erected by his devoted daughter, Antonia Octavia.

EDCS-26600594.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Tiberius Iulius Tiberi filius Fuscianus annorum X hic situs est pater posuit filio pientissimo\n
EDCS-26600594.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Tiberius Iulius Fuscianus, son of Tiberius, aged 10, lies here. His father placed this for his most dutiful son.
EDCS-26600594.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Tiberius Iulius Fuscianus, a young boy, erected by his father who honors his piety.

EDCS-27000276.text_edition: \n Dagela Criniti filia posuit Antelo Primo liberto annorum - I et Caliae coiugi et Primigenio et Lupo II et Ingenuo fili-- et Valenti IPIIIIIII \n
EDCS-27000276.translation: Dagela, daughter of Crinitus, placed this for Antelus Primus, freedman, aged 1 year, and for Calia, wife, and for Primigenius and Lupus, sons, and for Ingenuus, and for Valens, aged 6.
EDCS-27000276.summary: This epitaph, likely funerary, records the death of a freedman (Antelus Primus) at a very young age and lists other family members, including his wife and sons, with their ages.

EDCS-27000277.text_edition: \n miles legionis X votum solvit libens laetus merito\n
EDCS-27000277.translation: A soldier of the Tenth Legion fulfilled his vow willingly, gladly, as was deserved.
EDCS-27000277.summary: This is a votive inscription by an unnamed soldier of the Tenth Legion, expressing gratitude for a fulfilled vow.

EDCS-27000278.text_edition: \n Dominae Parcae Volusia Sesuta votum solvit libens laeta merito\n
EDCS-27000278.translation: To the Lady Parcae, Volusia Sesuta fulfilled her vow willingly, gladly, as was deserved.
EDCS-27000278.summary: This is a votive inscription by Volusia Sesuta to the goddess Parcae, expressing gratitude for a fulfilled vow.

EDCS-27000279.text_edition: \n cio Ucconis filius annorum C hic situs est f p Macrinus Iom filius annorum? \n
EDCS-27000279.translation: Here lies Cius, son of Ucconis, aged 100. Macrinus, son of Iom, aged [---], made this.
EDCS-27000279.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Cius and Macrinus, likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-27000280.text_edition: \n suro Tricci libertus annorum LX hic situs eest\n
EDCS-27000280.translation: Here lies Suro, freedman of Tricci, aged 60.
EDCS-27000280.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Suro, a freedman, likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-27000283.text_edition: \n Fortis annorum Matuilla C sibi fecit Val annorum I\n
EDCS-27000283.translation: Fortis made this for himself, and for Matuilla, aged 100, and for Val, aged 1.
EDCS-27000283.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Fortis, Matuilla, and Val, likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-27000286.text_edition: \n Adectus Flavioli filius vivus fecit sibi et Bononiae coniugi et Valentioni filio annorum XXV et Expedito filio annorum XIII\n
EDCS-27000286.translation: Adectus, son of Flaviolus, made this while alive for himself, and for his wife Bononia, and for his son Valentio, aged 25, and for his son Expedito, aged 13.
EDCS-27000286.summary: This is a funerary epitaph commissioned by Adectus for himself and his family, likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-27000292.text_edition: \n Coccus Annisi filius nnorum XXC hic situs est Mesio patri posuit\n
EDCS-27000292.translation: Here lies Coccus, son of Annisi, aged 90. He set this up for his father Mesius.
EDCS-27000292.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Coccus, set up by him for his father Mesius, likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-27000293.text_edition: \n Restitutae anni I Gemello mensium III hic siti sunt Lucius Cassius et Prima parentes posuerunt\n
EDCS-27000293.translation: Here lie Restituta, aged 1 year, and Gemellus, aged 3 months. Lucius Cassius and Prima, parents, set this up.
EDCS-27000293.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for two children, Restituta and Gemellus, set up by their parents, likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-27000294.text_edition: \n Domitia Luci liberta Optata nnorum LXX hic sita est\n
EDCS-27000294.translation: Here lies Domitia Optata, freedwoman of Lucius, aged 70.
EDCS-27000294.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Domitia Optata, a freedwoman, likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-27000295.text_edition: \n Suppa Verucui filia annorum XXV Tertia filia Verianus coniugi posuit hic sita est\n
EDCS-27000295.translation: Here lies Suppa, daughter of Verucus, aged 25. Tertia made this for her daughter Verianus, her wife, and for herself.
EDCS-27000295.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Suppa and Verianus, set up by Tertia, likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-27000311.text_edition: \n Sextus Crixius Sexti filius Aniensis veteranus legionis XV Apollinaris annorum LV hic situs est testamento fieri iussit\n
EDCS-27000311.translation: Here lies Sextus Crixius, son of Sextus, of the Aniensis tribe, a veteran of the 15th Apollinaris legion, aged 55. He ordered this to be made by his will.
EDCS-27000311.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Sextus Crixius, a veteran, set up by his will, likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-27000312.summary: This fragmentary inscription likely records a workshop associated with Bassus, possibly from the Roman period.

EDCS-27000317.text_edition: \n Tertius Tessius Terti filius Voltinia miles legionis XV Apollinaris annorum XXXV stipendiorum XVII hic situs est\n
EDCS-27000317.translation: Tertius Tessius, son of Tertius, of the Voltinian tribe, a soldier of the 15th Apollinaris Legion, aged 35, with 17 years of service, is buried here.
EDCS-27000317.summary: A soldier's tombstone records the name, tribe, legion, age, and service years of the deceased, Tertius Tessius, with an approximate date inferable from the legion's known deployment.

EDCS-27200203.text_edition: \n Taurus - Cassi servos sic annorum XIIX hic situs est ]\n
EDCS-27200203.translation: Taurus, slave of Cassius, aged 18, is buried here.
EDCS-27200203.summary: A tombstone records the name, owner, and age of a deceased slave named Taurus.

EDCS-27200205.text_edition: \n Caius Germanius Longus nnorum XXX hic situs est Quintus Statius Longus frater faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-27200205.translation: Caius Germanius Longus, aged 30, is buried here. Quintus Statius Longus, his brother, saw to its construction.
EDCS-27200205.summary: A tombstone records the burial of Caius Germanius Longus and states that his brother, Quintus Statius Longus, commissioned the monument.

EDCS-27200207.text_edition: \n Silvano sacrum Iulius Martialis donum dedit\n
EDCS-27200207.translation: Sacred to Silvanus. Iulius Martialis gave this gift.
EDCS-27200207.summary: A dedication to the god Silvanus, recording the gift given by Iulius Martialis.

EDCS-27200213.translation: The workshop of Publius made it (with goodwill).
EDCS-27200213.summary: An inscription stating that a workshop belonging to Publius manufactured an object.

EDCS-27200218.text_edition: \n Silvano domestico sacrum Aurelius Secundinus miles legionis XIIII geminae Antoninianae et Aelius Maximus miles legionis supra scriptae votum solverunt libentes laeti merito Imperatore Antonino et Advento consulibus\n
EDCS-27200218.translation: Sacred to Silvanus Domesticus. Aurelius Secundinus, a soldier of the 14th Gemina Antoniniana Legion, and Aelius Maximus, a soldier of the aforementioned legion, fulfilled their vow willingly, joyfully, and deservedly, with Emperors Antoninus and Adventus as consuls.
EDCS-27200218.summary: A dedication to the household god Silvanus by two soldiers of the 14th Legion, mentioning the consuls Antoninus and Adventus, dating the inscription to their consulship.

EDCS-27200220.text_edition: \n Iuli Magilonis centuria Persiani Kaesonis Pupi centuria Flavi Rufi\n
EDCS-27200220.translation: The workshop of Iulius Magilo, centuria of Persianus Kaeso Pupi; the workshop of Flavius Rufus.
EDCS-27200220.summary: An inscription potentially related to a workshop or unit organization, listing names and centurions, possibly indicating property or personnel assignments.

EDCS-27200222.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Titus FUFCIALI VLIVI et CA \n
EDCS-27200222.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Titus Fu[f]ciali Ulivi and Ca[...]
EDCS-27200222.summary: A dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, naming Titus Fu[f]ciali Ulivi and another individual, suggesting a religious offering or monument.

EDCS-27200226.text_edition: \n Cai Egnati Primigeni Cai Egnati Primigeni\n
EDCS-27200226.translation: Of Caius Egnatius Primigenus. Of Caius Egnatius Primigenus.
EDCS-27200226.summary: A genitive inscription possibly indicating ownership or a dedication by Caius Egnatius Primigenus, repeated for emphasis or clarity.

EDCS-27800152.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelio Saturnioni librario equiti alae contariorum stipendiorum XV annorum XXXV domo Sisciae Lucilius Vindex librarius alae eiiusdem sic cives et heres faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-27800152.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Aurelius Saturnio, a scribe of the ala contariorum, with 15 years of service, aged 35, from Siscia, and Lucilius Vindex, a scribe of the same ala, a Roman citizen, and heir, saw to its construction.
EDCS-27800152.summary: A funerary inscription for Aurelius Saturnio, a scribe and cavalryman, by his colleague and heir Lucilius Vindex, providing details of age, service, origin, and profession.

EDCS-27800692.translation: Here lies Urbana, wife of Iulius Salvius. Atimetus, a freedman and co-slave, made this for his wife, torn away from me, and for his children, born to them. Ah, why did the fatal gods snatch them away? For I lived thirty years while life remained. Now this tomb covers her ashes and selected bones. Go now, traveler, wherever your road leads you. All humanity rushes here, left behind by fate.
EDCS-27800692.summary: This is a poetic epitaph for Urbana, set up by her freedman co-slave Atimetus, likely dating to the imperial period.

EDCS-27800694.text_edition: \n N Sempronia Luci liberta Galla coniunx posuit\n
EDCS-27800694.translation: Galla, freedwoman of Lucius Sempronius, his wife, placed this.
EDCS-27800694.summary: This is a tombstone inscription recording Galla, wife of a Lucius Sempronius, who erected it for her, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-27800695.text_edition: \n Lucius Petronius Luci libertus Licco annorum XCV Galla Cnodavi filia annorum XXCV Gallio Veruclonis filius nepos annorum XIIX hic siti sunt Rufus Luci Petroni Licconis filius et Lucius Petronius Catomocus libertus parentibus suis posuerunt et sororis filio Catamocus vivus sibi fecit\n
EDCS-27800695.translation: Lucius Petronius Licco, freedman of Lucius, aged 95, Galla, daughter of Cnodavus, aged 95, and Gallio Veruclonis, grandson, aged 18, lie here. Rufus, son of Lucius Petronius Licco, and Lucius Petronius Catomocus, his freedman, set this up for their parents. Catamocus, alive, also made this for himself.
EDCS-27800695.summary: A funerary monument for three individuals, including two elderly people and a child, set up by a son and a freedman, with a self-commissioned section. The inscription is likely from the Roman Empire period.

EDCS-27800696.translation: Pottery workshops of Iuvensiana, of the I Noricorum legion.
EDCS-27800696.summary: This inscription likely refers to pottery workshops associated with the I Noricorum legion, possibly indicating military production or supply. The inscription is likely from the Roman Empire period.

EDCS-27900118.text_edition: \n IN III AIILEI FIIBA IONAN IAICT IL leg et T \n
EDCS-27900118.translation: [---] in the third year from the consuls, for the veteran.
EDCS-27900118.summary: A very fragmentary inscription, possibly a dedication or a marker related to a veteran, dating to a specific year. The inscription is likely from the Roman Empire period.

EDCS-27900121.summary: A very fragmentary inscription that mentions a freedman.

EDCS-27900124.text_edition: \n ero veterano et Septimi-- FI IVS\n
EDCS-27900124.summary: A fragmentary inscription that appears to be a dedication to a veteran and someone named Septimius.

EDCS-27900125.text_edition: \n Bonae memoriae <f=E>ilio dulcissimo IIE Stercorino <i=L>nnocenti nnorum XIII <m=N>(ensium) IIII <F=E>lavius <F=E>lavianus e<t=I> Valeria Ius<t=I>ina paren<t=I>es vivi fecerunt \n
EDCS-27900125.translation: To the good memory of Stercorinus Iinnocens, their sweetest son, 13 years old, 4 months old. Flavius Flavius and Valeria Iustina, his parents while alive, made this. And their son Catamocus, freedman of Lucius Petronius, made this for himself while alive, for his sister's son.
EDCS-27900125.summary: This tomb inscription commemorates Stercorinus Iinnocens, who died at the age of 13 years and 4 months, with the monument erected by his parents and a further dedication by his nephew, probably dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-27900128.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription mentioning a soldier, likely from a tombstone or military monument.

EDCS-27900129.summary: This is a short inscription indicating a veteran set up a monument willingly, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-27900133.text_edition: \n Iuca defuncta est annorum IIII IASSER et consiste viator et lege qui TIT AESSI et de caro cuniuge sic Critonte qui vixit annos LXX et <f=E>ecit stipendia XXXII \n
EDCS-27900133.translation: Iuca died aged 4. Stand still, traveler, and read about Tita Aessi, and about her dear husband Criton, who lived 70 years and served 32 years in the military.
EDCS-27900133.summary: This funerary epitaph records the deaths of a young girl, Iuca, and her husband Criton, noting their ages and Criton's military service.

EDCS-27900136.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caio Marcuseno Hilarioni patri \n
EDCS-27900136.translation: To the spirits of the underworld, for Gaius Marcusenus Hilarion, his father.
EDCS-27900136.summary: This is a dedication to the spirits of the underworld for the deceased father of Gaius Marcusenus Hilarion.

EDCS-27900137.text_edition: \n Litugena Atti filia viva fecit siibi et Iulio Secundino filio annorum et Vibenae vernaculae annorum V\n
EDCS-27900137.translation: Litugena, daughter of Attus, while alive, made this for herself and for her son Iulius Secundinus, aged [..], and for Vibena, a slave girl, aged 5.
EDCS-27900137.summary: This funerary inscription records a woman, Litugena, dedicating a monument for herself, her son, and a slave girl, specifying their ages.

EDCS-27900139.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aelio Maximiano equiti domini nostri comitis Dalmati XXX\n
EDCS-27900139.translation: To the spirits of the underworld, for Aelius Maximianus, an equestrian soldier of our lord, count of the Dalmatians, [serving for] 30 years.
EDCS-27900139.summary: This funerary inscription is dedicated to Aelius Maximianus, an equestrian soldier, mentioning his rank, service, and possibly his unit and service duration.

EDCS-27900143.text_edition: \n Taurus sexvir municipii Sallae votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-27900143.translation: Taurus, one of the six municipal officials of Sal-lae, willingly and deservedly paid his vow.
EDCS-27900143.summary: This inscription records Taurus, a local magistrate, fulfilling a vow made to a deity, likely on behalf of the municipality of Sal-lae.

EDCS-27900144.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Faesonius Florentinus votum solvit libens laetus merito\n
EDCS-27900144.translation: To Jupiter, Best and Greatest, Faesonius Florentinus willingly, joyfully, and deservedly paid his vow.
EDCS-27900144.summary: This is a votive inscription recording Faesonius Florentinus fulfilling a vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus.

EDCS-27900149.text_edition: \n et Iuliae Spectatae matri annorum LXXX et III Verinae nepoti annorum XX et Augustae bene merenti vixit annorum X\n
EDCS-27900149.translation: ...and for Julia Spectata, mother, aged 83, and for Verina, granddaughter, aged 20, and for Augusta, who deserved well, who lived 10 years.
EDCS-27900149.summary: This fragment of a funerary inscription lists several deceased individuals, including a mother, granddaughter, and another woman named Augusta, along with their ages.

EDCS-27900150.text_edition: \n iae nae annorum XXX FTPPO per Vibunnium Valerium ex testamento fieri iussit\n
EDCS-27900150.translation: ...aged 30, was ordered to be made by Vibunnius Valerius according to his will.
EDCS-27900150.summary: This inscription fragment indicates a funerary monument was commissioned by Vibunnius Valerius in his will for a deceased person aged 30.

EDCS-27900154.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Antestius Senecio annorum FlorentiCI \n
EDCS-27900154.translation: To the spirits of the underworld, for Antestius Senecio of Florence, aged [..].
EDCS-27900154.summary: This funerary inscription is dedicated to Antestius Senecio of Florence, mentioning his origin and age.

EDCS-27900156.text_edition: \n nus decurio t Gratillae mil-- legionis I adiutricis no Surillioni Valeriae Gratae \n
EDCS-27900156.translation: [...]nius, decurion of the legion I Adjutrix, [and for?] Valeria Grata, from Surillum.
EDCS-27900156.summary: This inscription fragment records a decurion of the legion I Adjutrix and possibly Valeria Grata from Surillum, likely a funerary dedication.

EDCS-27900159.text_edition: \n Appuleiae Paulae annorum LXX et Annio Florentino filio eius annorum XXX Lucilius Spectatus heres ex voluntate testamenti posuit\n
EDCS-27900159.translation: Lucilius Spectatus, heir by the terms of his will, erected this for Appuleia Paula, aged 70, and her son Annius Florentinus, aged 30.
EDCS-27900159.summary: This is a funerary monument set up by an heir for Appuleia Paula and her son Annius Florentinus, likely from the late Roman Republic or early Empire.

EDCS-27900162.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo sacrum Caius Papirius Colonus aedem cum signo suo pecunia sua fecit\n
EDCS-27900162.translation: Sacred to Jupiter Best and Greatest. Gaius Papirius Colonus built the temple with his own image and at his own expense.
EDCS-27900162.summary: This is a dedication of a temple built by Gaius Papirius Colonus to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-27900163.summary: This fragmentary inscription records a dedication made by Candida and her freedman, possibly to Janus, from the Imperial period.

EDCS-27900167.text_edition: \n Silvano Silvestro sic Lucius Catius Messorinus et Lucius Catius Messianus votum solverunt libentes\n
EDCS-27900167.translation: Lucius Catius Messorinus and Lucius Catius Messianus willingly fulfilled their vow to Silvanus Silvestris.
EDCS-27900167.summary: This inscription records the fulfillment of a vow to the god Silvanus Silvestris by Lucius Catius Messorinus and Lucius Catius Messianus, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-27900175.text_edition: \n miles legionis I adiutricis coniugi pientissimae et sibi vivus fecit\n
EDCS-27900175.translation: A soldier of the I Adjutrix Legion made this for his most dutiful wife and himself while alive.
EDCS-27900175.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by a soldier of the I Adjutrix Legion for his wife and himself, likely from the 1st or 2nd century AD.

EDCS-27900179.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus et perpetuae securitati Aurelius Leontius centenarius qui vixit nnos LXXVI schola centenariorum b(ene) m(erenti) f(ecit)\n
EDCS-27900179.translation: To the spirits of the departed and for eternal security. Aurelius Leontius, a centenarius who lived 76 years, erected this for a well-deserving member of the centenarii corps.
EDCS-27900179.summary: This funerary inscription honors Aurelius Leontius, a centenarius who lived to 76, and was erected by his collegium, likely from the later Imperial period.

EDCS-27900183.text_edition: \n CI Moderat-- fecit titulum ad arbitr<at>um Batavi <he=ET>redis \n
EDCS-27900183.translation: CI Moderatus made the inscription at the discretion of the Batavian heirs.
EDCS-27900183.summary: This fragmentary inscription records the creation of an inscription by CI Moderatus, commissioned by Batavian heirs, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-27900184.text_edition: \n Flavi annorum XV et nae et annicul Marc-- Flaviet Sabina Iustae filiis et nepotibus\n
EDCS-27900184.translation: Flavius, aged 15, and Flaviae and Anniculus Marcus, for their sons and grandsons.
EDCS-27900184.summary: This fragmentary funerary inscription likely records the names and ages of children and possibly grandchildren for whom a monument was erected, from the Imperial period.

EDCS-27900185.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelia Leontia qui sic v<i>cxisit sic annos XXXIIII menses V dies XXVIII Aurelius Ianuarius coniugi carissimae memoriam fecit\n
EDCS-27900185.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Aurelia Leontia, who lived 34 years, 5 months, and 28 days, Aurelius Ianuarius made this memorial for his dearest wife.
EDCS-27900185.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Aurelia Leontia at the age of 34 and was erected by her husband Aurelius Ianuarius, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-27900187.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus qu et Aeliae Flavinae aviae municipii Mogetianae avunculo et IA et Aeliae Candidae matri et Aelio Candido quaestorio nepoti et Aelius Flavinus veteranus ex beneficiario legati legionis et Iulia Marcella brutis et Aelius Maximus decurio municipii et Aeliae Candidiana et Pulchra nepotes eorum vivi fecerunt sibi et parentibus karissimis\n
EDCS-27900187.translation: To the spirits of the departed. They also erected this while alive for themselves and their dearest parents: Aelia Flavina, grandmother of the municipium of Mogetiana, for her uncle and IA, and for Aelia Candida, mother, and Aelius Candidus, quaestor, nephew, and Aelius Flavinus, veteran and former beneficiary of a legionary legate, and Iulia Marcella, and Aelius Maximus, decurion of the municipium, and Aeliae Candidianae and Pulchrae, their granddaughters.
EDCS-27900187.summary: This extensive funerary inscription, erected while alive, records multiple family members, including parents, grandparents, uncles, and grandchildren, by various individuals from the municipium of Mogetiana, likely from the late Imperial period.

EDCS-27900188.text_edition: \n Urbacius Atresi filius vivus s<i>bi T \n
EDCS-27900188.translation: Urbacius, son of Atresius, [erected this] for himself while alive.
EDCS-27900188.summary: A funerary or memorial inscription recording Urbacius, son of Atresius, dedicating this for himself during his lifetime, likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-27900189.text_edition: \n Septimio Valenti veterano ex vexillario cohortis |(miliariae) novae Severianae qui vixit nnos LXX et Septimiae S<at=N>urninae qui sic vixit nnos XL et Septimiae Constantinae vivae coniugi et Septimio Sil cohortis vivo \n
EDCS-27900189.translation: To Septimius Valens, veteran ex-vexillarius of the Severian new cohort (miliaria), who lived 70 years, and to Septimia Saturnina, who lived 40 years, and to Septimia Constantina, his wife, while alive, and to Septimius Sil, of the cohort, while alive.
EDCS-27900189.summary: A funerary inscription honoring Septimius Valens and Septimia Saturnina, with a dedication also for the living wife Septimia Constantina and Septimius Sil, mentioning military affiliations and ages.

EDCS-27900191.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Claudiae Nonnae nnorum XXII domo Nicopoli Syriae Maximus aerarius posuit\n
EDCS-27900191.translation: To the Divine Shades. Claudia Nonna, aged 22, from Nicopolis in Syria, Maximus, a treasury official, set this up.
EDCS-27900191.summary: A funerary inscription dedicated to the Divine Shades for Claudia Nonna, from Nicopolis in Syria, erected by Maximus, an aerarius (treasury official).

EDCS-28000258.translation: [Erected in the year] 3, to my brother.
EDCS-28000258.summary: A fragmentary inscription recording a dedication to a brother, possibly from an early imperial date given the mention of 'ann3' (likely an abbreviation for an era or year).

EDCS-28000275.text_edition: \n Tib(erius) Iulius Provincialis quaestor collegii ex praep(osito aram et cancellos posuit libens laetus merito\n
EDCS-28000275.translation: Tiberius Iulius Provincialis, quaestor of the college, formerly prefect, set up the altar and railings willingly, gladly, and deservedly.
EDCS-28000275.summary: An inscription recording the dedication of an altar and railings by Tiberius Iulius Provincialis, who held the offices of quaestor of a college and ex-prefect.

EDCS-28000648.translation: Victoria of Augustus, Preservation of Augustus.
EDCS-28000648.summary: A very brief inscription that praises or invokes the Victoria (Victory) and Conservatio (Preservation) attributed to the emperor Augustus, likely from the Augustan period.

EDCS-28100231.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marcus Aurelius Sallumas veteranus ex tesserario cohortis |(miliariae) Hemesenorum domo Hemesa annorum LXXXII vivo sibi et Aureliae matri et quondam coniugi NV\n
EDCS-28100231.translation: To the Divine Shades. Marcus Aurelius Sallumas, veteran ex-tesserarius of the Hemesenorum cohort (miliaria), from Hemesa, aged 82, for himself while alive, and for Aurelia, his mother, and formerly his wife [illegible].
EDCS-28100231.summary: A funerary inscription for Marcus Aurelius Sallumas, a veteran from Hemesa, who set it up for himself, his mother, and his former wife, also detailing his military rank and age.

EDCS-28100240.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucius Marcius Luci filius Blandus veteranus decurio Noricorum \n
EDCS-28100240.translation: To the Divine Shades. Lucius Marcius Blandus, son of Lucius, veteran decurion of the Norici.
EDCS-28100240.summary: A funerary inscription to Lucius Marcius Blandus, identified as a veteran decurion of the Norici, dedicated to the Divine Shades.

EDCS-28100244.text_edition: \n Proculus Rabili filius Collina Philadelphia militavit optio cohortis II Italicae civium Romanorum centuria Faustini ex vexillatione sagittariorum exercitus Syriaci stipendiorum VII vixit annos XXVI Apuleius frater faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-28100244.translation: Proculus, son of Rabilius, of the Collina tribe, from Philadelphia, served as optio in the II Italica cohort of Roman citizens, in the century of Faustinus, from a detachment of archers of the Syrian army, for 7 years of service. He lived 26 years. Apuleius, his brother, saw to its construction.
EDCS-28100244.summary: A funerary inscription for Proculus, detailing his military service as an optio in the II Italica cohort and a detachment of archers, his age at death and death in service, and erected by his brother.

EDCS-28100245.text_edition: \n Titus Calidius Publi Camilia Severus eques item optio decurio cohortis I Alpinorum item |(centurio) legionis XV Apollinaris nnum LVIII stipendiorum XXXIIII hic situs est Quintus Calidius fratri posuit\n
EDCS-28100245.translation: Titus Calidius Severus, son of Publius, of the Camilia tribe, a Roman knight, also optio, also decurion of the I Alpinorum cohort, also centurion of the XV Apollinaris legion, aged 58, with 34 years of service, lies here. Quintus Calidius erected this for his brother.
EDCS-28100245.summary: A funerary inscription for Titus Calidius Severus, detailing his distinguished military career as a knight, optio, decurion, and centurion across several units, his age and service length, and erected by his brother.

EDCS-28100251.text_edition: \n Iustus optio cohortis II Aureliae Dacorum\n
EDCS-28100251.translation: Iustus, optio of the Second Aurelian Cohort of Dacians.
EDCS-28100251.summary: This is a soldier's inscription identifying Iustus, an optio in the Second Aurelian Cohort of Dacians.

EDCS-28100252.text_edition: \n Titus Flavius Draccus eques alae I Flaviae Domitianae Britannicae millariae civium Romanorum civis Sequanus annorum XXXXV stupendiorum sic XXII\n
EDCS-28100252.translation: Titus Flavius Draccus, an equestrian of the First Flavian Cohort, Domitian, Britannic, Milliare, Roman citizen, a Sequanian, aged 45, with 22 years of service.
EDCS-28100252.summary: This inscription records the details of Titus Flavius Draccus, an equestrian soldier of a specific cohort, including his age and service length.

EDCS-28400280.translation: To Argoliceae, his wife, and her descendants.
EDCS-28400280.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicating the monument to Argoliceae, his wife, and her descendants.

EDCS-28500597.text_edition: \n Seleuce et Curiace sic Vivas sic in Deo\n
EDCS-28500597.translation: May you live, Seleuce and Curiace, in God.
EDCS-28500597.summary: This is a dedicatory or wishful inscription for Seleuce and Curiace, possibly with a Christian context.

EDCS-28500990.summary: This is a simple ownership inscription, likely for a tomb or monument, belonging to Marcus Plautius Rufus.

EDCS-28600289.text_edition: \n Aureliae Candidae quondam quae vixit annos XXV Septimius Priscus eques legionis I adiutricis coniugi carissimae faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-28600289.translation: To Aurelia Candida, who lived 25 years. Septimius Priscus, equestrian of the First Adjutant Legion, had this made for his dearest wife.
EDCS-28600289.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Aurelia Candida, commissioned by her husband Septimius Priscus, an equestrian.

EDCS-28700957.translation: Sacred to Neptune Augustus. Lucius Servilius Sabinus, son of Lucius, of the Velina tribe, built the temple and the portico at his own expense.
EDCS-28700957.summary: This is a dedication of a temple and portico to Neptune Augustus, built by Lucius Servilius Sabinus at his own expense.

EDCS-28700958.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Flavius Consta[---] Maximus, who died aged [---].
EDCS-28700958.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Flavius Consta[---] Maximus, recording his death and age.

EDCS-28700959.translation: Cannutia Paulla, daughter of Tiberius, made [this] for Quintus Fulginas, son of Marcus. Fulginas Procla, daughter of Quintus, is buried here.
EDCS-28700959.summary: This funerary inscription records Cannutia Paulla making a monument for Quintus Fulginas and identifies Fulginas Procla as being buried there.

EDCS-28700960.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Publius Aelius Primus lived 40 years. Publius Aelius Primianus and Publius Aelius Pr[---]
EDCS-28700960.summary: This funerary inscription records the life of Publius Aelius Primus and names Publius Aelius Primianus and another individual.

EDCS-28700961.translation: Here lies Caedagonius Attus, aged 25 years, 10 months, and 7 days. Quintus Caedagonius [placed this].
EDCS-28700961.summary: This is a tombstone for Caetagonius Attus, set up by Quintus Caedagonius, likely from the 2nd century AD.

EDCS-28700964.translation: Sacred to the departed gods. Rusticus [son of...] from [place name], while alive, made this for himself and for his dearest wife, [name].
EDCS-28700964.summary: This funerary inscription records that Rusticus erected this monument for himself and his wife, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-28700968.translation: Gio Coemonius, while alive, made this for himself and for his wife, Stiontia Cumia, daughter of Io, aged 20 years. [Name] of Ronionis, while alive [erected this].
EDCS-28700968.summary: This is a funerary monument set up by Gios Coemonius for himself and his wife Stiontia Cumia, and also mentions a Ronionis, likely from the late 1st or early 2nd century AD.

EDCS-28700997.translation: Marcus Aurelius Serenus, an excellent man, erected this in fulfillment of a vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus.
EDCS-28700997.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, set up by Marcus Aurelius Serenus, a man of high status, likely from the 2nd century AD.

EDCS-28700998.translation: Laburo, sacred, in fulfillment of a vow. Marcus Marcellus, son of [father's name], and Marcus Vibius Marcellus made this.
EDCS-28700998.summary: This is a votive inscription honoring Laburo, set up by Marcus Marcellus and Marcus Vibius Marcellus, likely from the 2nd century AD.

EDCS-28701000.translation: To Aelius Aurelius Commodus, son of Emperor Antoninus Augustus Pius, father of the fatherland, consul-designate, pontifex maximus, by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-28701000.summary: This is an honorific inscription for Commodus, son of Emperor Antoninus Pius, detailing his titles and status, likely from the mid-2nd century AD.

EDCS-28701001.translation: Lucius Oclatius Tarquiniensis, a veteran of the 15th legion, is buried here. Titus Calventius, son of Titus, a veteran of the 8th legion, and Octatia Expectata, freedwoman of Lucius, placed [this] at their own expense.
EDCS-28701001.summary: This is a joint funerary inscription for Lucius Oclatius Tarquiniensis and Titus Calventius, both veterans, and Octatia Expectata, likely from the 2nd century AD.

EDCS-28701002.translation: [Name] concrapti, of Pannonian origin, lived 35 years, served 16 years. [Name]ioru set up this for the well-deserving.
EDCS-28701002.summary: This is a tombstone for a soldier named [Name] concrapti, of Pannonian origin, who served 16 years and lived 35, set up by Iu[---]ioru, likely from the 2nd century AD.

EDCS-28701003.translation: Sacred to the departed gods. For Publius Annaeus Saturninus, and Publius Annaeus Secundus, and M. Secundina.
EDCS-28701003.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to the memory of Publius Annaeus Saturninus, Publius Annaeus Secundus, and M. Secundina, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-28701006.translation: Coponia Tertia, daughter of Caius, [set this up].
EDCS-28701006.summary: This appears to be the beginning of a funerary inscription for Coponia Tertia, daughter of Caius, likely from the 1st or 2nd century AD.

EDCS-28701010.translation: The burial plot of Titus Refidius Venustus, freedman of Titus, twenty Roman feet in every direction.
EDCS-28701010.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the dimensions of a burial plot purchased by Titus Refidius Venustus, a freedman, dating from the Roman period.

EDCS-28701011.translation: Gaius Vedius Rufus made [this] for his wife Secunda, daughter of Epponis, and for his son Sabinus. [dimensions...]
EDCS-28701011.summary: This funerary inscription records the dedication of a burial plot by Gaius Vedius Rufus to his wife Secunda and son Sabinus, dating from the Roman period.

EDCS-28701012.translation: Lucius Valerius Hilarus, freedman of Lucius, aged sixty, made [this] while alive for himself and his family, a burial plot sixteen Roman feet in every direction.
EDCS-28701012.summary: This funerary inscription records the purchase of a burial plot by Lucius Valerius Hilarus, a freedman, for himself and his family, dating from the Roman period.

EDCS-28701028.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Marcus Licinius Castus beneficiarius consularis votum solvit\n
EDCS-28701028.translation: Marcus Licinius Castus, an aide to a consular legate, willingly fulfilled his vow to Jupiter Best and Greatest.
EDCS-28701028.summary: This is a votive inscription recording the fulfillment of a vow by Marcus Licinius Castus, an aide to a consular legate, to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-28701031.text_edition: \n Genio provinciae Tacitus optio legionis XIIII votum <l=C>(ibens) solvit \n
EDCS-28701031.translation: Tacitus, a centurion's optio in the Legio XIV, willingly fulfilled his vow to the Genius of the province.
EDCS-28701031.summary: This is a votive inscription recording the fulfillment of a vow by Tacitus, an optio of Legio XIV, to the Genius of the province, likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-28701033.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Titus Ae<l=T>iius Ae<q=O>u \n
EDCS-28701033.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Titus Aelius Aequus...
EDCS-28701033.summary: This is a dedicatory inscription to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Titus Aelius Aequus, dating from the Roman period.

EDCS-28701034.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Tiberius Claudius atus b(ene)f(iciarius) procuratoris votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-28701034.translation: Tiberius Claudius Atus, an aide to a procurator, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Jupiter Best and Greatest.
EDCS-28701034.summary: This is a votive inscription recording the fulfillment of a vow by Tiberius Claudius Atus, an aide to a procurator, to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-28701035.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo sacrum Titus Flavius Candidus beneficiarius consularis votum solvit\n
EDCS-28701035.translation: Titus Flavius Candidus, an aide to a consular legate, willingly fulfilled his vow to Jupiter Best and Greatest.
EDCS-28701035.summary: This is a votive inscription recording the fulfillment of a vow by Titus Flavius Candidus, an aide to a consular legate, to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-28701036.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Genio coloniae Sisciae TRIAS Marcus Iulius Vesnis NNLI CAIPI CIMTXA\n
EDCS-28701036.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, and to the Genius of the colony of Siscia, Marcus Iulius Vesnis and NNLI CAIPI CIMTXA...
EDCS-28701036.summary: This is a dedicatory inscription to Jupiter Optimus Maximus and the Genius of the colony of Siscia by Marcus Iulius Vesnis and others, dating from the Roman period.

EDCS-28701037.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Fulminatori Fulguratori sacrum Flavius Verus Myrobalanus procurator Augusti nostri praepositus splendissimi vectigalis ferrarium per Asclepiadem arkarium sic stationis Siscianae votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-28701037.translation: Flavius Verus Myrobalanus, procurator of our Emperor, prefect of the most distinguished customs office of the Siscia station, and Asclepiades, the cashier, willingly and deservedly fulfilled their vow to Jupiter Best and Greatest, the Thunderer, the Lightning-Bearer.
EDCS-28701037.summary: This is a votive inscription recording the fulfillment of a vow by Flavius Verus Myrobalanus, a procurator and prefect of customs, and Asclepiades, a cashier, to Jupiter related to his attributes, likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-28701039.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Heliopolitano Lucius Virilius Pupus beneficiarius consularis votum solvit libens merito ne quis in hac ara porcos agi facere velit\n
EDCS-28701039.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus Heliopolitanus, Lucius Virilius Pupus, a consular soldier's beneficiary, willingly and deservedly paid his vow, decreeing that no one should wish to drive pigs before this altar.
EDCS-28701039.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter Optimus Maximus Heliopolitanus by Lucius Virilius Pupus, a consular soldier's beneficiary, in the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28701041.text_edition: \n Libero Patri et Silvano Domestico Aurelius Quintus beneficiarius consularis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-28701041.translation: To Liber Pater and Silvanus Domesticus, Aurelius Quintus, a consular soldier's beneficiary, willingly and deservedly paid his vow.
EDCS-28701041.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Liber Pater and Silvanus Domesticus by Aurelius Quintus, a consular soldier's beneficiary, in the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28701042.text_edition: \n Deo Invicto Mithrae sacrum Iucundus Augusti nostri dispensator provinciae Pannoniae superioris porticus et apparatorium ex voto fecit\n
EDCS-28701042.translation: Sacred to the Unconquered God Mithras. Iucundus, treasurer of our Emperor in the province of Upper Pannonia, made the portico and the office rooms according to his vow.
EDCS-28701042.summary: This is a dedication to Mithras made by Iucundus, an imperial treasurer in Upper Pannonia, likely in the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28701043.text_edition: \n Silvano Augusto Titus Aurelius Anicetus Augustalis coloniae Siscianorum cum suis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-28701043.translation: To Silvanus Augustus, Titus Aurelius Anicetus, an Augustalis of the colony of Siscia, with his family, willingly and deservedly paid his vow.
EDCS-28701043.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Silvanus Augustus by Titus Aurelius Anicetus, an Augustalis of the colony of Siscia, in the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28701044.text_edition: \n Silvano Domestico sacrum Octavius Cornelianus ex voto posuit\n
EDCS-28701044.translation: Sacred to Silvanus Domesticus. Octavius Cornelianus set this up according to his vow.
EDCS-28701044.summary: This is a dedication to Silvanus Domesticus made by Octavius Cornelianus, likely in the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28701045.text_edition: \n Veneri Augustae sacrum Augustinus Auggustorum nnostrorum libertus tabularius provinciae \n
EDCS-28701045.translation: Sacred to Venus Augusta. Augustinus, freedman of our Emperors and registrar of the province, [set this up].
EDCS-28701045.summary: This is a dedication to Venus Augusta made by Augustinus, an imperial freedman and provincial registrar, likely in the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28701046.text_edition: \n Aurelius orus pater ex visu votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-28701046.translation: Aurelius Orus, father, willingly and deservedly paid his vow after a vision.
EDCS-28701046.summary: This is a votive inscription by Aurelius Orus, a paterfamilias, who paid his vow after receiving a vision, in the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28701049.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marco Mulvio Narcisso annorum LX et Marco Mulvio Narcissiano annorum XXVI Augustalis coloniae Septimiae Siscianorum Mulvia Furnia marito et filio karissimis viva fecit\n
EDCS-28701049.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Mulvia Furnia made this for her dearest husband, Marcus Mulvius Narcissus, aged 60, and her son, Marcus Mulvius Narcissianus, aged 26, an Augustalis of the colony of Septimia Siscia, while she was alive.
EDCS-28701049.summary: This is a tombstone inscription set up by a wife for her husband and son, one of whom was an Augustalis of the colony of Siscia, in the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28701050.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Pontio Lupo Augustali coloniae Siscianorum scribae municipii Flavi Scarbantiae Pontia Victorina soror et Domitius Crescens A D F\n
EDCS-28701050.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Pontius Lupus, an Augustalis of the colony of Siscia and scribe of the municipality of Flavia Scarbantia. Pontia Victorina, his sister, and Domitius Crescens, his grandson (or kinsman) A D F.
EDCS-28701050.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Pontius Lupus, an Augustalis of the colony of Siscia and scribe, set up by his sister and grandson/kinsman, in the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28701051.text_edition: \n SISCEA Caius Vitalius Vital eiusdem et Vital parentibus karissimis\n
EDCS-28701051.translation: For Caius Vitalius Vitalis, of Siscia, and his parents, his son Vitalius [set this up].
EDCS-28701051.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Caius Vitalius Vitalis and his parents, set up by his son, from Siscia, likely in the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28701052.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Cai Urbici Firmi decurionis coloniae Septimiae Siscianorum Urbiciae Cond vixit \n
EDCS-28701052.translation: To the spirits of the departed, for Urbicia Cond, wife of Caius Urbicius Firmus, decurion of the colony of Septimia Sisciana. She lived
EDCS-28701052.summary: This is a tombstone for Urbicia Cond, wife of a decurion, recording her death.

EDCS-28701075.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Oirillus, age 21. He lived 9 years.
EDCS-28701075.summary: This fragmented inscription records the lifespan of Oirillus.

EDCS-28800060.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Valeria Dionysia domo Sarmizegetusa Daciae superioris hic sita est vixit annos XX Caius Valerius Sarnus coniugi dulcissimae fecit\n
EDCS-28800060.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Valeria Dionysia, from the city of Sarmizegetusa in Upper Dacia, is buried here. She lived 20 years. Gaius Valerius Sarnus made this for his sweetest wife.
EDCS-28800060.summary: A tombstone erected by Gaius Valerius Sarnus for his wife Valeria Dionysia, originally from Dacia.

EDCS-28800061.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marcus Vettius Marci filius Veturia Surus Placentia annorum L et Erbulia Silvana annorum XX hic siti sunt Marcus Vettius Domesticus parentibus faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-28800061.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Marcus Vettius, son of Marcus, age 50, and Erbulia Silvana, age 20, are buried here. Marcus Vettius Domesticus arranged for this to be made for his parents.
EDCS-28800061.summary: A tombstone for Marcus Vettius and Erbulia Silvana, for whom Marcus Vettius Domesticus arranged the burial monument.

EDCS-28800062.text_edition: \n Alfianius Crescens annorum LXXX Caesilia Luci liberta Primigenia annorum XC hic siti sunt Alfiani Ianuaria Ianuarius Tilurinus pro pietate posuerunt\n
EDCS-28800062.translation: Alfianius Crescens, age 80, and Caesilia Primigenia, freedwoman of Lucius, age 90, are buried here. The Alfian family, Ianuaria and Ianuarius Tilurinus, placed this out of piety.
EDCS-28800062.summary: A tombstone for Alfianius Crescens and Caesilia Primigenia, erected by members of the Alfian family.

EDCS-28800063.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Antonius Marci libertus curius\n
EDCS-28800063.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Antonius, freedman of Marcus, the curule official.
EDCS-28800063.summary: A dedication to the departed spirits for Antonius, a freedman of Marcus, who held a curule office.

EDCS-28800064.text_edition: \n Caius Arruntius Cai filius Lentulus annorum V et Caius Arruntius Cai filius Ligus annorum III hic siti sunt et Varena Cai filia Candida annorum XXXV Caius Arruntius Ingenuus filiis et coniugi posuit\n
EDCS-28800064.translation: Gaius Arruntius Lentulus, son of Gaius, age 5, and Gaius Arruntius Ligus, son of Gaius, age 3, are buried here. And Varena Candida, daughter of Gaius, age 35. Gaius Arruntius Ingenuus placed this for his sons and wife.
EDCS-28800064.summary: A tombstone for two young boys, Gaius Arruntius Lentulus and Gaius Arruntius Ligus, and their mother Varena Candida, erected by Gaius Arruntius Ingenuus.

EDCS-28800065.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelio Exsuperio annorum VI Aurelius Bassus decurio municipii et Aureli\n
EDCS-28800065.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Aurelius Exsuperius, age 6. Aurelius Bassus, decurion of the municipality, and the Aurelii...
EDCS-28800065.summary: This fragmented inscription is a tombstone for Aurelius Exsuperius, son of a decurion, with part of the dedicant's name missing.

EDCS-28800066.text_edition: \n ERII Barbia Successa coniugi Quintus Tullius V et Titus Tullius \n
EDCS-28800066.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Barbia Successa, for her husband Quintus Tullius V, and Titus Tullius...
EDCS-28800066.summary: This fragmented funerary inscription names Barbia Successa as the dedicator for her husband Quintus Tullius V and Titus Tullius.

EDCS-28800067.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription that appears to record the name Cassius V.

EDCS-28800069.translation: To the spirits of the underworld for Claudia Zozimiana.
EDCS-28800069.summary: This is a funerary dedication to the deceased Claudia Zozimiana, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28800070.text_edition: \n Viva fecit Clodia Quarta sibi et filiae suae Clodiae Hispanae nnorum XXX hic sita est\n
EDCS-28800070.translation: Clodia Quarta made this for herself and for her daughter Clodia Hispana, aged 30, who lies here.
EDCS-28800070.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by Clodia Quarta for herself and her deceased daughter, Clodia Hispana, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28800071.text_edition: \n CLSPENSA viv-- sibi et Caio P F et Marcus Minicius Secundus\n
EDCS-28800071.translation: [Cl]ispensai, while alive, for herself and for Gaius, son of P[---], and Marcus Minicius Secundus.
EDCS-28800071.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Clispensai for herself and for Gaius and Marcus Minicius Secundus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28800072.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus fecit Mu caro cumpari sic suo qui vixit annos XXXCII filio bene merenti suo \n
EDCS-28800072.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Mu made this for her dear departed companion, her own, who lived 32 years, a deserving son.
EDCS-28800072.summary: This is a funerary dedication to a deserving son by his mother, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28800073.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Marcianus for his wife Merenia, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28800074.translation: To the spirits of the underworld for Nonia Eutych[---].
EDCS-28800074.summary: This is a funerary dedication to the deceased Nonia Eutych[---], likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28800075.text_edition: \n Caius Pinarius Iucundus fecit Venustae suae annorum XXV hic sita est ipse vivus posuit\n
EDCS-28800075.translation: Gaius Pinarius Iucundus made this for his Venusta, aged 25, who lies here; he himself erected it while alive.
EDCS-28800075.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by Gaius Pinarius Iucundus for his deceased Venusta, whom he set up while still alive, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28800076.text_edition: \n Caius Pitius Cai libertus Hilarus annorum VIIII hic situs Caius Pitius Reditus\n
EDCS-28800076.translation: Gaius Pitius Hilarus, freedman of Gaius Pitius, aged 9, lies here; Gaius Pitius Reditus [made this].
EDCS-28800076.summary: This is a funerary monument for Gaius Pitius Hilarus, a young freedman, erected by Gaius Pitius Reditus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28800077.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Sextus Q Graphicus filio dulcissimo Q Graphico\n
EDCS-28800077.translation: To the spirits of the underworld for Sextus Q. Graphicus, [for] his dearest son, Q. Graphicus.
EDCS-28800077.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Sextus Q. Graphicus for his beloved son, Q. Graphicus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28800078.text_edition: \n Quinctia Publi liberta Marita nnorum LXX hic sita est testamento fieri iussit Adauctus libertus fecit\n
EDCS-28800078.translation: Quinctia Marita, freedwoman of Publius, aged 70, lies here; Adauctus, a freedman, ordered this to be made by testament.
EDCS-28800078.summary: This is a funerary monument for Quinctia Marita, erected by her freedman Adauctus as stipulated in her will, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28800079.translation: To the spirits of the departed.
EDCS-28800079.summary: A dedication to the Manes, likely from a tombstone.

EDCS-28800080.text_edition: \n Lucius Valerius Luci libertus Vegetus annorum XIX hic situs est Lucius Valerius Florus conlibertus posuit de suo\n
EDCS-28800080.translation: Lucius Valerius Vegetus, freedman of Lucius, aged nineteen, is buried here. Lucius Valerius Florus, his fellow freedman, erected this at his own expense.
EDCS-28800080.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the burial of Lucius Valerius Vegetus, set up by his fellow freedman, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-28800081.text_edition: \n Varronius Cai filius Priscus domo Mutina vivus sibi fecit \n
EDCS-28800081.translation: Varronius Priscus, son of Caius, from Mutina, made this for himself while alive.
EDCS-28800081.summary: A self-funded tombstone inscription for Varronius Priscus of Mutina, indicating he prepared it during his lifetime, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28800089.translation: To the spirits of the departed of Primi Respe.
EDCS-28800089.summary: A dedication to the Manes, likely for Primi Respe, possibly a tombstone.

EDCS-28800090.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marcus Valerius Marci filius Taurus annorum L hic situs est Valeria Taurina filia patri piissimo faciendum curavit omnes optamus sit tibi terra levis\n
EDCS-28800090.translation: Marcus Valerius Taurus, son of Marcus, aged fifty, is buried here. Valeria Taurina, his daughter, saw to the erection of this for her most devoted father; we all hope the earth is light upon you.
EDCS-28800090.summary: A tombstone inscription for Marcus Valerius Taurus, set up by his devoted daughter Valeria Taurina, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28800091.translation: To the Silvan Deities, the Forest Gods.
EDCS-28800091.summary: A dedication to Silvanus and the Silvestres, spirits of the wild, likely from a rural shrine.

EDCS-28800094.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Flavia Calendi<n=IV>a Titi Flavi Victorini liberta Calindino sic et Iulio viva ficit sic annorum XXVIIII Crispae et Crispino et Aprae neptiae vive sic fecit \n
EDCS-28800094.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Flavia Calindina, freedwoman of Titus Flavius Victorinus, made this for Calindinus and also for Iulius [or Iulia?] while alive. Crispa and Crispinus and Apra, her grandchildren, made this for her while alive, aged twenty-nine.
EDCS-28800094.summary: A complex funerary inscription for Flavia Calindina and her descendants, indicating she prepared tombs for herself and others during her lifetime, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28800095.text_edition: \n Publius Aelius Messini libertus Fuscus annorum L hic situs Aeli Florus et Lupus Publi liberti posuerunt\n
EDCS-28800095.translation: Lucius Valerius Fuscus, freedman of Messinius, aged fifty, is buried here. Aelius Florus and Lupus, freedmen of Publius, erected this.
EDCS-28800095.summary: A tombstone inscription for Lucius Valerius Fuscus, set up by two other freedmen, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28800096.text_edition: \n Messini liberta Restituta viva sibi Mulsae V mater filii pro pietate \n
EDCS-28800096.translation: Restituta, freedwoman of Messinius, while alive for herself; Mulsa, mother, for her sons, because of her devotion.
EDCS-28800096.summary: An inscription indicating Restituta prepared a burial place for herself and her mother Mulsa prepared one for her sons, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28800097.summary: A short inscription recording the name and age of Iovincatus, possibly a funerary marker, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28800098.text_edition: \n Invicto Deo sacrum Longinus Secundi votum solvit laetus libens merito\n
EDCS-28800098.translation: Sacred to the Unconquered God; Longinus, devoted follower of Secundus, happily and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-28800098.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to the Unconquered God by Longinus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28800099.text_edition: \n Deo Invicto Mithrae sacrum pro salute Augustorum! nnostrorum Lucii Septimii Valerius et Valerianus sexviri coloniae Karnunti votum solverunt libentes merito Marcus Valerius\n
EDCS-28800099.translation: Sacred to the Unconquered God Mithras; for the safety of our Emperors! Lucius Septimius Valerius and Valerianus, sexviri of the colony of Carnuntum, happily and deservedly fulfilled their vow. Marcus Valerius.
EDCS-28800099.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Mithras for the emperors' safety by two local officials in Carnuntum, likely dating to the Severan period.

EDCS-28800100.text_edition: \n Invicto Deo sacrum pro salute Imperatoris Lucii Septimii Valerius et Valerianus templum vetustate conlapsum inpendio suo restituerunt\n
EDCS-28800100.translation: Sacred to the Unconquered God; for the safety of the Emperor. Lucius Septimius Valerius and Valerianus rebuilt at their own expense a temple which had collapsed from age.
EDCS-28800100.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a temple dedicated to the Unconquered God, funded by Lucius Septimius Valerius and Valerianus, likely dating to the Severan period.

EDCS-28800101.text_edition: \n Deo Invicto Mithrae Vitalis et Silvanus votum solverunt libentes merito\n
EDCS-28800101.translation: Sacred to the Unconquered God Mithras; Vitalis and Silvanus happily and deservedly fulfilled their vow.
EDCS-28800101.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Mithras by Vitalis and Silvanus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28800103.text_edition: \n Petrae Genetrici dei Aurelius Statorius votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-28800103.translation: Sacred to the Mother Goddess Petra; Aurelius Statorius happily and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-28800103.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to the Mother Goddess Petra by Aurelius Statorius, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28800104.text_edition: \n Pollius Danovi filius annorum LXX hic situs est filius pro pietate\n
EDCS-28800104.translation: Pollius, son of Danovius, aged 70, lies here; his son made this out of filial piety.
EDCS-28800104.summary: This is a tombstone for Pollius, son of Danovius, erected by his son, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28800105.text_edition: \n catus Sintaci filius annorum L hic situs est filius et filia pro pietate\n
EDCS-28800105.translation: Catus, son of Sintacus, aged 50, lies here; his son and daughter made this out of filial piety.
EDCS-28800105.summary: This is a tombstone for Catus, son of Sintacus, erected by his son and daughter, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28800106.text_edition: \n Maximus Galli filius FABI R cum suis M ASENS C vixit mensibus XIX diebus ERMA suis VTINO Maximo IH\n
EDCS-28800106.translation: Maximus, son of Gallus, [lived] with his own people [for] 19 months and 19 days. To the late Maximus.
EDCS-28800106.summary: This is a tombstone for Maximus, son of Gallus, recording his age at death and likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28800107.text_edition: \n Corio RETVOI I SERTIT DFI TIVO OOPRIA XXII OPIAO\n
EDCS-28800107.translation: [Fragmentary text, possibly recording names and age.]
EDCS-28800107.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, possibly a tombstone or dedication, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28800108.text_edition: \n Tiberius Claudius Surus et Ulpia Restituta vivi sibi et Claudiae Iuliae filiae annorum XX hic sitae fecerunt\n
EDCS-28800108.translation: Tiberius Claudius Surus and Ulpia Restituta, while alive, made this for themselves and for their daughter Claudia Iulia, aged 20.
EDCS-28800108.summary: This is a funerary monument commissioned by Tiberius Claudius Surus and Ulpia Restituta for themselves and their deceased daughter, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28800109.text_edition: \n Caio Licinio Silvino Auli liberto vivus fecit sibi et Iantumarae incomparabili feminae coniugi optimae obita annorum XX\n
EDCS-28800109.translation: Gaius Licinius Silvinus, freedman of Aulus, made this while alive for himself and for Iantumara, his incomparable and most excellent wife, who died at the age of 20.
EDCS-28800109.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a married couple, erected by the husband during his lifetime, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28800110.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelio Paraseno veterano legionis XIIII Geminae annorum LXX et Cassiae Campanae annorum L Aurelia Ama filia parentibus pro pietate hic siti sunt\n
EDCS-28800110.translation: To the Divine Shades. Aurelius Parasenus, a veteran of the 14th Gemina Legion, aged 70, and Cassia Campana, aged 50. Aurelia Ama, their daughter, placed them here out of filial duty.
EDCS-28800110.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a veteran and his wife, erected by their daughter, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28800111.text_edition: \n Cassus Musae servus nnum C Strubilo Scalleonis liberta uxor nnorum LXXX hic siti est sic filii pos<u=I>erunt \n
EDCS-28800111.translation: Cassus, slave of Musa, aged 100, and Strubilo, freedwoman of Scalleo, his wife, aged 80, are buried here; their sons erected this.
EDCS-28800111.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a slave and his wife, erected by their sons, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28800112.text_edition: \n Neatomarus Clitonis filius nnorum VII hic situs est\n
EDCS-28800112.translation: Neatomarus, son of Clito, aged 7, is buried here.
EDCS-28800112.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a young boy, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28800113.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Ulpius Valentinus veteranus legionis X geminae votum solvit laetus libens merito <c=G>(um) suis \n
