EDCS-28800836.summary: This tombstone records the burial of Claudia Quarta (55) and her son Claudius Cives (80), who arranged for her monument and was also buried there, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28800837.text_edition: \n Lucio Torio Quinto annorum LXX Flavia Valentina viva fecit sibi et coniugi\n
EDCS-28800837.translation: To Lucius Torius Quintus, 70 years old. Flavia Valentina made this while alive for herself and for her husband.
EDCS-28800837.summary: This tombstone records the burial of Lucius Torius Quintus (70) and was erected by Flavia Valentina for herself and her husband, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28800838.text_edition: \n Similis Quarti filius annorum XXXX et VER \n
EDCS-28800838.translation: Similis, son of Quartus, aged 40, and Ver...
EDCS-28800838.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Similis, son of Quartus, who was 40 years old.

EDCS-28800839.text_edition: \n Samuc Respecta annorum viva fecit sibi et Vannio Vivibi annorum L coniugi\n
EDCS-28800839.translation: Samuc Respecta, aged [around 50], made this herself and for her husband Vanni Vivibi, aged 50.
EDCS-28800839.summary: This funerary inscription records a woman named Samuc Respecta, who dedicated a monument for herself and her husband Vanni Vivibi.

EDCS-28800840.text_edition: \n Lucius Atilius Saturninus et Iulia Luci filia Domitia sibi et Lucio Atilio Prisco militi legionis XIIII geminae Martiae victricis annorum XX filio\n
EDCS-28800840.translation: Lucius Atilius Saturninus and Iulia Domitia, daughter of Lucius, for themselves and for their son Lucius Atilius Priscus, soldier of the 14th Legion Gemina Martia Victrix, aged 20.
EDCS-28800840.summary: This funerary inscription records Lucius Atilius Saturninus and Iulia Domitia dedicating a monument for themselves and their son, a soldier in the 14th Legion.

EDCS-28800841.translation: To the spirits of the departed, to Maturo.
EDCS-28800841.summary: This is a brief funerary inscription dedicated to the deceased named Maturo.

EDCS-28900050.text_edition: \n Brogimalinus ARVS PLOPIO ISS ISSI filius A anno--- XXX MR Classici pater posuit\n
EDCS-28900050.translation: Brogimalinus, son of Arvs Plopius Issisi, aged [around 30], his father Classicus placed [this].
EDCS-28900050.summary: This funerary inscription records that Classicus placed a monument for his son Brogimalinus, who was approximately 30 years old.

EDCS-28900051.text_edition: \n Domisi Aionis annorum LX hic situs est et Becna coniux viva parentibus filii posuerunt\n
EDCS-28900051.translation: Domisia, daughter of Aionis, aged 60, is buried here, and her wife Becna, while alive, placed [this] for her parents and children.
EDCS-28900051.summary: This funerary inscription records the burial of Domisia, daughter of Aionis, aged 60, and notes that her wife Becna erected it for her parents and children.

EDCS-28900052.text_edition: \n Iulia Valentina Ulpio Abascanto Ulpiae IVNLEE anno--- XXV et GNI NOT N XIII et VICTI ILNVILI Ulpi-- CONFI N V ME V CI sui OTF\n
EDCS-28900052.translation: Iulia Valentina [erected this] for Abascantus, freedman of Ulpius, aged 25, and Gni Not N, aged 13, and Victi Ilnvili, Ulpi-- Conf... [and others]...
EDCS-28900052.summary: This funerary inscription records Iulia Valentina dedicating a monument for several individuals, including Abascantus, aged 25, and Gni Not N, aged 13.

EDCS-28900053.text_edition: \n Lucco IA Stiponis annorum XXXXV posuit patri\n
EDCS-28900053.translation: Lucco, son of Iastipon, aged 45, placed [this] for his father.
EDCS-28900053.summary: This funerary inscription records Lucco, son of Iastipon, aged 45, placing a monument for his father.

EDCS-28900054.text_edition: \n Magiovinda Domionis filia annorum I hic sita Vabrilo annorum C vivus sibi et coniugi posuit\n
EDCS-28900054.translation: Magiovinda, daughter of Domionis, aged 1, is buried here. Vabrilo, aged 100, while alive, placed [this] for himself and his wife.
EDCS-28900054.summary: This funerary inscription records the burial of an infant named Magiovinda and a monument erected by Vabrilo, aged 100, for himself and his wife.

EDCS-28900056.text_edition: \n Caius Pitius Cai libertus Iucundus TI TO \n
EDCS-28900056.translation: Gaius Pitius Iucundus, freedman of Caius, TI TO...
EDCS-28900056.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription for Gaius Pitius Iucundus, a freedman of Gaius.

EDCS-28900057.text_edition: \n VCOannorum LXXX et Rutilio Gemino annorum III hic siti sunt Rutilia Ursa et Rutilia Sancta matri et avo et filio pro pietate faciendum curaverunt et Longinio Olympo vivo\n
EDCS-28900057.translation: Rutilia Ursa and Rutilia Sancta are buried here, aged 80 and 3 respectively, along with their mother and grandfather and son. Longinius Olympus arranged for this to be done in his lifetime.
EDCS-28900057.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Rutilia Ursa and Rutilia Sancta, mentioning family members and the dedicator Longinius Olympus, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-28900058.text_edition: \n Vassa Saccavi filia annorum C hic sita est Tessillo Vindoroici filius nnorum XXXXV hic situs est Lippo parentibus posuit\n
EDCS-28900058.translation: Vassa, daughter of Saccavius, aged 100, is buried here. Tessillus, son of Vindoroicus, aged 45, is buried here. Lippo placed [this] for his parents.
EDCS-28900058.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the burials of Vassa and Tessillus, with Lippo as the dedicator, likely from the late Roman Republic or early Empire.

EDCS-28900060.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription, likely part of a funerary monument, mentioning a husband who was alive at the time of its erection.

EDCS-28900062.text_edition: \n TRIO annorum XX hic sit-- est N \n
EDCS-28900062.summary: This is a very short and incomplete funerary inscription recording the age of an individual.

EDCS-28900068.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Publio Licinio Cornelio Valeriano nobilissimo Caesari principi iuventutis vias et pontes vetustate conlapsas restituit a Vindobona milia passuum II\n
EDCS-28900068.translation: He restored roads and bridges collapsed by age for the Emperor Caesar Publius Licinius Cornelius Valerianus, most noble Caesar, prince of youth, from Vindobona, 2 miles.
EDCS-28900068.summary: This is a dedication for the restoration of roads and bridges undertaken in honor of Caesar Valerianus, likely from the mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-28900071.translation: For Emperor Caesar Gaius Aurelius Valerius Diocletian, Pious, Fortunate, Invincible Augustus, and for Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Valerius Maximian, Pious, Fortunate, Invincible Augustus, and for Flavius Valerius Constantius [and] Galerius Valerius Maximian, most noble Caesars.
EDCS-28900071.summary: This is a dedication to the emperors Diocletian and Maximian, and the Caesars Constantius and Galerius Maximian, likely from the late 3rd or early 4th century CE.

EDCS-28900072.text_edition: \n conla<p=B>sas restituit per alam III Thracum civium Romanorum Decianam a Brigetione milia passuum X \n
EDCS-28900072.translation: collapsed, restored through the 3rd Cohort of Thracian Roman Citizens, the Decian, from Brigetio, 10 miles.
EDCS-28900072.summary: This inscription records the restoration of roads and bridges by the 3rd Cohort of Thracian Roman Citizens (Deciana) between Brigetio and another location, likely from the 3rd century CE.

EDCS-28900076.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Marcus Iulius Philippus invictus Augustus et Marcus Iulius Philippus nobilissimus Caesar et Otacilia Severa mater Augusti et castrorum vias et pontes vetustate conlapsas restituerunt per alam III Thracum Philippianam a Brigetione milia passuum XI\n
EDCS-28900076.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Julius Philippus, invincible Augustus, and Marcus Julius Philippus, most noble Caesar, and Otacilia Severa, mother of the Augustus and of the camp, restored roads and bridges collapsed by age through the 3rd Cohort of Thracians, the Philippian, from Brigetio, 11 miles.
EDCS-28900076.summary: This is a dedication for road and bridge restoration by Emperor Philip the Arab, his son Caesar Philip, and Empress Otacilia Severa, undertaken by the 3rd Cohort of Thracians, Philippiana, likely dating to their reign (244-249 CE).

EDCS-28900077.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Marcus Iulius Philippus Pius Felix invictus Augustus pontifex maximus tribuniciae potestatis consul pater patriae proconsul et Marcia Otacilia Severa sanctissima Augusta con<i>ux Augusti nostri vias et pontes vetustate conlabsas sic per alam III Thracum Philippianam restituerunt a Brigetione milia passuum XI\n
EDCS-28900077.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Julius Philippus, Pious, Fortunate, Invincible Augustus, pontifex maximus, holder of tribunician power, consul, father of the country, proconsul, and Marcia Otacilia Severa, most holy Augusta, wife of our Augustus, restored roads and bridges collapsed by age through the 3rd Cohort of Thracians, the Philippian, from Brigetio, 11 miles.
EDCS-28900077.summary: This is a dedication for road and bridge restoration by Emperor Philip the Arab and Empress Otacilia Severa, undertaken by the 3rd Cohort of Thracians, Philippiana, detailing their titles and likely dating to their reign (244-249 CE).

EDCS-28900080.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Caio Iulio Vero Maximino Pio Felici Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate patri patriae procons(uli) Germanico maximo Dacico maximo Sarmatico maximo et Gaio Iulio Vero Maximo nobilissimo Caesari filio Germanico maximo Dacico maximo Sarmatico maximo vias et pontes vetustate labsas sic restituerunt a Brigetione milia passuum XXXII\n
EDCS-28900080.translation: For Emperor Caesar Gaius Julius Verus Maximinus, Pious, Fortunate, Invincible Augustus, pontifex maximus, holder of tribunician power, father of the country, proconsul, Germanicus Maximus, Dacicus Maximus, Sarmaticus Maximus, and for Gaius Julius Verus Maximus, most noble Caesar, son, Germanicus Maximus, Dacicus Maximus, Sarmaticus Maximus, they restored roads and bridges collapsed by age through the 3rd Cohort of Thracians, the Philippian, from Brigetio, 32 miles.
EDCS-28900080.summary: This is a dedication for road and bridge restoration in honor of Emperor Maximinus Thrax and his son Caesar Maximinus, mentioning their military titles and the 3rd Cohort of Thracians, Philippiana, likely dating to their co-reign (c. 236-238 CE).

EDCS-28900090.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus maximus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate VIIII imperator XI consul II proconsul pater patriae et Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Augustus Felix tribunicia potestate IIII proconsul [ via]s et pontes vetustate corruptas restituerunt curante Fabio Cilone legato Auggustorum pro praetore a Brigetione milia passuum III\n
EDCS-28900090.translation: Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus maximus, chief priest, with the tribunician power for the 9th time, emperor for the 11th time, consul for the 2nd time, proconsul, father of the country, and Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Augustus Felix, with the tribunician power for the 4th time, restored roads and bridges worn out by age, under the supervision of Fabius Cilone, governor of the emperors with praetorian power, 3 miles from Brigetio.
EDCS-28900090.summary: This inscription records the restoration of roads and bridges by Emperors Septimius Severus and Caracalla, supervised by Fabius Cilone, dated to their 9th and 4th tribunician powers respectively, likely in the early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-28900091.text_edition: \n Imperatoris Caesaris Titi Aeli Hadriani Antonini Augusti Pii patris patriae consulis III tribunicia potestate VI a Karnunto milia passuum XXI\n
EDCS-28900091.translation: Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, father of the country, consul for the 3rd time, with the tribunician power for the 6th time, 21 miles from Carnuntum.
EDCS-28900091.summary: This inscription records Emperor Antoninus Pius's restoration of roads, dating to his 6th tribunician power and 3rd consulship, likely in the mid-2nd century CE.

EDCS-28900092.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertianax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus maximus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate VIIII imperator XI consul II pater patriae proconsul et Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Augustus tribunicia potestate proconsul et [Publius Septimius Geta nobilissimus Caesar] per legionem X geminam piam fidelem curante Fabio Cilone legato Auggustorum pro praetore a Karnunto milia passuum XXI\n
EDCS-28900092.translation: Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertianax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus maximus, chief priest, with the tribunician power for the 9th time, emperor for the 11th time, consul for the 2nd time, father of the country, proconsul, and Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Augustus, with tribunician power, proconsul, and [Publius Septimius Geta, most noble Caesar], through the pious and faithful 10th Legion Gemina, under the supervision of Fabius Cilone, governor of the emperors with praetorian power, restored roads and bridges worn out by age, 21 miles from Carnuntum.
EDCS-28900092.summary: This inscription records the restoration of roads and bridges by Septimius Severus, Caracalla, and Geta, through the 10th Legion Gemina and supervised by Fabius Cilone, dating to Severus's 9th tribunician power, likely around 211 CE.

EDCS-28900094.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Marcus Antonius Gordianus Pius Felix Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate consul pater patriae proconsul vias et pontes vetustate conlabsas sic restituit a Karnunto milia passuum XXI\n
EDCS-28900094.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Antonius Gordianus Pius Felix Augustus, chief priest, with the tribunician power, consul, father of the country, proconsul, thus restored roads and bridges collapsed by age, 21 miles from Carnuntum.
EDCS-28900094.summary: This inscription records Emperor Gordian III's restoration of roads and bridges, dated by his tribunician power and consulship, likely in the mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-28900095.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Caius Messius Quintus Traianus Decius Pius Felix Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate consul pater patriae procons(ul) pontes et vias conlapsas vetustate restituit a Karnunto milia passuum XXI\n
EDCS-28900095.translation: Emperor Caesar Gaius Messius Quintus Traianus Decius Pius Felix Augustus, chief priest, with the tribunician power, consul, father of the country, proconsul, restored bridges and roads collapsed by age, 21 miles from Carnuntum.
EDCS-28900095.summary: This inscription records Emperor Decius's restoration of roads and bridges, dated by his tribunician power and consulship, likely in the mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-28900096.text_edition: \n Imperatori Publio Licinio Cornelio Valeriano nobilissimo Caesari principi iuventutis vias et pontes vetustate conlabsas sic restituit XXI\n
EDCS-28900096.translation: To Emperor Publius Licinius Cornelius Valerianus, most noble Caesar, prince of youth, thus restored roads and bridges worn out by age, 21 [miles from Carnuntum?].
EDCS-28900096.summary: This inscription records the restoration of roads and bridges by the Caesar Valerian (later Gallienus's co-emperor), dating to his time as prince of youth, likely in the mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-28900102.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertenax sic Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus maximus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate IX imperator XI consul II proconsul pater patriae et Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus \n
EDCS-28900102.translation: Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus maximus, chief priest, with the tribunician power for the 9th time, emperor for the 11th time, consul for the 2nd time, proconsul, father of the country, and Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus.
EDCS-28900102.summary: This inscription lists the titles and honors of Emperors Septimius Severus and Caracalla, dating to Severus's 9th tribunician power and 11th acclamation as emperor, likely around 211 CE.

EDCS-28900103.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Caius Messius Quintus Decius Traianus Pius Felix Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate pater patriae consul proconsul vias et pontes vetustate conlapsas restituit a Vindobona milia passuum IIII\n
EDCS-28900103.translation: Emperor Caesar Gaius Messius Quintus Decius Traianus Pius Felix Augustus, chief priest, with the tribunician power, father of the country, consul, proconsul, restored roads and bridges collapsed by age, 4 miles from Vindobona.
EDCS-28900103.summary: This inscription records Emperor Decius's restoration of roads and bridges, dated by his tribunician power and consulship, likely in the mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-28900104.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Publio Licinio Cornelio Valeriano nobilissimo Caesari principi iuventutis vias et pontes vetustate conlabsas sic restituit a Vindobona IIII\n
EDCS-28900104.translation: To Emperor Caesar Publius Licinius Cornelius Valerianus, most noble Caesar, prince of youth, thus restored roads and bridges worn out by age, 4 miles from Vindobona.
EDCS-28900104.summary: This inscription records the restoration of roads and bridges by the Caesar Valerian (later Gallienus's co-emperor), dating to his time as prince of youth, likely in the mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-28900105.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Publio Licinio Valeriano Pio Felici Augusto tribunicia potestate consuli proconsuli et Licinio [[Gallienio]] Augusto a Vindobona milia passuum IIII\n
EDCS-28900105.translation: To Emperor Caesar Publius Licinius Valerianus Pius Felix Augustus, with the tribunician power, consul, proconsul, and [Licinius] Gallienus Augustus, 4 miles from Vindobona.
EDCS-28900105.summary: This inscription honors Emperors Valerian and Gallienus, mentioning Valerian's titles and their presence or activity 4 miles from Vindobona, likely in the mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-29000320.text_edition: \n Nemesi Reginae pro salute ddominorum nnostrorum Auggustorum sacrum Caius Iulius Constans beneficiarius consularis et Iulia Severa eius tempulum sic constituerunt pro se suisque omnibus incolumibus votum solventes libentes merito\n
EDCS-29000320.translation: Sacred to Nemesis Regina for the safety of our lords the Emperors. Caius Julius Constans, a consular beneficiarius, and Iulia Severa, his wife, joyfully and deservedly fulfilled their vow by establishing this temple for themselves and all their household, that they may be safe.
EDCS-29000320.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a temple to Nemesis Regina by Caius Julius Constans and Iulia Severa for the well-being of the imperial family and their own family.

EDCS-29000321.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marcus Aurelius Castinus miles legionis I adiutricis vivus fecit sibi et Flaviae Dignae coniugi piae annorum XXX et Aurelio Castiniano filio annorum et Aureliae Quietae filiae annorum et Aurelio Maximo filio annorum\n
EDCS-29000321.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Marcus Aurelius Castinus, a soldier of the I Adiutrix legion, made this monument in his lifetime for himself, Flavia Digna, his pious wife, aged 30, and for his son Aurelius Castinianus, aged [...], and for his daughter Aurelia Quieta, aged [...], and for his son Aurelius Maximus, aged [...].
EDCS-29000321.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Marcus Aurelius Castinus, a soldier of the I Adiutrix legion, commemorating himself and his family, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-29000322.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Marcus Trebius Vitalis beneficiarius consularis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-29000322.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest. Marcus Trebius Vitalis, a consular beneficiarius, joyfully and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-29000322.summary: This inscription records the fulfillment of a vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Marcus Trebius Vitalis, a military official.

EDCS-29000327.summary: This is a fragment of an inscription, likely recording an event related to crossing something, with the rest lost.

EDCS-29000331.text_edition: \n Fortunae sacrum Priscinius Hilario miles legionis XXX Ulpiae victricis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-29000331.translation: Sacred to Fortuna. Priscinius Hilario, a soldier of the XXX Ulpia Victrix legion, joyfully and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-29000331.summary: This inscription records the fulfillment of a vow to Fortuna by Priscinius Hilario, a soldier of the XXX Ulpia Victrix legion.

EDCS-29000332.text_edition: \n Fortunae Mercurio Silvano Auggustis Aurelius Bassus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-29000332.translation: To Fortuna, Mercury, and Silvanus, sacred to the Emperors. Aurelius Bassus joyfully and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-29000332.summary: This inscription records the fulfillment of a vow to Fortuna, Mercury, and Silvanus by Aurelius Bassus, likely dedicating it to the emperors as well.

EDCS-29000335.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest. Aelius Lascius [...]
EDCS-29000335.summary: This is a partial inscription dedicating something to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Aelius Lascius.

EDCS-29000336.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo sacrum Lucius Albucius Claudia Albucianus Miseno |(centurio) legionis I adiutricis piae fidelis\n
EDCS-29000336.translation: Sacred to Jupiter Best and Greatest. Lucius Albucius and Claudia Albucianus, his wife, (dedicate this to) Miseno, centurion of the pious and faithful I Adiutrix legion.
EDCS-29000336.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Lucius Albucius and Claudia Albucianus, likely in honor of a centurion named Miseno.

EDCS-29000337.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo votum solvit libens merito Atilius Favor\n
EDCS-29000337.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest. Atilius Favor fulfilled his vow joyfully and deservedly.
EDCS-29000337.summary: This inscription records the fulfillment of a vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Atilius Favor.

EDCS-29000338.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo pro salute Impperatorum ddominorum nnostrorum Auggustorum Aurelius Vitalis veteranus pro se et Tolesibus omnibus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-29000338.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, for the safety of the Emperors, our lords. Aurelius Vitalis, a veteran, joyfully and deservedly fulfilled his vow for himself and all his household.
EDCS-29000338.summary: This inscription records the fulfillment of a vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by the veteran Aurelius Vitalis for the safety of the emperors and his own family.

EDCS-29000339.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Claudius Claudianus optio legionis I adiutricis votum solvit libens\n
EDCS-29000339.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Claudius Claudianus, optio of the I Adiutrix legion, willingly paid his vow.
EDCS-29000339.summary: This is a votive inscription to Jupiter, dedicated by Claudius Claudianus, an optio of the I Adiutrix legion, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-29000340.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo pro salute domini nostri Imperatoris Caesaris Marci Aureli Severi Alexandri Pii Felicis Augusti Caius Iulius Maximinus custos armorum legionis I adiutricis Severianae templum vicalem a solo inpendiis suis caemento exstructum ex voto restituit\n
EDCS-29000340.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, for the safety of our lord Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Pius Felix Augustus, Caius Julius Maximinus, custos armorum of the I Adiutrix Severiana legion, rebuilt the local temple, constructed from its foundations at his own expense with cement, according to his vow.
EDCS-29000340.summary: This is a votive and dedicatory inscription to Jupiter, recording the rebuilding of a local temple by Caius Julius Maximinus, a custos armorum of the I Adiutrix Severiana legion, during the reign of Severus Alexander (222-235 AD).

EDCS-29000341.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Iulius Messius veteranus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-29000341.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Julius Messius, a veteran, willingly and deservedly paid his vow.
EDCS-29000341.summary: This is a votive inscription to Jupiter, dedicated by Julius Messius, a veteran, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-29000342.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Silvinus GESA RA votum solvit libens laetus merito\n
EDCS-29000342.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Silvinus GESA RA willingly, joyfully, and deservedly paid his vow.
EDCS-29000342.summary: This is a votive inscription to Jupiter, dedicated by Silvinus, whose specific role is unclear due to abbreviation, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-29000344.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Publius Titius Victorinus cornicularius legionis II adiutricis ila votum solvit libens \n
EDCS-29000344.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Publius Titius Victorinus, cornicularius of the I Adiutrix legion ila, willingly paid his vow.
EDCS-29000344.summary: This is a votive inscription to Jupiter, dedicated by Publius Titius Victorinus, a cornicularius of the I Adiutrix legion, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-29000345.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Ulpius Severus d votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-29000345.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Ulpius Severus d., willingly and deservedly paid his vow.
EDCS-29000345.summary: This is a votive inscription to Jupiter, dedicated by Ulpius Severus, whose exact title is abbreviated, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-29000347.text_edition: \n Iovi Dolicheno Valerius Hermes votum solvit libens\n
EDCS-29000347.translation: To Jupiter Dolichenus, Valerius Hermes willingly paid his vow.
EDCS-29000347.summary: This is a votive inscription to Jupiter Dolichenus, dedicated by Valerius Hermes, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-29000348.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo exauditori et Fortunae fortissimae legionis I adiutricis piae fidelis Severianae Titus Sere Diogenes |(centurio) votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-29000348.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, the hearer, and to the most valiant Fortuna of the I Adiutrix pious faithful Severiana legion, Titus Sere Diogenes, centurion, willingly and deservedly paid his vow.
EDCS-29000348.summary: This is a votive inscription to Jupiter and Fortuna, dedicated by Titus Sere Diogenes, a centurion of the I Adiutrix pious faithful Severiana legion, likely from the 3rd century AD.

EDCS-29000349.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo et Iunoni Reginae pro salutem sic imperii Claudius Eltta et Claudius Citanus Claudius Felix magister primus Eleuther et M OVIIIA Claudi Felicis magistri Claudi Felicitas Aeterna Bassus signifer legionis I Claudius Titianus Claudius Victor miles legionis I Claudius Crescentinus Claudius Crescens Claudia Maura liberta \n
EDCS-29000349.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest and to Juno Regina, for the safety of the empire, Claudius Eltta and Claudius Citanus, Claudius Felix, first magister of Eleuther and M OVIIIA, the magistri of Claudius Felix, Claudius Felicitas Aeterna, Bassus, signifer of the I legion, Claudius Titianus, Claudius Victor, soldier of the I legion, Claudius Crescentinus, Claudius Crescens, Claudia Maura, freedwoman.
EDCS-29000349.summary: This is a complex communal votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter and Juno Regina for the safety of the empire, listing numerous members and officials, including soldiers and freedmen, of the I legion, likely from the 3rd century AD.

EDCS-29000350.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Iunoni Reginae Marti Aurelius cinus immunis legionis I adiutricis piae fidelis [Maximinianae] votum solvit libens merito Imperatore Caesare [domino nostro Maxi][mino Augusto] et Africano consulibus IIII Kalendas Ianuarias dedicavit\n
EDCS-29000350.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest and to Juno Regina and to Mars, Aurelius cinus, immunis of the I Adiutrix pious faithful [Maximinian] legion, willingly and deservedly paid his vow; dedicated on the fourth day before the Kalends of January, when Caesar [our lord Maxi][minus Augustus] and Africanus were consuls.
EDCS-29000350.summary: This is a votive and dedicatory inscription to Jupiter, Juno Regina, and Mars, dedicated by Aurelius cinus, an immunis of the I Adiutrix legion, on January 1st during the consulship of Maximinus Augustus and Africanus (likely 236 AD).

EDCS-29000353.text_edition: \n Silvano Sancto Domestico votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-29000353.translation: To the Holy Silvanus Domesticus, he willingly fulfilled his vow, as was deserved.
EDCS-29000353.summary: This inscription is a votive offering to the god Silvanus Domesticus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29000354.text_edition: \n Silvano Domestico Publius Aelius Eupropes votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-29000354.translation: To Silvanus Domesticus, Publius Aelius Eupropes willingly fulfilled his vow, as was deserved.
EDCS-29000354.summary: This inscription records a votive offering by Publius Aelius Eupropes to Silvanus Domesticus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29000355.text_edition: \n Silvano Domestico Iulius Fuscinus veteranus legionis I adutricis pro se et pro suis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-29000355.translation: To Silvanus Domesticus, Iulius Fuscinus, a veteran of the First Adjutrix Legion, willingly and gladly fulfilled his vow for himself and his family, as was deserved.
EDCS-29000355.summary: This inscription records a votive offering by Iulius Fuscinus, a veteran of Legio I Adjutrix, to Silvanus Domesticus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29000356.translation: A vow to Silvanus, the forest god.
EDCS-29000356.summary: This inscription is a short dedication to Silvanus, possibly indicating a votive offering, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29000357.text_edition: \n Mithrae Donnius votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-29000357.translation: To Mithras, Donnius willingly fulfilled his vow, as was deserved.
EDCS-29000357.summary: This inscription is a votive offering by Donnius to the god Mithras, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29000358.text_edition: \n Cautopati Marcus Masiac Maternianus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-29000358.translation: To Cautopates, Marcus Masiac Maternianus willingly fulfilled his vow, as was deserved.
EDCS-29000358.summary: This inscription records a votive offering by Marcus Masiac Maternianus to Cautopates, a figure associated with Mithras, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29000359.text_edition: \n Invicto Deo Mithrae Masuinnius Amicus Augustalis municipii Brigetionis Antoniniani votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-29000359.translation: To the Unconquered God Mithras, Masuinnius Amicus, Augustalis of the municipium of Brigetio Antoniniana, willingly and gladly fulfilled his vow, as was deserved.
EDCS-29000359.summary: This inscription records a votive offering by Masuinnius Amicus, an official from Brigetio, to Mithras, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period, possibly the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-29000360.text_edition: \n Terrae Materi et ministrae Priscillae ob commendatam et restitutam fidem Aelius Stratonicus votum libens laetus merito Brigetione\n
EDCS-29000360.translation: To Mother Earth and her attendant Priscilla, for the trust and restoration of faith, Aelius Stratonicus willingly and gladly fulfilled his vow at Brigetio.
EDCS-29000360.summary: This inscription records a votive offering by Aelius Stratonicus to Mother Earth and her attendant Priscilla, likely from Brigetio, for trust and restored faith, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29000362.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, possibly a name or title, with an incomplete dedication or record, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29000366.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus et perpetuae securitati Aeli Sabini pecuari legionis I adutricis Plotia Ulpia coniugi faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-29000366.translation: For the Spirits of the Departed and for the perpetual security of Aelius Sabinus, a soldier of the First Adjutrix Legion, Plotia Ulpia arranged for this to be made for her husband.
EDCS-29000366.summary: This inscription is a funerary dedication arranged by Plotia Ulpia for her husband Aelius Sabinus, a soldier of Legio I Adjutrix, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29000368.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marco Aurelio Emerito domo Sergia Karnunto |(centurioni) legionis I adiutricis Aureliae Maximinae filiae Aelia Faustina uxor heredes faciendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-29000368.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Marcus Aurelius Emeritus, from Colonia, former centurion of the I Adiutrix Legion. Aurelia Maximina, his daughter, and Aelia Faustina, his wife, and heirs saw to the construction.
EDCS-29000368.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Marcus Aurelius Emeritus, a former centurion of the I Adiutrix Legion, erected by his daughter and wife.

EDCS-29000369.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelio Plotiano militi legionis I adiutricis stipendiorum V annorum XXV Aurelius Iulius veteranus et Aurelia Festina parentes vivi faciendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-29000369.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Aurelius Plotianus, soldier of the I Adiutrix Legion, with 5 years of service and aged 25. Aurelius Iulius, a veteran, and Aurelia Festina, his parents, while alive, saw to the construction.
EDCS-29000369.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Aurelius Plotianus, a soldier of the I Adiutrix Legion, erected by his parents.

EDCS-29000370.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lictoriae Restutae quae vixit annos XXXXI faciendum curavit Aurelius Romulianus beneficiarius coniugi amantissimae ac obsequentissimae\n
EDCS-29000370.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Lictoria Restuta, who lived 41 years. Aurelius Romulianus, a beneficiary soldier, saw to the construction for his most loving and obedient wife.
EDCS-29000370.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Lictoria Restuta, erected by her husband, Aurelius Romulianus.

EDCS-29000372.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Bononius Vitalis annorum XLII Aur(elius) Ianuarius veteranus legionis I adiutricis piae fidelis IAIIONISINVS et Aelia Ingenua marito suo pientissimo posuerunt\n
EDCS-29000372.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Bononius Vitalis, aged 42. Aurelius Ianuarius, a veteran of the I Adiutrix Legion, pious and loyal, and Aelia Ingenua placed this for their most devoted husband.
EDCS-29000372.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Bononius Vitalis, a veteran of the I Adiutrix Legion, erected by his wife and possibly another veteran.

EDCS-29000373.text_edition: \n menses XI et dies XV Caecilius Rufus tribunus militum legionis I adiutricis et Ovidia Tertulla filiae dulcissimae ponendum curavit\n
EDCS-29000373.translation: For 11 months and 15 days. Caecilius Rufus, military tribune of the I Adiutrix Legion, and Ovidia Tertulla saw to the placement for their dearest daughter.
EDCS-29000373.summary: This inscription records the duration of a daughter's life (11 months, 15 days) and indicates her parents, a military tribune and his wife, commissioned her monument.

EDCS-29000374.text_edition: \n Marcus Iulius Iustus militabit sic sacro comitatu Augustalis qui vixit annos XL et Maxentiae coniugi eius quae vixit annos XXXV Proculinus miles legionis primae adiutricis sorori suae et conpari ob memoriam ponendum curavit sepulchrum fecit\n
EDCS-29000374.translation: Marcus Iulius Iustus will serve in the sacred Augustan cohort; he lived 40 years. And for Maxentia, his wife, who lived 35 years. Proculinus, soldier of the First Adiutrix Legion, erected this for his sister and companion in memory.
EDCS-29000374.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Marcus Iulius Iustus and his wife Maxentia, erected by Proculinus, possibly a relative serving in the I Adiutrix Legion.

EDCS-29000375.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucio Luciano Flavia Festo Siscia militi legionis XIIII geminae stipendiorum XXII Marcus Ulpius Victor bucinator legionis eiusdem heres faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-29000375.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Lucius Lucianus, soldier of the XIV Gemina Legion, with 22 years of service. Marcus Ulpius Victor, a bucinator (horn player) of the same legion, his heir, saw to the construction.
EDCS-29000375.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Lucius Lucianus, a soldier of the XIV Gemina Legion, erected by his heir, Marcus Ulpius Victor, also a soldier in the same legion.

EDCS-29000376.text_edition: \n Marcus Munatius Marci filius Collina Placidus Icconio sic veteranus legionis I adiutricis piae fidelis annorum L hic situs est Tertius Abascantus domestic(us) patrono bene merenti\n
EDCS-29000376.translation: Marcus Munatius Placidus, son of Marcus, of the Collina tribe. Here lies Icconius, a veteran of the I Adiutrix Legion, pious and loyal, aged 50. Tertius Abascantus, a domestic official, for his well-deserving patron.
EDCS-29000376.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Icconius, a veteran of the I Adiutrix Legion, erected by his domestic official, Tertius Abascantus.

EDCS-29000377.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucio Retonio Lucio quondam primo pilo legionis I adiutricis qui vixit nnos LXXVIII stipendiorum LVIII Retonii Romanus et Saturninus et Lucilla filii faciendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-29000377.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Lucius Retonius Lucius, formerly primus pilus (chief centurion) of the I Adiutrix Legion, who lived 78 years and had 58 years of service. Retonii Romanus, Saturninus, and Lucilla, his sons, saw to the construction.
EDCS-29000377.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Lucius Retonius Lucius, a former primus pilus of the I Adiutrix Legion, erected by his sons.

EDCS-29000378.text_edition: \n Rutilius Marcellinus patri matri fratribus et sororibus nepties sic nepotibus et Icaro filius sic Mario legionis I adiutricis qui vixit nnos IXXX menses V dies X SIFCIVSLIVS nnos menses I fili sic meae sic et Martiniano filiem sic praesentem sic qui vixit nnos XXXV menses V dies XIII et Aureliae coniugi eius qui sic vixit nnos X menses IIII dies V parentes vivi posuerunt\n
EDCS-29000378.translation: Rutilius Marcellinus for his father, mother, brothers, and sisters, and his grandsons, and Icarus, son of Marius, of the I Adiutrix Legion, who lived 80 years, 5 months, and 10 days. [And for whom?] ... [and for] Martinianus, his son, who lived 35 years, 5 months, and 13 days, and for Aurelia, his wife, who lived 40 years, 2 months, and 5 days. The parents, while alive, placed this.
EDCS-29000378.summary: This is a complex funerary inscription erected by parents for multiple family members, including a soldier of the I Adiutrix Legion named Icarus and his younger brother Martinianus, and their mother Aurelia.

EDCS-29000379.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Sempronio qui Leontio filio carissimo qui vixit annos II menses III Sempronius Romulus candidatus legionis I adiutricis pater faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-29000379.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Sempronius Romulus, a candidate for office and a soldier in the I Adiutrix legion, had this made for his dearest son, Leontius, who lived for 2 years and 3 months.
EDCS-29000379.summary: A funerary epitaph for a young boy, commissioned by his father who was a candidate and a soldier in the Legio I Adiutrix. The inscription is likely from the Roman Empire period.

EDCS-29000381.text_edition: \n -? Valerio Firmino annorum VI Lucio Valerio Longino annorum IIII coniugi et filiis piis Lucius Valerius Longinus veteranus legionis I adiutricis piae fidelis vivus sibi et suis fecit\n
EDCS-29000381.translation: Lucius Valerius Longinus, a veteran of the I Adiutrix legion, pious and faithful, alive, had this made for himself and his own, for Valerius Firmus, aged 6, and Lucius Valerius Longinus, aged 4, his devoted husband and sons.
EDCS-29000381.summary: A funerary epitaph commissioned by a veteran of the Legio I Adiutrix for his deceased husband and sons, and for himself and his family. The inscription is likely from the Roman Empire period.

EDCS-29000382.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Valerius Ursinus ducenarius qui vixit nnos XXV menses III dies V Aurelia Firmilla filia quae vixit nnos VIIII menses III dies V <e=I>t Flavius Firmus miles legionis I adiutricis qui vixit <a=V>nnos XLV menses V dies VII Aurelia Ursicina fratri et filiae et coniugi suo bene merentibus sepulcrum faciendum curavit vivite felices qui<b=R>u<s=R> est fortuna beata \n
EDCS-29000382.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Valerius Ursinus, a *duценarius* (officer), who lived 25 years, 3 months, and 5 days; Aurelia Firmilla, his daughter, who lived 9 years, 3 months, and 5 days; and Flavius Firmus, a soldier of the I Adiutrix legion, who lived 45 years, 5 months, and 7 days. Aurelia Ursicina had this sepulchre made for her brother, daughter, and husband, who deserved well of her. Live happy, you who are here, by blessed fortune.
EDCS-29000382.summary: A funerary epitaph for a soldier, his daughter, and husband, commissioned by Aurelia Ursicina, likely their relative, mentioning their ages and military affiliation. The inscription is likely from the Roman Empire period.

EDCS-29000383.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Valeriae Valerianae quae vixit nnos LX Valerius Valerianus tesserarius legionis I adiutricis et Valeria Valerina matri dulcissimae faciendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-29000383.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Valerius Valerianus, a *tesserarius* (junior officer) of the I Adiutrix legion, and Valeria Valerina had this made for their dearest mother, Valeria Valeriana, who lived 60 years.
EDCS-29000383.summary: A funerary epitaph commissioned by a soldier and his sister for their mother, mentioning her age and his military rank in the Legio I Adiutrix. The inscription is likely from the Roman Empire period.

EDCS-29000384.text_edition: \n vio ano tribuno militum legionis I adiutricis VA -CARTACONAM et RPSLS VAPRINICA ILI fecIS \n
EDCS-29000384.translation: To Vius, son of Anus, military tribune of the I Adjutrix legion, for the carts and the public roads of Vaprinca, he made [this].
EDCS-29000384.summary: This is a milestone or road marker dedicated by Vius, a military tribune, to improve the infrastructure of Vaprinca, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-29000385.text_edition: \n IRIVA MELAI Troianio Pitoniato equiti militi legionis I adutricis piae fidelis Arteia Exsuperata soror ADRIVIRVS\n
EDCS-29000385.translation: To Troianus Pitoniatus, equestrian soldier of the I Adjutrix Pia Fidelis legion. His sister Arteia Exsuperata made this for him.
EDCS-29000385.summary: This funerary inscription honors Troianus Pitoniatus, a cavalry soldier, and was erected by his sister Arteia Exsuperata, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-29000386.summary: This is a very brief funerary inscription for a soldier named Sabinus, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-29000389.text_edition: \n Memoriae Titi quondam Domnini sive Passeris negotianti splendido qui vixit annis XXVI interfecto a barbaris Titius Domninus pater infelix filiorum in hoc sarcofago duo corpora posuit in memoriam Titi Ursiniani qui vixit nnos XVIII Titius Domninus Augustalis municipii Brigetionis filio pientissimo faciendum curavit \n
EDCS-29000389.translation: In memory of Titus, formerly Domninus or Passer, a distinguished merchant, who was killed by barbarians. Titius Domninus, an unhappy father, placed two bodies in this sarcophagus in memory of Titus Ursinianus, who lived 18 years. Titius Domninus Augustalis of the municipality of Brigetio had this made for his most devoted son.
EDCS-29000389.summary: This is a sarcophagus inscription commemorating Titus Ursinianus and his brother Titus Domninus, both killed by barbarians, with the father Titius Domninus having the monument erected, likely dating to the 3rd century AD.

EDCS-29000391.text_edition: \n Quintus Antonius Quinti filius Claudia Firmus Savaria anorum XVII hic situs Hispana fratri fecit\n
EDCS-29000391.translation: Quintus Antonius Firmus, son of Quintus, 17 years old, from Savaria, lies here. His brother Hispana made this for him.
EDCS-29000391.summary: This funerary inscription records Quintus Antonius Firmus, who died at age 17, and was made by his brother Hispana, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-29000392.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelia Vera Aurelio Arriano coniugi carissimo\n
EDCS-29000392.translation: To the departed spirits of Aurelia Vera for her dearest husband Aurelius Arrianus.
EDCS-29000392.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated by Aurelia Vera to her husband Aurelius Arrianus, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-29000393.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aureliae Ursinae sorori posuit Septimius Atta frater faciendum curavit quae vixit nnos XXII opto sit tibi terra levis\n
EDCS-29000393.translation: To the Divine Shades. Septimius Atta erected this for his sister Aurelia Ursina, whom he took care to have made. She lived 22 years. May the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-29000393.summary: A tombstone set up by a brother for his deceased sister, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29000394.text_edition: \n A est Aurelio Basso TVC optamus sit tibi terra levis\n
EDCS-29000394.translation: To Aurelius Bassus. We wish you, TVC, may the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-29000394.summary: A tombstone for Aurelius Bassus, with an unknown abbreviation TVC, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29000395.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Cal Severo Claudiae Liccanae coniugibus Claudio Calvo filio et Ulpiae Proballae annorum V Iustinus filius fecit parentibus hic siti sunt\n
EDCS-29000395.translation: To the Divine Shades. For Claudius Calvus and Ulpia Proballa, his parents, and for Severus, his son. Iustinus made this for his parents who are buried here.
EDCS-29000395.summary: A funerary monument erected by a son, Iustinus, for his parents Cal Severus and Claudia Liccana, and his brother Severus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29000396.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Corneliae Valentinae annorum XIX Samuconia Marcellina filiae posuit\n
EDCS-29000396.translation: To the Divine Shades. Samuconia Marcellina set this up for her daughter Cornelia Valentina, who was 19 years old.
EDCS-29000396.summary: A tombstone erected by a mother for her deceased daughter, Cornelia Valentina, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29000397.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Helvio Vetali sic et Floro filio Helvia Successa patrono coniugi pientissumo sic et sibi viva fecit\n
EDCS-29000397.translation: To the Divine Shades. Helvia Successa made this while alive for her husband Helvius Vetali, most devoted patron, and for her son Florus, and for herself.
EDCS-29000397.summary: A funerary monument erected by a woman, Helvia Successa, for her deceased husband, son, and herself, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29000398.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Pompeiae Secundae nnorum XXII Quintus Pompeius Fortunatus coniugi optimae \n
EDCS-29000398.translation: To the Divine Shades. Quintus Pompeius Fortunatus for his most excellent wife Pompeia Secunda, who was 22 years old.
EDCS-29000398.summary: A tombstone set up by a husband for his deceased wife, Pompeia Secunda, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29000399.text_edition: \n Tito Soranio Titi filio domo Archelaide Felici annorum XL Sorania Agathe coniugi bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-29000399.translation: To Titus Soranius, son of Titus, from Archelaide, called Felix, 40 years old. Sorania Agathe, his deserving wife, made this for him.
EDCS-29000399.summary: This funerary inscription honors Titus Soranius, who died at age 40, and was erected by his wife Sorania Agathe, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-29000400.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marcus Ulpius filius nnorum Marcus Ulpius IHA pater P\n
EDCS-29000400.translation: To the Divine Shades. Marcus Ulpius, son of Marcus Ulpius, who was [---] years old. His father, Marcus Ulpius, [erected this].
EDCS-29000400.summary: A fragmentary tombstone commemorating Marcus Ulpius, son of Marcus Ulpius, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29000401.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Sex N IA annorum XXX Cnaeus Terentius Cra<c=I>ilis I AD monumentum heres posuit \n
EDCS-29000401.translation: To the Divine Shades. Cnaeus Terentius Cra[c]ilis, 30 years old, erected this monument; his heir as well.
EDCS-29000401.summary: A tombstone erected by Cnaeus Terentius Cra(c)ilis for himself or another, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29000403.text_edition: \n Alminus RID F annorum XXV titulum posuer<u=O>nt filio hered<e=D>s \n
EDCS-29000403.translation: Alminus RID F, 25 years old. His heirs set up this inscription.
EDCS-29000403.summary: A tombstone erected by the heirs for Alminus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29000404.text_edition: \n D(is) M(anibus) et memoriae V qui vixit annos Ur[cunia Ma------\n
EDCS-29000404.translation: To the spirits of the departed and the memory of Ur[---] who lived for [---] years.
EDCS-29000404.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription dedicated to the memory of an individual named Ur[---], likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29000414.text_edition: \n Marcus Aurelius us vexillarius alae primae contariorum Antoniniana et Elviae Bonae coniugi carissimae et filiae Aureliae Valerinae et Aeliae Sabinae et Elvio Verecundo annorum LXX Pulchrae Suri annorum LX et Elvio Vitali annorum XVIII et Aurelio Masuetiano annorum VII et Aelio Florido Marcus Aurelius Valerianus sibi et suis vivi fecerunt\n
EDCS-29000414.translation: Marcus Aurelius Valerianus, a standard-bearer of the First Wing of Contari (Catafractarii) Antoniniana, made this for himself and his family: his most cherished wife Elvia Bona, his daughters Aurelia Valeriana and Aelia Sabina, Pulchra Suri, aged 60, Elvius Verecundus, aged 70, Elvius Vitalis, aged 18, and Aurelius Masuetianus, aged 7.
EDCS-29000414.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by Marcus Aurelius Valerianus for himself and his family, including his wife, daughters, and several other individuals with their ages, likely dating to the Antonine or Severan period.

EDCS-29000420.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo et Iunoni Reginae pro salute Aeli Rufiniani decurionis municipii Mogetianae Aelius Rufinus \n
EDCS-29000420.translation: For Jupiter Optimus Maximus and Juno Regina, for the safety of Aelius Rufinianus, a decurion of the municipium of Mogetiana, Aelius Rufinus [dedicated this].
EDCS-29000420.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter and Juno by Aelius Rufinus for the well-being of Aelius Rufinianus, a decurion of Mogetiana, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29000421.text_edition: \n Marco Aurelio Marci filio Lucano veterano ex decurione alae III Thracum Severianae TI \n
EDCS-29000421.translation: To Marcus Aurelius Lucanus, son of Marcus, a veteran and former decurion of the Third Wing of Thracian Horsemen Severiana.
EDCS-29000421.summary: This inscription records the name and military career of Marcus Aurelius Lucanus, a veteran and former decurion, likely dating to the Severan period.

EDCS-29000422.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Aurelius Ianuarius tribunus Batavorum vir perfectissimus dux pro salute sua votum merito libens solvit ddominis nnostris VIII et VII Auggustis conssulibus die Iduum Iuliarum\n
EDCS-29000422.translation: Aurelius Ianuarius, tribune of the Batavians, a man of highest distinction, dutifully and gladly fulfilled his vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, the Protector, for his own safety and that of our lords, the emperors, during the consulship of the Eighth and Seventh Augusti, on the Ides of July.
EDCS-29000422.summary: This is a votive inscription by Aurelius Ianuarius, a high-ranking military tribune, to Jupiter for the safety of himself and the emperors, likely dating to the period of the co-emperors, possibly the 3rd century AD.

EDCS-29000423.text_edition: \n Silvano Domestico Spurius Primitivus miles legionis I adiutricis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-29000423.translation: Spurius Primitivus, a soldier of the First Legion Adiutrix, dutifully and gladly fulfilled his vow to Silvanus Domesticus.
EDCS-29000423.summary: This is a votive inscription by a soldier of the First Legion Adiutrix to Silvanus Domesticus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29000425.text_edition: \n Silvano Domestico sacrum Aurelia Felica votum solvit\n
EDCS-29000425.translation: Aurelia Felica gladly fulfilled her vow to Silvanus Domesticus.
EDCS-29000425.summary: This is a votive inscription by Aurelia Felica to Silvanus Domesticus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29000426.text_edition: \n Balti Augusto sacrum Aurelia Euretane votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-29000426.translation: Aurelia Euretane gladly and dutifully dedicated this sacred offering to Baltus Augustus.
EDCS-29000426.summary: This is a votive inscription by Aurelia Euretane to a deity identified as Baltus Augustus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29000428.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Conservatori sacrum Caius Stat(ius) Firmus pro se et suis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-29000428.translation: Gaius Statius Firmus, for himself and his family, dutifully and gladly fulfilled his vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, the Protector.
EDCS-29000428.summary: This is a votive inscription by Caius Statius Firmus for his family to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29000429.text_edition: \n Libero Patri Tarrutenius Ianuarius votum solvit merito\n
EDCS-29000429.translation: Tarrutenius Ianuarius fulfilled his vow to Liber Pater.
EDCS-29000429.summary: This is a votive inscription by Tarrutenius Ianuarius to Liber Pater, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29000430.text_edition: \n Nemesi Augustae sacrum Valerius Ursus vir sacerdotalis omnibus honoribus functus votum solvit libens laetus merito Satrius Firminus antestis sic patrono\n
EDCS-29000430.translation: Sacred to Augusta Nemesis. Valerius Ursus, a man who has held all priestly offices, gladly and joyfully fulfilled his vow as is deserved. Satrius Firminus, the presiding official, [did this] for his patron.
EDCS-29000430.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by Valerius Ursus, a former priest, to the goddess Nemesis, with Satrius Firminus acting as presiding official, likely from the 2nd-3rd century CE.

EDCS-29000432.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription that begins with the word 'Sphin' possibly referring to Sphinxes, its purpose is unclear.

EDCS-29000433.text_edition: \n Spincibus sic Auggustis sacrum Domitia Victorina - EN I \n
EDCS-29000433.summary: This inscription is dedicated to the 'Augustan Spincibus' by Domitia Victorina, the full context and date are unclear due to damage.

EDCS-29000435.text_edition: \n AIIIA sacrum Iulius Tullinus et Aurelius Antipater cives \n
EDCS-29000435.translation: Sacred... Julius Tullinus and Aurelius Antipater, citizens...
EDCS-29000435.summary: This inscription records a dedication to an unnamed deity by two citizens, Julius Tullinus and Aurelius Antipater, with the rest of the text missing.

EDCS-29000437.summary: This inscription contains only a partial name, Publius Antonius, with no context or further information available.

EDCS-29000438.text_edition: \n INOLLC annorum LX et Apuleiae Faustinae coniugi annorum et Titus Aurelius Faustinianus et Aurelia Respecta filii heredes ex testamento faciendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-29000438.translation: ...aged sixty years, and for his wife Apuleia Faustina, aged [..] years. Titus Aurelius Faustinianus and Aurelia Respecta, children and heirs, had this made in accordance with the will.
EDCS-29000438.summary: This funerary inscription records the erection of a monument by children, Titus Aurelius Faustinianus and Aurelia Respecta, for their deceased father (aged 60) and mother Apuleia Faustina, in accordance with a will.

EDCS-29000439.summary: This is a fragment mentioning a person named Maximus, son of Voltionis, likely part of a funerary or honorary inscription.

EDCS-29000440.text_edition: \n a Luci filia lina sibi et o filio I AN \n
EDCS-29000440.translation: ... daughter of Lucius, Lina for herself and for [her] son...
EDCS-29000440.summary: This fragmentary inscription mentions a woman named Lina, daughter of Lucius, dedicating something for herself and her son, the context is lost.

EDCS-29000441.summary: This inscription fragment names Quintio, a freedman, with no further context about the nature of the monument.

EDCS-29000445.summary: This inscription fragment likely belongs to a tombstone, recording the name Proculus and his age at death as thirty-one years.

EDCS-29000446.text_edition: \n Atebla Luci Attieni Rufi |(centurionis) libertus annorum XLV hic situs est Scarbantilla Luci Attieni serva annorum VI hic sita est filii et filiae posuerunt\n
EDCS-29000446.translation: Here lies Atebla, freedman of Lucius Attienus Rufus, aged 45. Here lies Scarbantilla, slave of Lucius Attienus, aged 6. Their children set this up.
EDCS-29000446.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a freedman and a slave, set up by their children, likely from the early imperial period.

EDCS-29000447.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Vallauno Dagorigis <f=E>(ilio) annorum IX et Secundinae Vae <f=E>(iliae) vivae I \n
EDCS-29000447.translation: To the departed spirits. Vallaunus, son of Dagorigis, aged 9, and Secundina, daughter of Vae, alive. I...
EDCS-29000447.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to two children, Vallaunus and Secundina, likely from the late Roman Republic or early Imperial period.

EDCS-29000451.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Flavius Fortinus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-29000451.translation: Flavius Fortinus gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Jupiter Best and Greatest.
EDCS-29000451.summary: This is a votive inscription recording the fulfillment of a vow to Jupiter, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-29000455.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Catulus cum Catulino filio votum solvit Idibus Maiis Tusco et Basso consulibus\n
EDCS-29000455.translation: Catulus with his son Catulinus gladly fulfilled his vow to Jupiter Best and Greatest on the Ides of May, when Tuscus and Bassus were consuls.
EDCS-29000455.summary: This is a votive inscription recording the fulfillment of a vow to Jupiter by Catulus and his son, dated to 117 AD.

EDCS-29000456.text_edition: \n Herculi Augusto sacrum Marcus Aurelius Cassius beneficiarius consularis \n
EDCS-29000456.translation: Sacred to Hercules Augustus. Marcus Aurelius Cassius, a *beneficiarius consularis*.
EDCS-29000456.summary: This is a sacred dedication to Hercules Augustus by Marcus Aurelius Cassius, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-29000457.text_edition: \n Nymphis salutaribus Augustis sacrum Iulius Maximus decurio municipii PF \n
EDCS-29000457.translation: Sacred to the August Nymphs who bring health. Iulius Maximus, decurion of the municipium of Forum Popili.
EDCS-29000457.summary: This is a sacred dedication to the Nymphs by Iulius Maximus, a decurion, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-29000458.text_edition: \n Nymphas sic salutares sic Marcus Rutilius Lupus tribunus militum legionis XXII quaestor tribunus plebis legatus Augusti legionis XIII geminae\n
EDCS-29000458.translation: To the Nymphs who bring health. Thus Marcus Rutilius Lupus, military tribune of the 22nd legion, quaestor, tribune of the plebs, imperial legate of the 13th Gemina legion.
EDCS-29000458.summary: This is a dedication to the Nymphs by Marcus Rutilius Lupus, recording his significant career progression, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-29000460.text_edition: \n Lucius Antonius Luci Pollia Modestus Industria veteranus legionis XIII geminae \n
EDCS-29000460.translation: Lucius Antonius Modestus, called Industria, veteran of the 13th Gemina legion, son of Lucius Antonius Pollio.
EDCS-29000460.summary: This is an epitaph for a veteran of the 13th Legion Gemina, Lucius Antonius Modestus, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-29000461.text_edition: \n Caius Cassius Cai filius Celer Aniensis Cremona veteranus legionis VIII Augustae annorum XL hic situs est heres et Tuce liberta posuerunt\n
EDCS-29000461.translation: Here lies Gaius Cassius Celer, son of Gaius, of the Voltinian tribe, from Cremona, veteran of the 8th Augusta legion, aged 40. His heir and Tuce, a freedwoman, set this up.
EDCS-29000461.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a veteran of the 8th Legion Augusta, Gaius Cassius Celer, set up by his heir and a freedwoman, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-29000462.text_edition: \n Caius Servilius Cai filius Aniensis Cremona miles legionis VIII Augustae eques nnorum XLIII stipendiorum XXI hic situs est heredes posierunt sic\n
EDCS-29000462.translation: Here lies Gaius Servilius, son of Gaius, of the Voltinian tribe, from Cremona, a soldier of the 8th Augusta legion, an *eques*, aged 43, with 21 years of service. His heirs set this up. Thus.
EDCS-29000462.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a soldier of the 8th Legion Augusta, Gaius Servilius, set up by his heirs, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-29000463.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Verrio Carino militi immuni legionis I adiutricis Aelius Communis miles legionis eiusdem heres eius et Claudius Onesimus amico\n
EDCS-29000463.translation: To the Divine Manes. For Verrius Carinus, an immune soldier of the I Adiutrix legion. Aelius Communis, a soldier of the same legion, his heir, and Claudius Onesimus, his friend [dedicated this].
EDCS-29000463.summary: A tombstone for Verrius Carinus, an immune soldier of the I Adiutrix legion, dedicated by his heir and a friend, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-29000464.text_edition: \n Deuso Agisi filius vivus fecit sibi et Adbugiounae Atnamati sic filiae coniugi suae Primio filius eorum nnorum XXXIII hic situs est\n
EDCS-29000464.translation: Deusos, son of Agis, made this while alive for himself and for his wife Adbugiouna, daughter of Atnamatus. Their son Primius, aged 33, is buried here.
EDCS-29000464.summary: A funerary monument erected by Deusos for himself and his wife, recording the burial of their son Primius, dated to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-29000465.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Herennia Ingenua Tito Iulio Marino coniugi karissimo et Iuliae Titianae filiae piissimae viva fecit et sibi et Iulio Bellino filio vivo\n
EDCS-29000465.translation: To the Divine Manes. For Herennia Ingenua. She made this while alive for her dearest husband Titus Iulius Marinus and her most devoted daughter Iulia Tiana, and for herself and her living son Iulius Bellinus.
EDCS-29000465.summary: A funerary monument dedicated by Herennia Ingenua for herself, her husband, daughter, and son, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-29000466.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucio Egnatuleio Luci filio Florentino annorum XXX pater faciendum curavit hic siti sunt\n
EDCS-29000466.translation: To the Divine Manes. For Lucius Egnatuleius Florentinus, son of Lucius, aged 30. His father had this made. They are buried here.
EDCS-29000466.summary: A tombstone for Lucius Egnatuleius Florentinus, aged 30, erected by his father, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-29000467.text_edition: \n Aconiae Cai filiae Salviae Caius Iulius Paternus uxori rarissimae \n
EDCS-29000467.translation: To Aconia Salvia, daughter of Caius. For her most rare wife Caius Iulius Paternus.
EDCS-29000467.summary: An epitaph possibly for a wife, Aconia Salvia, dedicated by Caius Iulius Paternus, her husband, from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-29000468.text_edition: \n Caius Iulius Maximi libertus Adietumarus annorum C hic situs est testamento fieri iussit sibi et Sporillae uxori annorum L\n
EDCS-29000468.translation: Caius Iulius Adietumarus, freedman of Maximi, aged 100, is buried here. He ordered this to be made in his will for himself and for his wife Sporilla, aged 50.
EDCS-29000468.summary: A funerary monument for Caius Iulius Adietumarus, a freedman aged 100, and his wife Sporilla, commissioned by Adietumarus in his will, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-29000469.text_edition: \n GAV Dis Manibus Pet Paullian- Pet Veriano obito annorum V et Apulei- et Rufin obit annorum \n
EDCS-29000469.translation: To the Divine Manes. For Pet... Paullian... Pet... Verianus, who died at the age of 5, and Apuleius... and Rufinus, who died at the age of...
EDCS-29000469.summary: A fragmented funerary inscription dedicated to the Divine Manes, commemorating children named Verianus and possibly Rufinus, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-29000470.text_edition: \n Sextus Caesernius Procincialis et Caesernia Maxima vivi fecerunt sibi\n
EDCS-29000470.translation: Sextus Caesernius Procincialis and Caesernia Maxima made this for themselves while alive.
EDCS-29000470.summary: A funerary marker for Sextus Caesernius Procincialis and Caesernia Maxima, who commissioned it for themselves during their lifetime, from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-29000471.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caius Iulius TR vivus fecit sibi etVI VICIATA \n
EDCS-29000471.translation: To the Divine Manes. Caius Iulius [TR?] made this for himself while alive and for VI Viciata.
EDCS-29000471.summary: A funerary monument dedicated by Caius Iulius to himself and VI Viciata, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-29000474.text_edition: \n EC uxori posuit carissimae suae Maximillae nomina tu<a=E> superos laudesque manebunt adque sic utinam superstes amico reciperes tua merita magna coniugio ereptam Firmam requiris sic ibidem \n
EDCS-29000474.translation: EC erected this for his most beloved wife Maximilla. Your names shall remain with the gods above and praises; and so, I wish you had survived. You seek your great merits in your husband, snatched from your marriage. You ask for her again, Firmam, right there.
EDCS-29000474.summary: A funerary epitaph for Maximilla, a beloved wife, praising her virtues and lamenting her loss, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-29000475.text_edition: \n Libero Patri Augusto sacrum Caius Volcenius Cai filius Lupercus et Valeria Quinti filia Saturnina donum dederunt votum solverunt\n
EDCS-29000475.translation: Sacred to Liber Pater Augustus: Gaius Volcenius Lupercus, son of Caius, and Valeria Saturnina, daughter of Quinti, gave this offering and fulfilled their vow.
EDCS-29000475.summary: This is a dedication to Liber Pater Augustus by Gaius Volcenius Lupercus and Valeria Saturnina, likely on a private altar or monument.

EDCS-29000476.text_edition: \n Marcus Mucius Hegetor medicus cohortis XXXII voluntariorum annorum XLVII hic situs est Mucia Corinthia sibi et patrono posuit\n
EDCS-29000476.translation: Marcus Mucius Hegetor, a doctor of the 32nd cohort of volunteers, aged 47, is buried here. Mucia Corinthia placed this for herself and her patron.
EDCS-29000476.summary: This is a tombstone recording the death of Marcus Mucius Hegetor, a doctor, and placed by Mucia Corinthia.

EDCS-29000477.text_edition: \n Idibus Augustis Herculi Gaius Ingenuius Gai filius Quirina Rufinianus decurio coloniae Septimiae Siscianorum Augustae quaestor rei publicae praefectus collegii centonariorum et Ingenuia Rufina cum suis donum dedit\n
EDCS-29000477.translation: On the Ides of August, Gaius Ingenuius Rufinianus, son of Gaius, of the Quirina tribe, a decurion of the colony of Septimia Siscianorum Augusta, quaestor of the republic, and prefect of the guild of cloth-makers, and Ingenuia Rufina, with their family, gave this offering to Hercules.
EDCS-29000477.summary: This is a dedication to Hercules made by Gaius Ingenuius Rufinianus and his family, detailing his numerous civic and guild offices.

EDCS-29000478.text_edition: \n Herculeni sic Augusto sacrum Lucius Spurius Restutianus\n
EDCS-29000478.translation: Sacred to Hercules Augustus: Lucius Spurius Restutianus.
EDCS-29000478.summary: This is a dedication to Hercules Augustus by Lucius Spurius Restutianus, likely on a private shrine or altar.

EDCS-29000480.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Marcus Ulpius Nigrinus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-29000480.translation: Marcus Ulpius Nigrinus gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus.
EDCS-29000480.summary: This is a votive dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Marcus Ulpius Nigrinus.

EDCS-29000481.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Conservatori Iunoni optimae omnipotenti Soli Genio loci Aurelius Antiochianus votum posuit\n
EDCS-29000481.translation: Aurelius Antiochianus set up his vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus the Preserver, to Juno the best and almighty, to the Sun, and to the Genius of the place.
EDCS-29000481.summary: This is a complex votive dedication by Aurelius Antiochianus to several deities, including Jupiter, Juno, Sol, and the local Genius.

EDCS-29000482.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo et Cereri Gaius Veratius Hispanus et Titus Flavius Campester - beneficiarii PR \n
EDCS-29000482.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus and Ceres: Gaius Veratius Hispanus and Titus Flavius Campester, beneficiaries of the Praetorian Guard.
EDCS-29000482.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus and Ceres by Gaius Veratius Hispanus and Titus Flavius Campester, who were beneficiaries of the Praetorian Guard.

EDCS-29000483.text_edition: \n Silvano Silvestri sacrum ur Otni servus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-29000483.translation: Sacred to Silvanus of the Woods: Ur Otni, a slave, gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-29000483.summary: This is a votive dedication to Silvanus, god of the woods, made by a slave named Ur Otni.

EDCS-29000484.text_edition: \n Silvanis Marcus Iulius Ingenuus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-29000484.translation: Marcus Iulius Ingenuus gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to the Silvani (woodland spirits).
EDCS-29000484.summary: This is a votive dedication to the Silvani by Marcus Iulius Ingenuus.

EDCS-29000485.translation: Sacred: Titus Attius the Younger ION.
EDCS-29000485.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, likely a dedication to a deity, mentioning Titus Attius the Younger.

EDCS-29000487.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Romania Naevia clarissima femina viva fecit sibi memoriam praecurrentibus Cletio Romuliano et Aurelio Calemero\n
EDCS-29000487.translation: To the Divine Manes. Romania Naevia, a most distinguished woman, made this memorial for herself during her lifetime, anticipating Cletius Romulianus and Aurelius Calemerus.
EDCS-29000487.summary: This funerary inscription records a woman, Romania Naevia, who dedicated a memorial for herself and preemptively for two others.

EDCS-29000488.text_edition: \n Tiberio Claudio Pontio |(centurioni) legionis XV Apollinaris annorum XXV heres \n
EDCS-29000488.translation: To Tiberius Pontius, centurion of the XV Apollinaris legion, aged 25. His heir [erected this].
EDCS-29000488.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Tiberius Pontius, a 25-year-old centurion, with his heir indicated as the dedicator.

EDCS-29000489.summary: This is a very brief funerary inscription simply naming Tiberius Claudius.

EDCS-29000490.text_edition: \n Vidaso et Thanae sacrum Quintus Iulius Ursus votum solvit\n
EDCS-29000490.translation: To Vidasus and Thana. Quintus Julius Ursus fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-29000490.summary: This votive inscription records Quintus Julius Ursus fulfilling a vow made to the deities Vidasus and Thana.

EDCS-29000491.text_edition: \n pro salute Imperatoris Marci Aureli Antonini Pii Felicis Augusti et Iuliae Augustae matris Augusti et castrorum Valerius Verus |(centurio) legionis XIIII geminae Antoninianae \n
EDCS-29000491.translation: For the safety of the Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, and Julia Augusta, mother of the Emperor and of the camp. Valerius Verus, centurion of the XIIII Gemina Antoniniana legion [dedicates this].
EDCS-29000491.summary: This inscription records a dedication made by Valerius Verus, a centurion, for the well-being of Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus and his mother Julia Augusta.

EDCS-29000494.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Senam sacrum Aurelius Vindicianus com sic suis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-29000494.translation: Sacred to Jupiter Optimus Maximus. Aurelius Vindicianus, with his family, gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-29000494.summary: This votive inscription records Aurelius Vindicianus, with his family, fulfilling a vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus.

EDCS-29000497.text_edition: \n Caius Marcius Cai filius Ceiler praeceptor gr annorum L hic situs Pompeia Quinti filia Respecta sibi et coniugi viva fecit\n
EDCS-29000497.translation: To Caius Marcius Ceiler, son of Caius, teacher, aged 50. Pompeia Respecta, daughter of Quintus, made this for herself and her husband during her lifetime.
EDCS-29000497.summary: This funerary inscription records Caius Marcius Ceiler, a teacher, and indicates that Pompeia Respecta commissioned the monument for herself and her husband.

EDCS-29000499.text_edition: \n Cornelius Per sibi et Corneliae ae uxori annorum C OF \n
EDCS-29000499.translation: For himself and Cornelia, his wife, aged 100 years. [Erected by...] Cornelius.
EDCS-29000499.summary: This funerary inscription records Cornelius and his wife Cornelia, with Cornelius indicated as the dedicator.

EDCS-29000501.text_edition: \n Firmidio Firmidiae liberto Verecundo annorum XXXV Marcius Ianuarius et Accepta filio pientissimo et sibi vivi fecerunt\n
EDCS-29000501.translation: Marcius Ianuarius and Accepta made this for their most dutiful son, Verecundus, freedman of Firmidius, aged 35 years, and for themselves during their lifetime.
EDCS-29000501.summary: This funerary inscription records the dedication made by Marcius Ianuarius and Accepta for their son Verecundus and for themselves.

EDCS-29000502.text_edition: \n Sextus Iulius Sexti libertus Hypatus annorum LX Iulia Avita uxor posuit\n
EDCS-29000502.translation: To Sextus Julius Hypatus, freedman of Sextus, aged 60 years. Iulia Avita, his wife, placed this.
EDCS-29000502.summary: This funerary inscription records Sextus Julius Hypatus, a freedman, and indicates that his wife Iulia Avita erected the monument.

EDCS-29000503.summary: This is a funerary inscription naming Gaius Laberius V., likely the deceased.

EDCS-29000505.text_edition: \n Silvano Augusto M Verissimus pro se ac suorum votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-29000505.translation: M. Verissimus gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Silvanus Augustus for himself and his family.
EDCS-29000505.summary: This is a dedication to the god Silvanus Augustus, with the vow fulfilled by M. Verissimus for himself and his family.

EDCS-29000506.text_edition: \n Publio Nertomario Quarto annorum LX et Meitae Optati filiae coniugi optimae vivae et Secundo filio annorum XXV Iustus et Nigrinus filii parentibus fecerunt\n
EDCS-29000506.translation: Iustus and Nigrinus, sons, erected this for their parents: Publius Nertomarius Quartus, aged 60, and his best wife Meita, daughter of Optatus, while she was alive, and their son Secundus, aged 25.
EDCS-29000506.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by two sons for their deceased parents and brother.

EDCS-29000510.summary: This is a fragmented dedication to the Great Mother, possibly naming Petronia.

EDCS-29000511.translation: L. G. M. fulfilled his vow to Victoria Augusta gladly and deservedly.
EDCS-29000511.summary: This is a votive inscription where L. G. M. fulfilled a vow to Victoria Augusta.

EDCS-29000512.translation: Sacred. Eumelus (?), Augustalis, by free decree of the decurions, and Dexter, on the occasion of its dedication.
EDCS-29000512.summary: This is a fragmented inscription recording a dedication made by Eumelus, an Augustalis, and Dexter, by decree of the town council.

EDCS-29000514.text_edition: \n Caius Clodius Cai filius Velina Secundus miles legionis XV Apollinaris Clodiae Cai filiae Tertiae sorori annorum numero XX hic sita est Caio Caestidio Luci filio Pollia Petronia Publi filia Dyssulla viva fecit\n
EDCS-29000514.translation: Gaius Clodius Secundus, son of Gaius, of the Velina tribe, a soldier of the XV Apollinaris legion, is buried here for his sister Clodia Tertia, daughter of Gaius, aged 20. Petronia, daughter of Publius, of the Pollia tribe, Dyssulla, erected this while alive for Gaius Caestidius, son of Lucius.
EDCS-29000514.summary: This is a complex funerary inscription for a soldier and his sister, erected by a woman named Dyssulla for another individual, Gaius Caestidius.

EDCS-29000515.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Of Iulius Fortunatus, one of the six high priests, and of the college of shipowners.
EDCS-29000515.summary: This is a fragmented funerary inscription dedicated to the deceased Iulius Fortunatus, who held priestly and collegial offices.

EDCS-29000516.translation: Flavus, son of L. Aemilius Berullus, aged 20, is buried here.
EDCS-29000516.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the burial of Flavus, son of L. Aemilius Berullus.

EDCS-29000521.translation: Planius Feucontis, son of Sextilius, ordered this monument to be made in his will for himself and for his wife Sextilia Tatsoria. The monument occupies 20 feet in every direction.
EDCS-29000521.summary: This is a funerary inscription detailing a burial plot established by Planius Feucontis in his will for himself and his wife, specifying dimensions.

EDCS-29000522.translation: To Pletoris, son of Potei Feuc, and to Lassonia Volta, daughter of Planus, wife of Pletoris, son of Po(r)teius Planius, aged 3 years; [erected by] the living Planus Po(r)teius Pletoris.
EDCS-29000522.summary: This inscription records a funerary monument for a family, mentioning several individuals and their parentage, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-29000523.translation: To Turoius Nepos, son of Votus, and to Feva, son of Turoius; erected by his wife, [in this] place for the monument, in all directions, [number of] feet [lost].
EDCS-29000523.summary: This inscription is a funerary dedication for Turoius Nepos and Feva, mentioning the wife of one of them and specifying the dimensions of the monument's plot, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-29000569.translation: To the god, erected by Titus Aelius.
EDCS-29000569.summary: This inscription is a dedication to a deity, funded by Titus Aelius, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-29000570.text_edition: \n ALS arunt E Capitone veterano et Tiberio us Aurelius Eutices sic us us\n
EDCS-29000570.translation: To Aruntius Capito, the veteran, and to Tiberius Aurelius Eutices.
EDCS-29000570.summary: This inscription records a dedication or memorial to two individuals, Aruntius Capito and Tiberius Aurelius Eutices, both veterans, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-29000576.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Marcus Aurelius Celerinus veteranus legionis II adiutricis piae fidelis Severianae beneficiarius tribuni una cum suis votum solvit libens merito Im[peratore Caesare Marco Aurelio S]evero Alexand[r]o Dione consulibus\n
