EDCS-30100493.summary: This inscription records the posthumous construction of a monument ordered by a high-ranking centurion and camp prefect of two legions, likely from the 2nd century AD.

EDCS-30100495.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Caius Vibius Trebonianus Gallus Pius Felix Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate consul II proconsul pater patriae et Imperator Caesar Caius Vibius Afinius Gallus Veldumnianus Volusianus \n
EDCS-30100495.translation: Emperor Caesar Caius Vibius Trebonianus Gallus Pius Felix Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power, consul for the second time, proconsul, father of the country, and Emperor Caesar Caius Vibius Afinius Gallus Veldumnianus Volusianus.
EDCS-30100495.summary: This is a titulature inscription for co-emperors Trebonianus Gallus and Volusianus, likely from the mid-3rd century AD.

EDCS-30100508.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Marcus Iulius Philippus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus pontifex maximus tribuniciae potestatis consul pater patriae et Marcus Iulius Philippus \n
EDCS-30100508.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Iulius Philippus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power, consul, father of the country, and Marcus Iulius Philippus.
EDCS-30100508.summary: This inscription records the titulature of Emperor Philip the Arab and his son, likely from the mid-3rd century AD.

EDCS-30100510.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Pius Felix Augustus pontifex maximus tribuniciae potestatis VIIII consul III pater patriae restituit a Brigetione milia passuum II\n
EDCS-30100510.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Pius Felix Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the ninth time, consul for the third time, father of the country, restored this, two miles from Brigetio.
EDCS-30100510.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a road by Emperor Severus Alexander, likely from the first half of the 3rd century AD.

EDCS-30100518.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Pius Felix Augustus pontifex maximus tribuniciae potestatis VIIII consul III pater patriae restituit milia passuum XVI\n
EDCS-30100518.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Pius Felix Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the ninth time, consul for the third time, father of the country, restored this, sixteen miles.
EDCS-30100518.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a road by Emperor Severus Alexander, likely from the first half of the 3rd century AD.

EDCS-30100526.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Caius Iulius Vero! Maximinus Pius Felix Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate III consul proconsul pater patriae et Caius Iulius Verus Maximus nobilissimus Caesar pontes et stratas vetustate conlapsas restituerunt a Bregetione! milia passuum V\n
EDCS-30100526.translation: Emperor Caesar Caius Iulius Vero! Maximinus Pius Felix Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the third time, consul, proconsul, father of the country, and Caius Iulius Verus Maximus, most noble Caesar, restored the bridges and roads collapsed by age, five miles from Bregetio!
EDCS-30100526.summary: This inscription records the restoration of bridges and roads by Emperors Maximinus Thrax and his son, likely from the early 3rd century AD.

EDCS-30100571.text_edition: \n Reparatum cancancellum stissemi tribunus Valentinus praefectus Sctius oram ntius Mestr hoc est votum Proiectus actuarius Maximi\n
EDCS-30100571.translation: Valentinus the tribune, prefect, repaired the cancelled cancellum. Proiectus, actuarius of Maximi, this is the vow from the shore.
EDCS-30100571.summary: This inscription records a repair or reconstruction by a tribune and prefect, likely a military dedication, dated to the 3rd century AD.

EDCS-30100573.text_edition: \n Aelius Castelianus tesserarius donum dedit Tertullo consule\n
EDCS-30100573.translation: Aelius Castelianus, the tesserarius, gave this gift in the consulship of Tertullus.
EDCS-30100573.summary: This inscription records a gift given by Aelius Castelianus, a military official, during the consulship of Tertullus, likely from the 3rd century AD.

EDCS-30100574.text_edition: \n Vital IIPP cornicularii proconsulis provinciae Achaiae um proconsulari \n
EDCS-30100574.translation: Vitalis, imperial aide (cornicularius) to the proconsul of the province of Achaia, and [---] from the proconsular service.
EDCS-30100574.summary: This inscription likely records individuals serving in the proconsular administration of Achaia, possibly around the late 2nd or early 3rd century AD.

EDCS-30100576.summary: This is a brief inscription identifying Aurelius as a senior centurion, likely from the Roman army.

EDCS-30100577.text_edition: \n Lucius Cliternius Luci Lib veterinarius legionis annorum L hic situs est Cliternia Ma patri suo posuit arbitratu Flaviae Secundae coniugis eius et Cliterni Pacati liberti eius\n
EDCS-30100577.translation: Lucius Cliternius, son of Lucius, a veterinarian of the legion, aged 50, is buried here. Cliternia Maxima placed this for her father, by the authority of her husband Flavia Secunda and her freedman Cliternius Pacatus.
EDCS-30100577.summary: This is a tombstone for Lucius Cliternius, a legionary veterinarian who died at age 50, erected by his daughter and son-in-law.

EDCS-30100578.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Titus Flavius Lucilius miles legionis XIIII geminae stipendiorum X annorum XXX hic situs est Flavius Lucius miles legionis eiusdem fratri bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-30100578.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Titus Flavius Lucilius, a soldier of the 14th Gemina legion, with 10 years of service and aged 30, is buried here. Flavius Lucius, a soldier of the same legion, placed this for his deserving brother.
EDCS-30100578.summary: This is a tombstone for Titus Flavius Lucilius, a soldier of the 14th Gemina legion, erected by his brother.

EDCS-30100579.text_edition: \n Caio Iulio Valenti veterano legionis I Adiutricis annorum LVII et Iuliae Iuliae coniugi eius annorum XXXVI et Iuliae Valentinae filiae eorum annorum XXX et Iulio Cassiano annorum V Caius Iulius Praetorinus parentibus et sorori carissim fecit\n
EDCS-30100579.translation: For Gaius Julius Valens, a veteran of the I Adiutrix legion, aged 57, and his wife Julia Julia, aged 36, and their daughter Julia Valentina, aged 30, and Iulius Cassianus, aged 5. Gaius Julius Praetorinus made this for his dearest parents and sister.
EDCS-30100579.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by Gaius Julius Praetorinus for his parents, sister, and a young child, all of whom were associated with the I Adiutrix legion.

EDCS-30100580.text_edition: \n L et Lartidio V nepoti Lucius Lartidius Vultilianus duplarius legionis supra scriptae fratri nepotibus ex voluntate testamenenti faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-30100580.translation: For L. and Lartidius V., grandson. Lucius Lartidius Vultilianus, duplarius of the aforementioned legion, arranged for this to be made for his brother's grandsons, according to his will.
EDCS-30100580.summary: This inscription records Lucius Lartidius Vultilianus arranging for a monument for his brother's grandsons, as per his will.

EDCS-30100581.text_edition: \n Liccai filius Colapianus miles cohortis I Ulpiae Pannoniorum annorum VL stipendiorum hic situs est Precio TAI\n
EDCS-30100581.translation: Colapianus, son of Liccaius, a soldier of the 1st Cohort Ulpia Pannoniorum, aged 45, with years of service [---], is buried here. Precio TAI [---].
EDCS-30100581.summary: This is a tombstone for Colapianus, a soldier of the 1st Cohort Ulpia Pannoniorum, whose age and years of service are partially preserved.

EDCS-30100582.text_edition: \n legionis XV Apollinaris annorum XL stipendiorum XIIX hic situs est Lucius Mercusenus fratri posuit\n
EDCS-30100582.translation: Of the 15th Apollinaris legion, aged 40, with 18 years of service, is buried here. Lucius Mercusenus placed this for his brother.
EDCS-30100582.summary: This is a tombstone for an unnamed soldier of the 15th Apollinaris legion, erected by his brother Lucius Mercusenus.

EDCS-30100584.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Claudiae Ursae nnorum XXIIII Septimius Veranus optio legionis XIIII Geminae coniugi carissimae et Septimio Vero filio nnorum X dierum VII faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-30100584.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Claudia Ursa, aged 24. Septimius Veranus, optatio of the 14th Gemina legion, arranged for this to be made for his dearest wife and for Septimius Verus, aged 10 years and 7 days.
EDCS-30100584.summary: This is a funerary monument for Claudia Ursa, wife of Septimius Veranus (an optio in the 14th Gemina legion), and their young son Septimius Verus.

EDCS-30100585.text_edition: \n Marcus Sulpicius Vepitta mill(es) legionis XIIII geminae Martiae victricis nnorum XXX hic situs heres Marcus Ulpius Augustinus faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-30100585.translation: Marcus Sulpicius Vepitta, soldier of the 14th Gemina Martia Victrix legion, aged 30, is buried here. The heir Marcus Ulpius Augustinus arranged for this to be made.
EDCS-30100585.summary: This is a tombstone for Marcus Sulpicius Vepitta, a soldier of the 14th Gemina legion, erected by his heir.

EDCS-30100586.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marco Tallanio Montano signiferi e numero legionis XIIII geminae stipendiorum XX hic situs est Nigrinus legionis eiusdem \n
EDCS-30100586.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Marcus Tallanius Montanus, standard-bearer from the ranks of the 14th Gemina legion, with twenty years of service, lies here. Nigrinus of the same legion [commissioned this].
EDCS-30100586.summary: A tombstone recording the burial of a standard-bearer of the 14th Gemina legion, erected by a comrade.

EDCS-30100587.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Annia Iusta annorum LXXX hic sita est Terentius Lupus matri et Lucius Terentius Ursulus veteranus? legionis XIIII geminae optimae fecerunt\n
EDCS-30100587.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Annia Iusta, aged 80, lies here. Terentius Lupus, to his mother, and Lucius Terentius Ursulus, a veteran of the 14th Gemina legion, made this.
EDCS-30100587.summary: A tombstone for Annia Iusta, erected by her son and grandson, one of whom was a veteran of the 14th Gemina legion.

EDCS-30100588.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iuliae Eliae annorum VIIII Titus Iulius? Titianus legionis XIIII geminae et ia A M \n
EDCS-30100588.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Julia Elia, aged 9. Titus Iulius (?) Titianus of the 14th Gemina legion and his mother [commissioned this].
EDCS-30100588.summary: A tombstone for a young girl, Julia Elia, erected by her father, a soldier in the 14th Gemina legion, and his mother.

EDCS-30100589.text_edition: \n Caius Valerius Cai filius Galeria Proculus Calagurri eques legionis XI Claudiae Fidelis centuria Vindicis annorum XXX stipendiorum IX hic situs est testamento fieri iussit heres faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-30100589.translation: Gaius Valerius Proculus, son of Gaius, of the Galeria tribe, from Calagurri, a cavalryman in the 11th Claudia Fidelis legion, in the 5th century of the centurion Vindex, aged thirty, with nine years of service, lies here. He ordered this to be made by his will; his heir had it carried out.
EDCS-30100589.summary: A tombstone for Gaius Valerius Proculus, a cavalryman in the 11th Claudia Fidelis legion, who specified its erection in his will.

EDCS-30100590.text_edition: \n legionis I a<d>iutricis beneficiarius consularis vixit annos XXXX stupendiorum sic XXII Marcus Ulpius Artlaus frater et heres pientiss \n
EDCS-30100590.translation: A staff officer of the 1st Adjututrix legion, beneficiary of a consul, lived 40 years, with 22 years of service. Marcus Ulpius Artlaus, his brother and heir, most devotedly [commissioned this].
EDCS-30100590.summary: A tombstone for a staff officer of the 1st Adjututrix legion, erected by his brother and heir.

EDCS-30100591.text_edition: \n Marcus Dec et VCI Clemens LXII stipendiorum \n
EDCS-30100591.translation: Marcus Decimus Clemens, aged 62, with sixty years of service.
EDCS-30100591.summary: A tombstone recording the age and service years of Marcus Decimus Clemens, likely a soldier.

EDCS-30100609.text_edition: \n S No natione Savariensis annorum LXV hic situs est Egnatia Iuven patr \n
EDCS-30100609.translation: S., of the Savariensian tribe, aged 65, lies here. Egnatia Iuven [commissioned this] for her father.
EDCS-30100609.summary: A tombstone recording the burial of a woman of the Savariensian tribe, erected by her daughter.

EDCS-30100611.text_edition: \n Caius Aemilius Cai filius Fabia Patavio lixa annorum XXV hic situs est ex testamento fieri iussit\n
EDCS-30100611.translation: Gaius Aemilius Sabinus, son of Gaius, of the Fabia tribe, from Patavium, a camp follower, aged 25, lies here. He ordered this to be made by his will.
EDCS-30100611.summary: A tombstone for Gaius Aemilius Sabinus, a camp follower, who specified its erection in his will.

EDCS-30100612.text_edition: \n Caius Albius Cai filius Sabinus RI \n
EDCS-30100612.translation: Gaius Albius Sabinus, son of Gaius, of the Quirina tribe [commissioned this].
EDCS-30100612.summary: A fragmentary tombstone, possibly recording the erection of a monument by Gaius Albius Sabinus.

EDCS-30100614.text_edition: \n QuinIII hic siti sunt ni fili eius\n
EDCS-30100614.translation: Three [people] lie here; [one] of his sons.
EDCS-30100614.summary: A very fragmentary tombstone, likely recording the burial of three individuals and mentioning a son.

EDCS-30100615.summary: This is a dedication by Barb, likely for a tomb or monument, with an unknown co-dedicant.

EDCS-30100617.text_edition: \n Fabius Quinti libertus Chrysanthus nnorum XVI hic situs est patronus posuit\n
EDCS-30100617.translation: Fabius Chrysanthus, freedman of Quintus, aged 16, lies here; his patron erected [this].
EDCS-30100617.summary: This is a tombstone for a freedman named Chrysanthus, erected by his patron, likely dating to the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-30100620.translation: Graecus made [this] for the departed spirits.
EDCS-30100620.summary: This is a dedication to the 'Dis Manibus' (departed spirits), made by someone named Graecus, likely for a tomb.

EDCS-30100625.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iulia Tertia Iuli filia annorum XXV hic sita est Lucius Vibius Luci filius Sisi coniugi pientissimae fecit et Iuliae Tertullinae sorori et Lucretiae Graecae matri et Tito Babonio Titi filio Severo posuit\n
EDCS-30100625.translation: To the departed spirits: Julia Tertia, daughter of Lucius, aged 25, lies here. Lucius Vibius Lucius son erected [this] for his most devoted wife Julia Tertullina, and also for Julia Tertullina her sister, and Lucretia Graeca his mother, and Titus Babonius Severus son of Titus.
EDCS-30100625.summary: This is a tombstone for Julia Tertia, erected by her husband Lucius Vibius, and also commemorates her sister, mother, and another relative, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30100629.text_edition: \n Petronia Luci liberta Melphomene sic nnorum XXX hic sita est\n
EDCS-30100629.translation: Petronia Melphomene, freedwoman of Lucius, lies here, aged 30.
EDCS-30100629.summary: This is a tombstone for Petronia Melphomene, a freedwoman, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30100630.text_edition: \n ] Priv Iovinus II Maximianus rum et Aurelia Viatori coniux eius et Lunilus et Sabinus et fili eorum parentibus (merentibus I faciendum curaberunt sic\n
EDCS-30100630.translation: Privius Iovinus, Maximianus, and Aurelia Viatoris his wife, and Lunilus and Sabinus and their sons, took care to have [this] made for their deserving parents.
EDCS-30100630.summary: This inscription records a funerary monument commissioned by sons for their parents, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30100631.summary: This is a brief epitaph recording the lifespan of Rufus.

EDCS-30100632.text_edition: \n Caius Varius Servatus annorum XXXVI hic situs est Varia Peregrina fecit sua pecunia\n
EDCS-30100632.translation: Caius Varius Servatus, aged 36, lies here. Varia Peregrina made [this] with her own money.
EDCS-30100632.summary: This is a tombstone for Caius Varius Servatus, erected by Varia Peregrina, likely his wife or close relative, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30100633.text_edition: \n Vibia Cai liberta Citheris annorum XI hic sita est virgo hic sepulta fida puella iacet ante quidem tempus fata rapuerunt mala scripsi ego per lacrimas miserabilis morte puellae pater posuit \n
EDCS-30100633.translation: Vibia Citheris, freedwoman of Caius, aged 11, lies here, a virgin buried here, a faithful girl. Alas, cruel fate snatched her away before her time. I have written these sad words through tears. Her grieving father erected [this].
EDCS-30100633.summary: This is a tombstone for Vibia Citheris, a young freedwoman, erected by her father who laments her premature death, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30100660.text_edition: \n Fur es VIIII XVII Pientianus Artia Proclia C XXVI lunis Maurentius artifex lunis Ursus viator XIII ABCDEFGHILMNOPQR XVII PIEIIIA T N\n
EDCS-30100660.translation: You are a thief. Pientianus (aged) 9, Artia Proclia (aged) 26 years and 20 months, Maurentius the craftsman (aged) X years, Ursus the traveler (aged) 13 years. ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQR (aged) 17 years. T. N.
EDCS-30100660.summary: This inscription appears to be a funerary or memorial text listing individuals with their ages and some with occupations, possibly a playful or sarcastic epitaph, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30100706.text_edition: \n Petrae Genetrici Felix Prudentis Antoni Rufi publici portorii vilici vicarius ex viso\n
EDCS-30100706.translation: To Petraea Genitrix, Felix, deputy for Antonius Rufus, publican of tolls, from the estate.
EDCS-30100706.summary: This is a dedication to the goddess Petraea Genitrix by Felix, a deputy customs official, likely dating from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-30100709.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Marcus Appuleius Crispinus and his Appulei.
EDCS-30100709.summary: A dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus made by Marcus Appuleius Crispinus and other members of the Appuleian family, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-30100710.translation: To the Great Mother of the Gods, Clodia Clementilla, daughter of Lucius.
EDCS-30100710.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the Great Mother of the Gods (Cybele) by Clodia Clementilla, daughter of Lucius, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-30100711.translation: To the spirits of the departed: to Lucius Aelius Nigrinus, soldier of the fleet of Pannonia, [---].
EDCS-30100711.summary: A tombstone inscription for Lucius Aelius Nigrinus, a soldier in the Pannonian fleet, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-30100712.translation: To the spirits of the departed: [---] soldier of the 13th Legion Gemina, [---] Celerina(?), to Aelianus, [---] aged 2 years, most unfortunate parents [placed this].
EDCS-30100712.summary: A tombstone inscription for an unknown soldier of the 13th Legion Gemina and an infant named Aelianus, mentioning his mother Celerina, from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-30100713.translation: Quintus Polius Vitalis, alive, made [this] for himself and [---].
EDCS-30100713.summary: A funerary inscription recording that Quintus Polius Vitalis erected this monument for himself and others while still alive, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-30100714.translation: [---] Vibunnia Matrona, in the land, 50.5 feet, in front, 28 feet.
EDCS-30100714.summary: This inscription appears to record the dimensions of a plot of land belonging to Vibunnia Matrona, likely a boundary marker from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-30100715.translation: Marcus Volusius Potens, alive, made [this] for himself and for Glesonia Nigilla, his excellent wife.
EDCS-30100715.summary: A funerary inscription by Marcus Volusius Potens for himself and his wife Glesonia Nigilla, erected by him while alive, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-30100716.translation: [---] who died before reaching adulthood, snatched away [---], survived by them in life [---]. Marcellinus(?), lived 3 years. Johannes, lived 5 years, 8 months, 20 days. Marinus the father and Ursa the mother, devoted parents, made this memorial.
EDCS-30100716.summary: A tombstone commemorating three children, Marcellinus (?), Johannes, and two others, erected by their parents Marinus and Ursa, dating from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-30100717.translation: To the spirits of the departed: Iustus made [this] for his dearest son, aged 20 years and 1 month, [and] for himself and his family.
EDCS-30100717.summary: A tombstone inscription by Iustus for his son, who died at the age of 20 years and 1 month, and for himself and his family, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-30100718.translation: Sacred to Silvanus, by Veroni(?) Panun.
EDCS-30100718.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the god Silvanus, likely by a person or persons named Veroni(?) Panun, and dates to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30100719.text_edition: \n Invicto Mithrae et Transitu Dei Theodorus publici portorii scrutator stationis Poetovionensis ex visu\n
EDCS-30100719.translation: To the Invincible Mithras and the Transitus Dei (Passage of God), Theodorus, an inspector of the public customs-house of the station at Poetovio, [dedicated this] in a vision.
EDCS-30100719.summary: This is a dedication to the god Mithras and the Transitus Dei by Theodorus, a customs inspector at Poetovio, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30100720.text_edition: \n Transitui Caius Caecina Calpurnius templum redemit et resstituit sic\n
EDCS-30100720.translation: Caius Caecina Calpurnius redeemed and restored the temple to the Transitus Dei.
EDCS-30100720.summary: This inscription records the restoration and redemption of a temple dedicated to the Transitus Dei by Caius Caecina Calpurnius, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30100721.text_edition: \n Naturae dei Prudens Primi Antoni Rufi publici portorii vilici vicarius\n
EDCS-30100721.translation: Prudens, vicarius of the customs-house supervisor of Primus Antonius Rufus, dedicated [this] to the divine Nature.
EDCS-30100721.summary: This is a dedication to the divine Nature by Prudens, an assistant to a public customs official named Primus Antonius Rufus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30100723.text_edition: \n Cauti sacrum Venulus Aponi Ingenui votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-30100723.translation: Venulus, son of Aponius Ingenuus, gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Cautus.
EDCS-30100723.summary: This inscription records the fulfillment of a vow to the deity Cautus by Venulus, son of Aponius Ingenuus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30100724.text_edition: \n Cautopati sacrum Crescens Gabini Antoni servus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-30100724.translation: Crescens, slave of Gabinius Antonius, gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Cautopates.
EDCS-30100724.summary: This inscription records the fulfillment of a vow to the deity Cautopates by Crescens, a slave of Gabinius Antonius, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30100725.text_edition: \n Primitivos sic Cai Antoni Rufi procuratoris Augusti publici portorii |contrascriptor in memoriam Hyacinthi\n
EDCS-30100725.translation: Primitivos, scribe of Caius Antonius Rufus, procurator of the public customs-house of the emperor, [erected this] in memory of Hyacinthus.
EDCS-30100725.summary: This inscription is a memorial erected by Primitivos, a scribe under the imperial customs official Caius Antonius Rufus, in memory of Hyacinthus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30100726.text_edition: \n Primitivos sic Cai Antoni Rufi procuratoris Augusti publici portorii |contrascriptor in memoriam Hyacinthi\n
EDCS-30100726.translation: Primitivos, scribe of Caius Antonius Rufus, procurator of the public customs-house of the emperor, [erected this] in memory of Hyacinthus.
EDCS-30100726.summary: This inscription is a memorial erected by Primitivos, a scribe under the imperial customs official Caius Antonius Rufus, in memory of Hyacinthus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30100728.text_edition: \n Caius Cornelius Cai Pollia Proculus Regio miles legionis XIII geminae annorum XXX stipendiorum XII benefici<a>rius tribuni hic situs est testamento fieri iussit\n
EDCS-30100728.translation: Caius Cornelius Proculus, a soldier of the Legio XIII Gemina, aged 30, with 12 years of service, an adjutant to the tribune, is buried here; he ordered this to be made in his will.
EDCS-30100728.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Caius Cornelius Proculus, a soldier of the Legio XIII Gemina who served as a tribune's adjutant, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30100732.text_edition: \n SelEne ann--- XXXX Lucius Attius O veteranus legionis coniugi I \n
EDCS-30100732.translation: Selene, [aged] 40. Lucius Attius O..., a veteran of the legion, [erected this] for his wife.
EDCS-30100732.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Selene, erected by her husband Lucius Attius O..., a legionary veteran, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30100733.text_edition: \n Marti Deo Veracius Augustinus miles legionis decimae geminae veteranus misso sic missione honesta\n
EDCS-30100733.translation: To the god Mars, Veracius Augustinus, a soldier of the Tenth Gemina legion, a veteran honorably discharged, [dedicated this].
EDCS-30100733.summary: A dedication to Mars by a veteran soldier of the Tenth Gemina legion, likely a tombstone or dedication marker, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30100734.translation: Candidus made this sacred to Silvanus Augustus.
EDCS-30100734.summary: A dedication to Silvanus Augustus by Candidus, likely a votive offering or a marker for a shrine, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30100735.translation: Candidus made this sacred to Silvanus.
EDCS-30100735.summary: A dedication to Silvanus by Candidus, likely a votive offering or a marker for a shrine, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30100736.text_edition: \n Silvano sac<r>um Ulpius PCA miles legionis X- votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-30100736.translation: Ulpius PCA, a soldier of the 10th legion, fulfilled his vow to Silvanus, gladly and deservedly.
EDCS-30100736.summary: A votive inscription to Silvanus by Ulpius PCA, a soldier of the 10th legion, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30100737.text_edition: \n Silvanabus sacrum Lucius Aemilius Fronto votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-30100737.translation: Lucius Aemilius Fronto fulfilled his vow to the Silvan Mothers, gladly and deservedly.
EDCS-30100737.summary: A votive inscription to the Silvan Mothers by Lucius Aemilius Fronto, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30100740.text_edition: \n Coelius Tullio missicius legionis XIIII geminae annorum XXXV Lucius Coelius Pupio annorum XL Valerius Maxim S \n
EDCS-30100740.translation: Coelius Tullio, a discharged soldier of the 14th Gemina legion, aged 35, [and] Lucius Coelius Pupio, aged 40, [and] Valerius Maxim S [are buried here].
EDCS-30100740.summary: An epitaph recording the burial of three men, including a discharged soldier of the 14th Gemina legion, with their ages, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30100741.text_edition: \n Hilarus Luci et Titi Semproniorum natione Dalmata annorum LXXXX et Sassa coniunx et conliberta natione Daca annorum LXX hic siti sunt Flaccus filius parentibus posuit\n
EDCS-30100741.translation: Hilarus, from Dalmatia, aged 90, and Sassa, his wife and freedwoman, from Dacia, aged 70, are buried here; Flaccus, their son, placed this for his parents.
EDCS-30100741.summary: An epitaph for Hilarus and Sassa, a married couple of different origins and ages, placed by their son Flaccus, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30100742.text_edition: \n ICI hic sit-- sunt Lucio Arellio Hieraci filio defuncto annorum XIIII\n
EDCS-30100742.translation: ICI here lie -- Lucio Arellio Hieraci, son, deceased, aged 14.
EDCS-30100742.summary: An incomplete epitaph for Lucio Arellio Hieraci, son, who died at the age of 14, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30100743.text_edition: \n Campestribus Lucius Volumnius Horatianus tribunus militum laticlavius legionis I adiutricis piae fidelis\n
EDCS-30100743.translation: Lucius Volumnius Horatianus, military tribune with senatorial rank, of the 1st Adjutrix pious and faithful legion, [dedicated this] to the Campestres.
EDCS-30100743.summary: A dedication to the Campestres by Lucius Volumnius Horatianus, a high-ranking officer (tribune with laticlavian status) of the 1st Adjutrix legion, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30100744.text_edition: \n Campestribus Marcus Ulpius Rufus eques legionis I adiutricis piae fidelis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-30100744.translation: Marcus Ulpius Rufus, an equestrian soldier of the 1st Adjutrix pious and faithful legion, fulfilled his vow, gladly and deservedly.
EDCS-30100744.summary: A votive inscription to the Campestres by Marcus Ulpius Rufus, an equestrian officer of the 1st Adjutrix legion, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30100745.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Augusto sacrum Iunoni Reginae Minervae Lucius Septimius Cupitus veteranus legionis I adiutricis piae fidelis Severianae votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-30100745.translation: Sacred to Jupiter Best and Greatest, August, to Juno Regina, and Minerva. Lucius Septimius Cupitus, a veteran of the First Adjutant Legion, pious, faithful, Severian, gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-30100745.summary: A veteran dedicates an offering to Jupiter, Juno, and Minerva, likely from the Severan period (late 2nd to early 3rd century CE).

EDCS-30100746.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo sacrum pro salute Auggustorum Caius Iulius Catullinus miles legionis XIIII geminae Martiae victricis conductor prati Furiani lustro Nerti Celerini primi pili votum solvit libens laetus merito Kalendis Octobribus Imperatore Antonino Augusto II et Geta Caesare consulibus\n
EDCS-30100746.translation: Sacred to Jupiter Best and Greatest for the safety of the Augusti. Gaius Iulius Catullinus, a soldier of the Fourteenth Gemina Martia Victrix Legion, contractor of the Furian meadow, during the census of Nertius Celerinus, primipilaris, gladly and joyfully and deservedly fulfilled his vow on the Kalends of October, when Emperor Antoninus Augustus II and Geta Caesar were consuls.
EDCS-30100746.summary: A soldier of the Fourteenth Legion dedicates an offering for the emperors' safety in 205 CE, specifying the location of a meadow and the consulship.

EDCS-30100747.text_edition: \n Libero Liberae Fortunae Mercurio lustro Ansi Proculi primi pili Ansius Archelaus ex voto posuit\n
EDCS-30100747.translation: To Liber, Libera, and Mercury. Ansius Archelaus, from the census of Ansius Proculus, primipilaris, set this up in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-30100747.summary: An ex-soldier dedicates an offering to Liber, Libera, and Mercury, possibly in the 2nd or early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-30100750.text_edition: \n Lucius Cassius Claudia Albanus Aequiculis miles legionis XV Apollinaris centuria Salvi Marcelli stipendiorum XII annorum XXXV Sextus Valerius Macer heres posuit\n
EDCS-30100750.translation: Lucius Cassius Claudia Albanus Aequiculis, a soldier of the Fifteenth Apollinaris Legion, in the century of Salvius Marcellus, with twelve years of service and aged thirty-five, Sextus Valerius Macer, his heir, set this up.
EDCS-30100750.summary: The heir of a deceased soldier of the Fifteenth Legion, Lucius Cassius Claudia Albanus Aequiculis, erects a tombstone, recording his service and age.

EDCS-30100751.text_edition: \n Caio Cassio Marino Velina Aquileia filio nnorum VIII et Cassiae Proculae Velina Aquileia sorori nnorum XVI Caius Cassius Aelianus |(centurio) legionis XV Apollinaris fecit\n
EDCS-30100751.translation: To Gaius Cassius Marinus Velina Aquileia, son aged 8, and to Cassia Procula Velina Aquileia, sister aged 16. Gaius Cassius Aelianus, centurion of the Fifteenth Apollinaris Legion, made this.
EDCS-30100751.summary: A centurion of the Fifteenth Legion erects a monument for his young son and daughter.

EDCS-30100753.text_edition: \n Cohortis I centuria Claudi Acris principis posterioris\n
EDCS-30100753.translation: Of the First Cohort, of the century of Claudius Acris, the principalis posterior.
EDCS-30100753.summary: This fragment likely records a unit designation and a soldier's rank within the Roman army, possibly from the 1st Cohort.

EDCS-30100754.text_edition: \n Aulus Eburius Celer miles legionis XV Apollinaris centuria Decci Maxsimi sic vixit nnos XXX stipendiorum VIIII hic situs est frater ei fecit\n
EDCS-30100754.translation: Aulus Eburius Celer, a soldier of the Fifteenth Apollinaris Legion, in the century of Deccius Maximus, lived for 30 years with 9 years of service. His brother erected this monument here for him.
EDCS-30100754.summary: A soldier of the Fifteenth Legion, Aulus Eburius Celer, is honored by his brother on his tombstone, noting his age and service years.

EDCS-30100755.text_edition: \n Caius Fabius Cai filius Teretina miles legionis XV Apollinaris nnrum XXXVII stipendiorum XVII hic situs est testamento fieri iussit Lucius Statius heres titulum merenti posuit\n
EDCS-30100755.translation: Caius Fabius, son of Caius, Teretina, a soldier of the Fifteenth Apollinaris Legion, aged 37 years with 17 years of service, was buried here. His heir, Lucius Statius, placed this inscription for him as he deserved.
EDCS-30100755.summary: A soldier of the Fifteenth Legion, Caius Fabius, is buried here, and his heir, Lucius Statius, erected his tombstone, stating his age and service.

EDCS-30100757.text_edition: \n Sextus Iulius Sexti Sergia Quintilus Antiochia miles legionis XV Apollinaris centuria Messi Secundi stipendiorum XXI nnrum XL hic situs est heres faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-30100757.translation: Sextus Iulius, son of Sextus, Sergia Quintilus, Antiochia, a soldier of the Fifteenth Apollinaris Legion, in the century of Messius Secundus, with 21 years of service and aged 40, is buried here. His heir took care to have this made.
EDCS-30100757.summary: An heir arranges for the burial and tombstone of Sextus Iulius, a soldier of the Fifteenth Legion, recording his service length and age.

EDCS-30100758.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Decimo Miscenio Verecundo veterano legionis XV Apollinaris\n
EDCS-30100758.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Decimus Miscenius Verecundus, a veteran of the Fifteenth Apollinaris Legion.
EDCS-30100758.summary: A dedication to the underworld deities for the deceased veteran Decimus Miscenius Verecundus of the Fifteenth Legion.

EDCS-30100759.text_edition: \n Marcus Praeconius Iucundus Sirmio tubicen legionis XV Apollinaris centuria Sibidieni Maximi Aponius Pudens fratri pro pietate annorum XL stipendiorum XX donis donatus hic situs est\n
EDCS-30100759.translation: Marcus Praeconius Iucundus, a trumpeter of the XV Apollinaris legion, from Sirmio, of the centuria of Sibidienus Maximus, 40 years old with 20 years of service, honored with gifts, lies here. Aponius Pudens made this for his brother out of devotion.
EDCS-30100759.summary: This funerary inscription records Marcus Praeconius Iucundus, a trumpeter in the XV Apollinaris legion, honored for his service and erected by his brother, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-30100760.text_edition: \n Titus Terentius Titi filius Menenia Fronto miles legionis XV Apollinaris imaginifer nnorum XXXII stipendiorum XI hic situs est cura acta per collegas eius\n
EDCS-30100760.translation: Titus Terentius Fronto, son of Titus, of the Menenia tribe, a standard-bearer of the XV Apollinaris legion, 32 years old with 11 years of service, lies here. His comrades saw to its erection.
EDCS-30100760.summary: This funerary inscription records Titus Terentius Fronto, a standard-bearer in the XV Apollinaris legion, who died after 11 years of service, and was commemorated by his fellow soldiers, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-30100762.text_edition: \n Caius Domitius Zmaragdus domo Antiochia decurio municipii Aeli Carnunti amphitheatrum impensa sua solo publico fecit\n
EDCS-30100762.translation: Caius Domitius Zmaragdus, from Antioch, a decurion of the municipality of Aelia Carnuntum, built the amphitheater at his own expense on public land.
EDCS-30100762.summary: This inscription records the construction of an amphitheater by Caius Domitius Zmaragdus, a local official of Aelia Carnuntum, at his own expense, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-30100763.text_edition: \n Caio Vallio Cai filio Sergia Scriboniano decurioni coloniae Septimiae Aureliae Antoninianae Karnunti equiti Romano adlecto in V decurias inter selectos flamini omnibus honoribus perfuncto Vallia Censorina patri piissimo\n
EDCS-30100763.translation: To Gaius Vallius, son of Gaius, of the Sergia tribe, Scribonianus, a decurion of the colony Septimia Aurelia Antoniniana of Carnuntum, a Roman knight appointed to the V decuriae among the select, who had filled the office of flamen and all other honors, Vallia Censorina (erected this) to her most dutiful father.
EDCS-30100763.summary: This is a funerary monument dedicated by Vallia Censorina to her father, Gaius Vallius, a prominent local official and Roman knight in Carnuntum, likely dating to the Antonine period.

EDCS-30100764.text_edition: \n Aulius Luci filius Tromentina miles legionis XV Apolinaris annorum XXXV stipendiorum XVI hic situs est Aulius frater posuit Fidelis Cai Auli annorum XIX hic situs est\n
EDCS-30100764.translation: Aulius, son of Lucius, of the Tromentina tribe, a soldier of the XV Apollinaris legion, aged 35, with 16 years of service, lies here. Aulius Fidelis, brother, erected this. Gaius, son of Aulus, aged 19, lies here.
EDCS-30100764.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the death of Aulius, a soldier of the XV Apollinaris legion, and Gaius, likely his younger brother, both buried in the same location and commemorated by Aulius Fidelis.

EDCS-30100765.summary: This is a tombstone inscription identifying the deceased as Bussurus, son of Attua, and stating his age, likely from a Roman province in a Celtic-speaking region.

EDCS-30100766.summary: This is a fragmented inscription identifying an individual named Suadru, son of Adnamus, possibly a funerary or dedicatory text.

EDCS-30100767.text_edition: \n Venisa Domionis filia nnorum IIII et Gaura Domionis filia annorum III hic sitae sunt Domio pater posuit\n
EDCS-30100767.translation: Venisa, daughter of Domio, aged 4 years, and Gaura, daughter of Domio, aged 3 years, lie here. Domio, their father, erected this.
EDCS-30100767.summary: This is a funerary inscription commemorating the early deaths of two young sisters, Venisa and Gaura, erected by their father Domio.

EDCS-30100768.text_edition: \n Aiiuca sic Combrissae filia viva sibi fecit et Ituna filiae annorum VI et servi Bitua annorum XXIII et Masarius VN\n
EDCS-30100768.translation: Aiiuca, daughter of Combrissa, while alive, made (this) for herself, and for her daughter Ituna, aged 6, and for the slave Bitua, aged 23, and for the slave Masarius.
EDCS-30100768.summary: This is a funerary monument intended for self-dedication and for the deceased members of a household, including Aiiuca, her daughter Ituna, and two slaves, Bitua and Masarius.

EDCS-30100769.text_edition: \n Comalus Verclovi filius annorum LIII hic situs est patri posi Aisogio sic annorum III hic situs est\n
EDCS-30100769.translation: Comalus, son of Verclovus, aged 53, lies here. (Erected by) Aisogius, for his father, aged 3, lies here.
EDCS-30100769.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the death of Comalus and Aisogius, with the latter possibly being a very young child buried with his father.

EDCS-30100942.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Heliopolitano Augusto sacrum Offellius sic ex voto\n
EDCS-30100942.translation: Sacred to Jupiter Optimus Maximus Heliopolitanus Augustus, Offellius, according to his vow.
EDCS-30100942.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus Heliopolitanus Augustus, made by Offellius in fulfillment of a vow, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30100947.summary: This inscription likely records the name of Marcus Antonius Tiberianus, possibly as a dedication or memorial, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30100949.translation: By the hand of Salvinius Atticus.
EDCS-30100949.summary: This inscription notes that the work was done by Salvinius Atticus, possibly a craftsman or scribe, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30101115.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aeliis Viatori Marcello pos<uit=TVI> memoriam Iustiniano Frequentianus \n
EDCS-30101115.translation: Frequentianus erected this in memory of Aelius Viator Marcellus for the departed spirits.
EDCS-30101115.summary: This is a funerary inscription by Frequentianus commemorating Aelius Viator Marcellus, likely from the late Roman period.

EDCS-30101116.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Valens et Melania Caeserni Aviti servi sibi et Valentinae annorum XX et Donico annorum II filiis karissimis\n
EDCS-30101116.translation: For the departed spirits: Valens and Melania, slaves of Caesernus Avitus, set this up for themselves and for their dearest sons, Valentina, aged 20, and Donicus, aged 2.
EDCS-30101116.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by two slaves, Valens and Melania, for themselves and their young sons, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30101117.text_edition: \n Vero primo pilo legionis legionis IIII Flaviae Proculinae @(obitae) nnorum L \n
EDCS-30101117.translation: Vero, first-rank soldier of the Fourth Flavian Proculian Legion, aged 50.
EDCS-30101117.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the name and age of Vero, a soldier from the Legio IIII Flaviae Proculinae, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30101174.translation: Blastus for the welfare of D. Lupianus.
EDCS-30101174.summary: This inscription is a prayer or dedication by Blastus for the well-being of D. Lupianus, possibly from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30200459.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo et Iunoni sacrum pro salute sua et collatorum us et Aelius Valerius et Aelius Secundinus magistri donum dederunt\n
EDCS-30200459.translation: Sacred to Jupiter Optimus Maximus and Juno, for their own welfare and that of their colleagues. Aelius Valerius and Aelius Secundinus, the directors, have given this gift.
EDCS-30200459.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter and Juno made by Aelius Valerius and Aelius Secundinus, who held the office of 'magistri', for their collective welfare, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30200460.text_edition: \n Silvano Domestico sacrum Quintus Crispius Ga<ll=II>icanus \n
EDCS-30200460.translation: Sacred to Silvanus Domestticus, Quintus Crispius Gallicanus.
EDCS-30200460.summary: This is a dedication to Silvanus Domestticus by Quintus Crispius Gallicanus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30200461.text_edition: \n Marcus Aurelius Diogenes legionis XIIII geminae Severianae bbenefficiarii consularis votum solverunt libentes merito\n
EDCS-30200461.translation: Marcus Aurelius Diogenes, benefit officials of the Fourteenth Gemina Severian Legion, have paid their vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-30200461.summary: This inscription records the fulfillment of a vow by Marcus Aurelius Diogenes and other benefit officials of the Legio XIIII Gemina Severiana, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30200462.text_edition: \n Domus aeterna Gaudentio filio piissimo qui vixit annos VI menses VI dies X horas noctis VIII fati munus complevit\n
EDCS-30200462.translation: An eternal home for Gaudentius, most dutiful son, who lived 6 years, 6 months, 10 days, and 8 hours of the night, fulfilling the gift of fate.
EDCS-30200462.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a young boy named Gaudentius, likely dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30200463.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Lucilius Sextus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-30200463.translation: Lucilius Sextus gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus.
EDCS-30200463.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Lucilius Sextus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30200464.text_edition: \n Apollinaris Isidi votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-30200464.translation: Apollinaris gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Isis.
EDCS-30200464.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to the goddess Isis by Apollinaris, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30200466.text_edition: \n Deo Soli Invicto Mithrae pro salute dominorum nostrorum Augustorum et [Getae] Caesaris Salvianus eorundem servus |contrascriptor stationis Atrantinae votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-30200466.translation: For the safety of our lords the Augusti and Caesar Geta, Salvianus, their slave and counter-scribe of the Atrantinian station, gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to the Unconquered Sol Mithras.
EDCS-30200466.summary: This is a military or administrative votive inscription dedicated to Sol Invictus Mithras for the safety of the emperors, by a slave named Salvianus, likely dating to the early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-30200467.text_edition: \n Deo Soli Invicto Mithrae pro salute Flavi Iovini quot sic votum susceperat it sic super nascentem deum posuit Peregrino et Aemiliano consulibus patribus Virdio Firmo et Antonio Celeri\n
EDCS-30200467.translation: For the safety of Flavius Iovinus, who undertook this vow and placed it here upon the rising god, Peregrinus and Aemilianus being consuls, and Virdius Firmus and Antonius Celer being the fathers.
EDCS-30200467.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Sol Invictus Mithras, mentioning the consuls Peregrinus and Aemilianus, and likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30200468.text_edition: \n Deo Invicto Mithrae Licinius Maximinus miles legionis II Italicae votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-30200468.translation: Licinius Maximinus, a soldier of the II Italica legion, gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to the Unconquered Mithras.
EDCS-30200468.summary: This is a military votive inscription dedicated to Sol Invictus Mithras by a soldier named Licinius Maximinus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30200469.text_edition: \n Deo Invicto Mithrae pro salute Charidemi Augusti nostri vilici stationis Enensis Marcus Antonius Celer votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-30200469.translation: For the safety of Charidemus, steward of our Augustus at the Enensis station, Marcus Antonius Celer gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to the Unconquered Mithras.
EDCS-30200469.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Sol Invictus Mithras for the safety of a steward named Charidemus, by Marcus Antonius Celer, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30200470.text_edition: \n Deo Soli Invicto Mithrae pro salute Aureli \n
EDCS-30200470.translation: For the safety of Aurelius [---] to the Unconquered Sol Mithras.
EDCS-30200470.summary: This is a partial votive inscription dedicated to Sol Invictus Mithras for the safety of an individual named Aurelius, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30200471.text_edition: \n Deo Invicto Mithrae Secundus Acuti votum solvit libens laetus merito\n
EDCS-30200471.translation: Secundus Acuti gladly, joyfully, and deservedly fulfilled his vow to the Unconquered Mithras.
EDCS-30200471.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Sol Invictus Mithras by Secundus Acuti, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30200472.text_edition: \n Deo Soli Mithrae Ulpius Lupuspro salute sua \n
EDCS-30200472.translation: Ulpius Lupus for his own safety to the Unconquered Sol Mithras.
EDCS-30200472.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Sol Invictus Mithras for his own safety by Ulpius Lupus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30200473.text_edition: \n Deo Soli Invicto Mithrae votum solvit Ulpius Ve posuit pro se e<t=S> suis pro suorum \n
EDCS-30200473.translation: Ulpius fulfilled his vow to the unconquered god Mithras; he placed this for himself and his family, and for his family members.
EDCS-30200473.summary: A dedication to the god Sol Invictus Mithras, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30200477.text_edition: \n Nutricibus pro salute Iucundae dominae et Heracliti fili Philo pater votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-30200477.translation: Philo, the father, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow for the health of his mistress Iucunda and of his son Heraclitus, for their nurses.
EDCS-30200477.summary: A votive dedication by a father for his household staff, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30300556.summary: An incomplete inscription possibly recording a name or title, date unknown.

EDCS-30300795.text_edition: \n Lucius Cossutius Luci filius Sabatina Costa tribunus militum legionis XV Apollinaris\n
EDCS-30300795.translation: Lucius Cossutius, son of Lucius, of the Sabatina tribe, from Costa, a military tribune of the XV Apollinaris legion.
EDCS-30300795.summary: An inscription identifying a military officer serving in the XV Apollinaris legion, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30300796.text_edition: \n Publius Sulpicius Publi filius Aniensi Cremona eques legionis XV Apollinaris annorum XL stipendiorum XXI hic situs est testamento fieri iussit arbitratu Cai Lucili Luci filii Publilia heredis\n
EDCS-30300796.translation: Publius Sulpicius, son of Publius, of the Aniensi tribe, from Cremona, a cavalryman of the XV Apollinaris legion, aged 40, with 21 years of service, is buried here; he ordered this to be made by his will under the arbitration of Caius Lucilius, son of Lucius, of the Publilia tribe, his heir.
EDCS-30300796.summary: A funerary inscription for a cavalryman of the XV Apollinaris legion, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30300800.text_edition: \n Lucius Arunteius Celer miles legionis XV Apollinaris nnorum LIIII stipendiorum XXIIII hic situs <e=P>(st) \n
EDCS-30300800.translation: Lucius Arunteius Celer, a soldier of the XV Apollinaris legion, aged 54, with 24 years of service, is buried here.
EDCS-30300800.summary: A funerary inscription for a soldier of the XV Apollinaris legion, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30300806.summary: A dedication likely to the goddess Flora by a woman named Scaptina, date unknown.

EDCS-30300807.text_edition: \n Legio XIIII gemina Martia victricis cohors VI (centuria) Lutati Expectati\n
EDCS-30300807.translation: The VI cohort of the VIth legion Gemina Martia Victrix, the century of Lucius Expectatus.
EDCS-30300807.summary: A military inscription identifying a unit and a centurion within the XIIII Gemina legion, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30300811.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar divi Marci Antonini Pii Germanici Sarmatici filius divi Commodi frater divi Antonini Pii nepos divi Hadriani pronepos divi Traiani Parthici abnepos divi Nervae adnepos Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus maximus pontifex maximus tribnicia potestate XIIII imperator XIII consul III pater patriae proconsul et Imperator Caesar Luci Septimi Severi Pii Pertinacis Augusti Arabici Adiabenici Parthici maximi filius divi Marci Antonini Pii Germanici Sarmatici nepos divi Antonini Pii pronepos divi Hadriani abnepos divi Traiani Parthici et divi Nervae adnepos Marcus Aurellius sic Antoninus Pius Augustus tribunicia potestate VIIII consul II proconsul nomina militum qui militaverunt in cohortibus praetoriis decem I II III IIII V VI VII VIII VIIII X piis vindicibus qui pie et fortiter militia functi sunt ius tribuimus conubi dumtaxat singulis et primis uxoribus ut etiamsi peregrini iuris feminas in matrimonio suo iunxerint proinde liberos tollant ac si ex duobus civibus Romanis natos ante diem VIII Kalendas Martias Marco Nummio Albino et Lucio Fulvio Aemiliano consulibus cohortis X praetoriae piae vindicis Caio Iulio Cai filio Aelia Passari Mogionibus descriptum et recognitum ex tabula aerea quae fixa est Romae in muro post templum divi Augusti ad Minervam Marci Aureli Aquilini Cai Valeri Marcellini Luci Corneli Augurini Cai Fabi Septimini Marci Aureli Firmini Luci Tertini Vitalis Cai Valeri Vitalis\n
EDCS-30300811.translation: Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus, Chief Priest, with tribunician power for the 14th time, Imperator for the 13th time, Consul for the 3rd time, Father of the Fatherland, Proconsul; and Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Augustus, son of Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus, grandson of divine Marcus Antoninus Pius Germanicus Sarmaticus, great-grandson of divine Antoninus Pius, great-great-grandson of divine Hadrian, great-great-great-grandson of divine Trajan Parthicus, and great-great-great-great-grandson of divine Nerva, with tribunician power for the 9th time, Consul for the 2nd time, Proconsul. To the soldiers who served in the ten Praetorian cohorts, the pious vindicators, who faithfully and bravely performed their military service, we grant the right of marriage only to a single and first wife, so that even if they join women of peregrine status in marriage, they may have children as if born from two Roman citizens, on the day before the 8th day before the Kalends of March, during the consulship of Marcus Nummius Albinus and Lucius Fulvius Aemilianus. For the 10th Praetorian cohort, the pious vindicator. Recorded and confirmed from a bronze tablet fixed at Rome on the wall behind the Temple of Divine Augustus, near the Temple of Minerva, by Marcus Aurelius Aquilinus, Caius Valerius Marcellinus, Lucius Cornelius Augurinus, Caius Fabius Septiminus, Marcus Aurelius Firminus, Lucius Tertinius Vitalis, Caius Valerius Vitalis.
EDCS-30300811.summary: An imperial rescript issued by Emperors Septimius Severus and Caracalla granting the right of marriage to soldiers and their wives, dated to March 1, 197 AD.

EDCS-30301324.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Titus Accius Severus beneficiarius consularis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-30301324.translation: Titus Accius Severus, a staff officer of the consular legate, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus.
EDCS-30301324.summary: A votive dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by a military staff officer, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30301325.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Marcus Ulpius Placidinus beneficiarius consularis II Silanis consulibus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-30301325.translation: To Jupiter, Best and Greatest. Marcus Ulpius Placidinus, staff-bearer of the governor, paid his vow willingly and deservedly during the consulship of Silanis, for the second time.
EDCS-30301325.summary: A dedication to Jupiter by a military officer, Marcus Ulpius Placidinus, likely from the late 2nd or early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-30301326.text_edition: \n Terrae Matri Genetrici Caius Cassius Karinus decurio municipii Mogetianae IIIIvir flamen pro se suisque omnibus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-30301326.translation: To Mother Earth, the Progenitress. Caius Cassius Karinus, a decurion of the municipality of Mogetiana, a four-man official and priest, paid his vow willingly and deservedly for himself and all his family.
EDCS-30301326.summary: A dedication to the Earth Mother by Caius Cassius Karinus, a local official in Mogetiana, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-30301327.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aelio Caro tribuno militum legionis XXX Ulpiae Germaniae inferioris qui vixit annis LIII Aelia Statuta coniugi bene merenti\n
EDCS-30301327.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Aelia Statuta to her well-deserving husband, Aelius Caro, tribune of the soldiers of the XXX Ulpia Victrix legion of Lower Germany, who lived 53 years.
EDCS-30301327.summary: A tombstone inscription for Aelius Caro, a military tribune, set up by his wife Aelia Statuta, likely from the late 2nd or early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-30301328.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelius Ianuarius eques legionis I adiutricis piae fidelis et Ulpiae Ianuariae coniugi carissimae et Aureliae Ianuariae filiae pientissimae vixit nnos XII et Aurelio Materno filio \n
EDCS-30301328.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Aurelius Ianuarius, an equestrian of the First Adjutant Pia Fidelis legion, and for his dearest wife Ulpia Ianuaria, and for his most dutiful daughter Aurelia Ianuaria, who lived 12 years, and for his son Aurelius Maternus.
EDCS-30301328.summary: A family tombstone inscription for Aurelius Ianuarius, a soldier, his wife, and children, likely from the late 2nd or early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-30301329.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marco Aurelio Aelia Mogetiana Neratio beneficiario tribuni legionis I adiutricis stipendiorum XXVII Gaius Iulius Surus beneficiarius praefecti legionis eiusdem Marcus Aurelius Oneratus bene merenti posuerunt\n
EDCS-30301329.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Gaius Iulius Surus, staff-bearer of the prefect of the same legion, and Marcus Aurelius Oneratus, for their well-deserving Marcus Aurelio Aelia Mogetiana Neratio, staff-bearer of the tribune of the First Adjutant legion, with 27 years of service, erected this monument.
EDCS-30301329.summary: A tombstone inscription for a soldier, Marcus Aurelio Aelia Mogetiana Neratio, erected by two other military personnel, likely from the late 2nd or early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-30301330.text_edition: \n Nemesi Auggustorum Caius Publ Censorinus veteranus legionis XIIII geminae ex optione custodiarum votum solvit libens laetus merito Muciano et Fabiano consulibus\n
EDCS-30301330.translation: To Nemesis, Augusta. Caius Publilius Censorinus, a veteran of the XIV Gemina legion, chosen for guard duty, paid his vow willingly, joyfully, and deservedly during the consulship of Mucianus and Fabius.
EDCS-30301330.summary: A dedication to the goddess Nemesis by a veteran soldier, Caius Publilius Censorinus, likely from the late 2nd or early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-30301331.text_edition: \n miles legionis XV Apollinaris centuria Cai Calidi Firmi annorum XXXV stipendiorum XIIII hic situs est heredes posierunt sic Caius Aurelius et Titus Aurelius\n
EDCS-30301331.translation: Here lies a soldier of the XV Apollinaris legion, of the century of Caius Calidius Firmus, aged 35, with 14 years of service. His heirs set up this monument: Caius Aurelius and Titus Aurelius.
EDCS-30301331.summary: A tombstone inscription for a soldier of the XV Apollinaris legion, erected by his heirs, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-30301332.text_edition: \n Tricuai Ancluiccus annorum XXV Cossia annorum XV frater possuit sic\n
EDCS-30301332.translation: Tricuaius Ancluiccus, aged 25. Cossia, aged 15. His brother set this up.
EDCS-30301332.summary: A tombstone inscription for two young individuals, Tricuaius Ancluiccus and Cossia, set up by their brother, likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-30501245.text_edition: \n et Q Blaesus contubernalis eius de suo libens laetus dedit\n
EDCS-30501245.translation: And his tent-mate Q. Blaesus gave this willingly and joyfully at his own expense.
EDCS-30501245.summary: A supplementary inscription likely related to another monument, recording a gift from a soldier named Q. Blaesus to his tent-mate, from the Roman period.

EDCS-30600076.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. To Titus Aurelius...
EDCS-30600076.summary: The beginning of a tombstone inscription dedicated to Titus Aurelius, likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-30600078.text_edition: \n Marcus Ulpius Priscianus Prisci filius annorum XVIII\n
EDCS-30600078.translation: Marcus Ulpius Priscianus, son of Priscus, aged 18.
EDCS-30600078.summary: This inscription records the name and age of Marcus Ulpius Priscianus, likely from a tombstone.

EDCS-30600079.text_edition: \n Nymphis quae in nemore sunt arulam Titus Pomponius Atticus numini adiutrici devotam dedicavit\n
EDCS-30600079.translation: Titus Pomponius Atticus dedicated here an altar consecrated to the Nymphs of the grove, as a helper deity.
EDCS-30600079.summary: This inscription records the dedication of an altar to the Nymphs by Titus Pomponius Atticus, likely in a rural or sacred grove setting.

EDCS-30600081.summary: This inscription records a sacred dedication to Silvanus Domesticus, the god of household and boundary spirits, likely on a shrine or altar.

EDCS-30600082.summary: This is a brief inscription recording a sacred dedication, possibly to an unnamed deity, made by Lucius Saxianus.

EDCS-30600083.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo pro salute Augusti Atilius Severus veteranus legionis X geminae votum solvit\n
EDCS-30600083.translation: For the safety of our Emperor Augustus, Atilius Severus, a veteran of Legio X Gemina, fulfilled his vow to Jupiter Best and Greatest.
EDCS-30600083.summary: This inscription records a veteran, Atilius Severus, fulfilling a vow to Jupiter for the emperor's safety, dating it to the period when Augustus was emperor.

EDCS-31000217.summary: This inscription simply records the name Titus Aurelius Nica, likely from a tombstone or dedication.

EDCS-31000219.text_edition: \n Tiberi Claudi Themisonis lysiponum ad lacrimas restringendas Tiberi Claudi Themisonis diasmyrnes post impetum ex ovo Tiberi Claudi Themisonis stactum ad claritatem Tiberi Claudi Themisonis <sp=PS>haerion post impetum \n
EDCS-31000219.translation: Tiberius Claudius Themiso's perfume for stopping tears, Tiberius Claudius Themiso's myrrh oil after a blow, Tiberius Claudius Themiso's fragrant oil for clarity, Tiberius Claudius Themiso's [---] after a blow.
EDCS-31000219.summary: This inscription lists several cosmetic or medicinal products attributed to Tiberius Claudius Themiso, suggesting his profession as a perfumer or apothecary.

EDCS-31000365.summary: This inscription records the name and age of Petrucil, likely from a tombstone for a child.

EDCS-31000515.summary: This inscription identifies Hippumenes as belonging to the emperor, possibly as a freedman or an imperial official.

EDCS-31000779.summary: This inscription likely records a dedication or memorial for two individuals named Traulus and Crispinilla.

EDCS-31001338.text_edition: \n ROT S EI XXXXV Scorpianus agitator factionis natione Afer vicit DCC XX ET\n
EDCS-31001338.translation: Scorpianus, charioteer of the Green faction, an African by nation, won 720 races.
EDCS-31001338.summary: This is a record of the racing achievements of Scorpianus, a charioteer from the Green faction, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31001355.summary: This inscription is a dedication or memorial to Lucius Rasinius Pisanus, dating possibly to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31001365.summary: This inscription appears to be a dedication or memorial to Intincus, son of Dubnac, possibly of Gallic origin, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31001366.summary: This is a wish for good health or longevity addressed to Mariniana, likely a personal inscription from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31001375.summary: This is a maker's inscription, indicating that Saturio(?) was the craftsman or producer of the object, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31200561.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Quintus Iulius Emeritus \n
EDCS-31200561.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Quintus Iulius Emeritus.
EDCS-31200561.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Quintus Iulius Emeritus, likely from a private or public religious context in the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31200715.text_edition: \n Nemesi sacrum pro salute Augusti Claudius Victor votum solvit libens laetus merito\n
EDCS-31200715.translation: Claudius Victor, in fulfillment of a vow, gladly, joyfully, and deservedly dedicated this sacred offering to Nemesis for the safety of the Emperor.
EDCS-31200715.summary: This inscription records a votive offering made by Claudius Victor to the goddess Nemesis for the emperor's safety, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31200718.text_edition: \n Man primus pilus legionis XIIII g(eminae)\n
EDCS-31200718.translation: The first-rank soldier of the Legio XIIII Gemina.
EDCS-31200718.summary: This inscription identifies a soldier, possibly a tombstone or military record, as a primus pilus (senior centurion) of the Legio XIIII Gemina, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31200720.translation: Titus Flavius Viator made the burial place.
EDCS-31200720.summary: This inscription records that Titus Flavius Viator constructed a burial place or tomb, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31200721.text_edition: \n Pro salute Augusti Deo Invicto Magnius Heracla donum dedit\n
EDCS-31200721.translation: For the safety of the Emperor, Magnius Heracla gave this gift to the Unconquered God.
EDCS-31200721.summary: This inscription is a dedication by Magnius Heracla of a gift to an Unconquered God for the emperor's safety, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31200722.text_edition: \n Caius Iulius Propinquus parietem ex voto fecit\n
EDCS-31200722.translation: Gaius Julius Propinquus built the wall as a vow.
EDCS-31200722.summary: This inscription records a private dedication by Gaius Julius Propinquus of a wall, likely a votive offering.

EDCS-31200723.text_edition: \n Caius Iulius Propinquos sic parietem ex voto impensa sua fecit\n
EDCS-31200723.translation: Gaius Julius Propinquus willingly and deservedly built the wall himself as a vow.
EDCS-31200723.summary: This inscription records a private dedication by Gaius Julius Propinquus of a wall, stating he built it at his own expense as a votive offering.

EDCS-31200725.text_edition: \n Valerius Crescens Herculi votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-31200725.translation: Valerius Crescens willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Hercules.
EDCS-31200725.summary: This inscription records a private vow fulfillment by Valerius Crescens to the god Hercules.

EDCS-31200726.text_edition: \n Iunoni sacrum Firminilla votum solvit merito\n
EDCS-31200726.translation: Firminilla fulfilled her vow to Juno willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-31200726.summary: This inscription records a private vow fulfillment by Firminilla to the goddess Juno.

EDCS-31200727.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Caius Iulius Vere veteranus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-31200727.translation: Gaius Julius Vere, veteran, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus.
EDCS-31200727.summary: This inscription records a veteran named Gaius Julius Vere fulfilling a vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus.

EDCS-31200729.text_edition: \n Silvanis et Quadrubiis sacrum AIII ia votum solvit libens laeta merito\n
EDCS-31200729.translation: Sacred to the Silvanes and Quadrubiae; AIIIia willingly and joyfully fulfilled her vow, as deserved.
EDCS-31200729.summary: This inscription records a private vow fulfillment by AIIIia to the deities Silvanes and Quadrubiae.

EDCS-31200730.text_edition: \n Silvano Domestico sacrum ius Rufus votum solvit libens laetus merito\n
EDCS-31200730.translation: Sacred to Silvanus Domestus; Rufus willingly and joyfully fulfilled his vow, as deserved.
EDCS-31200730.summary: This inscription records a private vow fulfillment by Rufus to the deity Silvanus Domestus.

EDCS-31200731.text_edition: \n Primigenius Pelli Arti votum libens merito\n
EDCS-31200731.translation: Primigenius willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Pella Arta.
EDCS-31200731.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfillment by Primigenius to a person named Pella Arta.

EDCS-31200746.text_edition: \n Phaontis Augusti liberti a rationibus Logi\n
EDCS-31200746.translation: Logi, formerly a freedman of Augustus, in charge of accounts.
EDCS-31200746.summary: This inscription identifies Logi, a freedman of Augustus and an official of accounts (a rationibus), likely marking his property or status.

EDCS-31300273.text_edition: \n Silvano et Silvanis et Quadrubiis sic sacrum Lucius Minicius Honoratus signifer legionis X geminae votum solvit libens laetus\n
EDCS-31300273.translation: Sacred to Silvanus, the Silvanes, and the Quadrubiae; Lucius Minicius Honoratus, standard-bearer of the tenth Gemina legion, willingly and joyfully fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-31300273.summary: This inscription records Lucius Minicius Honoratus, a standard-bearer in the tenth Gemina legion, fulfilling a vow to the deities Silvanus, Silvanes, and Quadrubiae.

EDCS-31300293.text_edition: \n Libero Patri et Liberae Dionysius actor Brittici Crescentis primi pili votum solvit\n
EDCS-31300293.translation: To Liber Pater and Libera, Dionysius, agent for Britticus Crescentis, a centurion of the first rank, fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-31300293.summary: This is a votive inscription by Dionysius, an agent for Britticus Crescentis, to the gods Liber Pater and Libera, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31300297.text_edition: \n ium scandulam et tegerunt navale Consentius et Aurelianus et Rufianus discens illorum\n
EDCS-31300297.translation: Consentius, Aurelianus, and Rufianus, his students, covered the shed roof with shingles.
EDCS-31300297.summary: This inscription records the work of roofing a shed, performed by Consentius, Aurelianus, and their student Rufianus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31500095.translation: [---] the heir of our lower circus [---]
EDCS-31500095.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription mentioning an heir related to a 'lower circus', its purpose and date are unclear.

EDCS-31500096.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription, possibly mentioning a name like Aurelius, with its purpose and date unknown.

EDCS-31500098.text_edition: \n Titus Vedius Fuscinus et Mercusenia Mesia Cai liberta fecerunt Iulia Vera Cai filia annorum XX\n
EDCS-31500098.translation: Titus Vedius Fuscinus and Mercusenia Mesia, freedwoman of Caius, made this; Iulia Vera, daughter of Caius, aged 20.
EDCS-31500098.summary: This inscription records the makers of a monument as Titus Vedius Fuscinus and Mercusenia Mesia, with Iulia Vera, aged 20, also mentioned, likely a funerary monument from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31500099.text_edition: \n o annorum XI e annorum II ens viv(us) fecit et sibi et filiis et Salae coniugi pientissimae NNEANIMNISO Senili MELCSEC\n
EDCS-31500099.translation: [---] aged 11, [---] aged 2, made this while alive for himself and his sons and his most devoted wife Salae, [illegible names].
EDCS-31500099.summary: This inscription records a man dedicating a monument for himself, his sons, and his wife Salae, and also mentions two children of unspecified ages, likely a funerary monument from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31500102.text_edition: \n Silvano Caius Iulius Gem eques votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-31500102.translation: Gaius Julius Gem, an equestrian, willingly fulfilled his vow to Silvanus, as was deserved.
EDCS-31500102.summary: This is a votive inscription by Gaius Julius Gem, an equestrian, to the god Silvanus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31500105.translation: Sextus Aelius made this for the Fates.
EDCS-31500105.summary: This inscription records Sextus Aelius dedicating something to the Fates, likely a religious dedication from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31500106.text_edition: \n Genio |(centuriae) Aurelius Trofimas sic tesserarius legionis XIIII geminae Martiae victricis votum libens solvit\n
EDCS-31500106.translation: To the Genius of the century, Aurelius Trofimas, tessellarius (badge-bearer) of the 14th Legion Gemina Martia Victrix, willingly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-31500106.summary: This is a votive inscription by Aurelius Trofimas, a tessellarius of the 14th Legion Gemina Martia Victrix, to the Genius of his century, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31500109.text_edition: \n Minervae Publius Ateius Emeritus Iovi Optimo Maximo Primitivus votum solvit\n
EDCS-31500109.translation: To Minerva, Publius Ateius Emeritus; to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Primitivus fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-31500109.summary: This inscription records two separate votive dedications: Publius Ateius Emeritus to Minerva, and Primitivus to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31500110.text_edition: \n Dis Nocturnis Mard votum libens laetus solvit\n
EDCS-31500110.translation: Mard has freely and joyfully fulfilled his vow to the Gods of the Night.
EDCS-31500110.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by Mard to the Gods of the Night.

EDCS-31500112.text_edition: \n Quadriviis sacrum Caius Sulpicius Seneca votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-31500112.translation: Dedicated to the crossroads; Gaius Sulpicius Seneca freely and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-31500112.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by Gaius Sulpicius Seneca at a crossroads.

EDCS-31500113.text_edition: \n Quadribis! sacrum Caius Sulpicius Seneca Cai Sulpicii filius DV \n
EDCS-31500113.translation: Dedicated to the crossroads! Gaius Sulpicius Seneca, son of Gaius Sulpicius, [made this dedication].
EDCS-31500113.summary: This inscription records a dedication at a crossroads by Gaius Sulpicius Seneca.

EDCS-31500114.text_edition: \n Quadriviis sacrum Caius S votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-31500114.translation: Dedicated to the crossroads; Gaius S. freely and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-31500114.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by Gaius S. at a crossroads.

EDCS-31500116.text_edition: \n V Aug sacrum Quinti filius Priscus N CVO et\n
EDCS-31500116.translation: Dedicated to the divine Augustus; Priscus, son of Quintus, [made this dedication].
EDCS-31500116.summary: This inscription records a dedication to the divine Augustus by Priscus.

EDCS-31500118.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Silvanus Domesticius.

EDCS-31500119.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Silvanus Domesticius.

EDCS-31500120.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Silvanus Domesticius.

EDCS-31500121.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Silvanus Domesticius.

EDCS-31500122.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Silvanus Domesticius.

EDCS-31500123.text_edition: \n Silvano Domestico Iulius Florus votum solvit libens laetus merito\n
EDCS-31500123.translation: Julius Florus gladly, joyfully, and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Silvanus Domesticus.
EDCS-31500123.summary: This inscription records the fulfillment of a vow to the god Silvanus Domesticus by Julius Florus, likely a private dedication.

EDCS-31500124.text_edition: \n Silvanis et Quadrubiis sic sacrum Gr pro Aquilino nepote votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-31500124.translation: Sacred to the Silvan deities and the Quadrubiae; Gr fulfilled his vow gladly and deservedly for his grandson Aquilinus.
EDCS-31500124.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by Gr for his grandson Aquilinus, dedicated to the deities Silvanus and Quadrubiae.

EDCS-31500127.text_edition: \n Aulus Mumius Victurus votum libens merito\n
EDCS-31500127.translation: Aulus Mumius Victurus gladly and deservedly [fulfilled his vow].
EDCS-31500127.summary: This inscription records Aulus Mumius Victurus gladly fulfilling a vow, likely a private dedication.

EDCS-31500129.text_edition: \n Lucius Gavidius Luci filius heres ex testamento posuit\n
EDCS-31500129.translation: Lucius Gavidius, son of Lucius, erected this as his heir according to the will.
EDCS-31500129.summary: This inscription records Lucius Gavidius, acting as heir by testament, setting up a monument.

EDCS-31500132.text_edition: \n F et SSA Proculinus et Proculus lus ERENI\n
EDCS-31500132.summary: This inscription is too fragmentary to determine its full meaning, but it appears to mention two individuals, Proculinus and Proculus.

EDCS-31500138.summary: This inscription records a soldier of the XIII Legion, but is too damaged to provide further context.

EDCS-31500140.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Pius Felix Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate VIIII consul III pater patriae restituit a Brigetione milia passuum IX\n
EDCS-31500140.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Pius Felix Augustus, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the 9th time, consul for the 3rd time, father of the country, restored this 9 miles from Brigetio.
EDCS-31500140.summary: This imperial inscription records Emperor Alexander Severus restoring a road or structure for a distance of 9 miles from Brigetio, dated by his tribunician and consular powers.

EDCS-31500168.summary: This inscription likely refers to membership in a group or division called the Hesperian Decuria.

EDCS-31500172.summary: This inscription appears to be a simple name, possibly marking ownership or a dedication by Gaius Marius Victor.

EDCS-31500174.summary: This inscription is a genitive form of a name, likely indicating possession or association with Gaius Marius Valentis.

EDCS-31500175.summary: This is a very brief inscription likely naming a person with initials P. R. E.

EDCS-31500176.summary: This inscription likely records the name of a person, possibly Gaius Sabe.

EDCS-31500178.summary: This inscription records the name of Seius Peculiaris, possibly as a tombstone or dedication.

EDCS-31500179.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Iovinius Seius Xarius.

EDCS-31500181.summary: This is a very brief inscription likely naming a person with initials C. V. S.

EDCS-31900012.summary: This inscription names Flavius, who held the office of prefect.

EDCS-32000869.text_edition: \n Silvano Domestico sacrum Ulpius Potens beneficarius consularis votum solvit\n
EDCS-32000869.translation: Sacred to Silvanus Domesticus; Ulpius Potens, a beneficiarius of the consular governor, willingly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-32000869.summary: This dedication records Ulpius Potens, a subordinate official to a consular governor, fulfilling a vow to the god Silvanus Domesticus.

EDCS-32000870.text_edition: \n Silvano Deo Domestico solvit sacrum libens Lucius Veturius Titianus beneficiarius consularis\n
EDCS-32000870.translation: Sacred to Silvanus, the Domestic God; Lucius Veturius Titianus, a beneficiarius of the consular governor, willingly and joyfully fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-32000870.summary: This dedication records Lucius Veturius Titianus, a subordinate official to a consular governor, willingly fulfilling a vow to the god Silvanus Domesticus.

EDCS-32000871.text_edition: \n Herculi Augusto Ulpius Martinus candidatus domini nostri votum solvit libens laetus merito\n
EDCS-32000871.translation: To Hercules Augustus; Ulpius Martinus, a candidate of our lord, willingly and joyfully fulfilled his vow as is right.
EDCS-32000871.summary: This dedication records Ulpius Martinus, a candidate for an unspecified office under an emperor, fulfilling a vow to Hercules Augustus.

EDCS-32000875.text_edition: \n Lucius Antonius Luci filius Lemonia Magnus Bononia miles legionis X geminae centuria Cottae annorum LV aerum XXXVI hic situs est Octavia Data coniugi de suo posuit donum merenti sit tibi terra levis\n
EDCS-32000875.translation: Lucius Antonius Magnus, son of Lucius, of the Lemonia tribe, from Bononia, a soldier in the X Gemina legion, in the century of Cotta, aged 55, XXXVI years of service, lies here; Octavia Data placed this gift for her deserving husband from her own property; may the earth lie lightly upon you.
