EDCS-52200563.summary: A votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter by Marcus Afranius Hannibalianus, a tribunus of the First Ulpian Cohort of Pannonia, likely in the late 2nd or early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-52200568.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Aurelius Vesbsinus vexillarius Sall3 Lucius veteranus votum libentes merito solverunt\n
EDCS-52200568.translation: To Jupiter, Best and Greatest. Aurelius Vesbsinus, a standard-bearer, and Lucius Sall[--], a veteran, have freely and deservedly fulfilled their vow.
EDCS-52200568.summary: A votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter by Aurelius Vesbsinus and Lucius Sall[--] a veteran, probably from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-52200569.text_edition: \n tiniani vixit annos LX Ursio nius Lucianus\n
EDCS-52200569.translation: Died at the age of 60. Ursionius Lucianus [erected this].
EDCS-52200569.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the death at 60 years of age of Ursionius Lucianus, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-52200570.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Avonia quae vixit nnos XXX et menses III et Mogitimarus heres sibi vivo titulum memoriae posuit\n
EDCS-52200570.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Avonia, who lived 30 years and 3 months, had this tombstone set up for herself while alive by Mogitimarus, her heir.
EDCS-52200570.summary: A funerary epitaph for Avonia, who died at the age of 30 years and 3 months, with the monument erected by her heir, Mogitimarus, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-52200571.text_edition: \n Eutycheni Tiberius Claudius Iulianus libertus et coniugi Claudiae Chresimae conlibertae piissimae titulum fecit\n
EDCS-52200571.translation: Tiberius Claudius Iulianus, his freedman, made this monument for Eutyches, and for Claudia Chresima, his fellow freedwoman and most devoted wife.
EDCS-52200571.summary: A funerary monument erected by Tiberius Claudius Iulianus for his freedman Eutyches and his wife Claudia Chresima, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-52302288.text_edition: \n Adverssarios sic nosstros sic Gaius Dometius sic Secundos sic et Lucius Larcios sic et Secundos sic Vacarus Cubalenses sic et Publius Citronius Cicorellius Narbonensis et Lucius Licc<i>nius sic Sura Hisspanus sic et Luccillius sic Vallen<s=TE> sic ne possint cuntra sic sse sic faceri sic avertat illos a maentes sic cantra sic locui ne mali illoru<m=S> mutum os faciat Gaius Domitius Ssecundos sic et Lucius Larcio Luci Cubalenses sic Muta Tagita sic Bona illorum Mandata data istos Savo curam agat depremat adversario(s) nosstros sic obmutuat ne contra nos lucuiant Data deprementi sic \n
EDCS-52302288.translation: So that our opponents may not be able to act against us, may Gaius Domitius Secundus, Lucius Larcius, Vacarus Cubalenses, Publius Citronius Cicorellius of Narbo, Lucius Liccinius Sura the Spaniard, Luccillius, and Vallen[--] avert them from us, and may their evil words make them mute. Given for the suppression of our opponents, so that they may not speak against us. Given for suppression.
EDCS-52302288.summary: A magical text invoking curses and binding spells against adversaries, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-54300026.text_edition: \n Lucius Tullius Luci filius Valerius nnorum LXXV Tulliae Cai filiae t vivus fecit coniugi \n
EDCS-54300026.translation: Lucius Tullius, son of Lucius, of the Valerius tribe, aged 75, made this for Tullia, daughter of Caius, his wife, while still alive.
EDCS-54300026.summary: A funerary monument erected by Lucius Tullius, aged 75, for his wife Tullia, while he was still alive, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-54300028.text_edition: \n D(is)? M(anibus) Tito Aelio iano tesserario legionis I adiutricis piae fidelis vixit annos XXVII menses VI dies XV etinae matri vivae et Ulpiae Valentinae sorori vivae et Senecioni et Aeternae filiis vivis Ulpius Aeternalis custos armorum legionis supra scriptae fratri karissimo faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-54300028.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Titus Aelius Ianus, a quartermaster of the First Legion Adiutrix Pia Fidelis, who lived 27 years, 6 months, and 15 days, and to his living mother, Etina, and his living sister, Ulpia Valentina, and his living children, Senecio and Aeterna. Ulpius Aeternalis, a weapons custodian of the aforementioned legion, had this made for his dearest brother.
EDCS-54300028.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to Titus Aelius Ianus by his brother, Ulpius Aeternalis, and also honors his mother and sister who were alive at the time of his death, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-54500952.text_edition: \n AV T AEL votum solv--- liben--- laet-- merito\n
EDCS-54500952.translation: T. Aelius fulfilled his vow willingly, with pleasure, deservedly.
EDCS-54500952.summary: This is a votive inscription recording the fulfillment of a vow by T. Aelius, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-54500957.text_edition: \n Ia lia annorum et Caio Sempronio Avito annorum XX ug coniuonis \n
EDCS-54500957.translation: Ialia, [aged...] years, and Gaius Sempronius Avitus, aged 20, [his?] wife.
EDCS-54500957.summary: This is a funerary inscription mentioning Ialia and Gaius Sempronius Avitus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-54500967.text_edition: \n Eventui Bono Gaius E Favorinus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-54500967.translation: Gaius Favorinus paid his vow willingly, deservedly, to Eventus Bonus.
EDCS-54500967.summary: This is a votive inscription recording Gaius Favorinus fulfilling a vow to the deity Eventus Bonus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-54500970.summary: This is a dedication to the deity Fons Perennis, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-54500971.text_edition: \n Numinibus Rubrius Euporio et Memmius Emeritus iussi curia I Caesius Sabinus Caesius Victor Caesius Probus Marcius Vindex Marcius Vindex Iunior Iulius Decibalus Ulpius Nigrinus Plosurnius Pantheriscus Rubrius Euporio Memmius Emeritus Petronius Onesiphorus Petronius Petronianus immunis Petronius Romulus Paeon Caesi Magni Narius Passer Flavius Iustus Vitellius Terminalis Vitellius Frontinus Petronius Ursulus Aelius Pegasus Domitius Eutyches Claudius Severinus Consilius Candidianus Symphorus Terrasiani Turellius Flavinus curia II EX Iustus Consilius Quintianus Claudius Privatus Valerius Valerianus Valentinus colonorum Manilius Secundus immunis Aurelius Ursulus Titius Ariston Rubrius Balbinianus Postumius Cassianus S<e=I>cundinus colonorum Ulpius Albus Blassius Sozomenus curia III Domitius Dionysius Domitius Domitianus Carminius Iuvencus Claudius Claudianus Antonius Avitus Apuleius Epitynchanus Plosurnius Tertius Eutyches Apuleiorum Valentinus Caesi Iulius Secundianus pel Claudius Valentinus Iulius Valentinus immunis curia IIII Quietius Iustinus Annius Valentinus Petronius Salvianus Sa<f=E>inius Saturninus Iulius Maximus Rufinus Flaviani Lamponius Verinus Lamponius Verus immunis Flavius Memor Farrax Alypus Dionysius Vimio<r=S>(rum) Bononius Niger Marcius Securus Claudius Apollonius Kanius Crescens Caesius Iustus Flavius Fortunatus curia V Valerius Firmus Arrius Cassianus Torius Severianus Aelius Calpurnianus Aelius Licinianus Servilius Victor Pacilius Attius Pacilius Bassus immunis Aurelius Fabricius Stlabonius Fuscinus Claudius Quintus Caesius Genetivus Ulpius Viator Lucilius Ursinus Lucilius Primus Atilius Restutus Caesius Candidus Colonius Valerianus Colonius Vindicianus immunis S<e=I>leucus colonorum Ulpius Quintianus Fusciano et Silano II consulibus sacerdote Romano \n
EDCS-54500971.translation: In the lower curia, with the freedmen of the colony. In the second curia, with the freedmen of the colony. In the third curia, with the freedmen of the colony. In the fourth curia, with the freedmen of the colony. In the fifth curia, with the freedmen of the colony, during the consulship of Fuscianus and Silanus, with the Roman priest.
EDCS-54500971.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription listing numerous individuals grouped by 'curia' (possibly local administrative or social divisions) and mentioning freedmen of a colony, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-54500973.text_edition: \n Receptus Claudius Ingenuus Tiberius Iulius Pa Antonius SM \n
EDCS-54500973.translation: Receptus, Claudius Ingenuus, Tiberius Iulius Pa..., Antonius S.M. ...
EDCS-54500973.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription mentioning several individuals, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-54500982.text_edition: \n SAB ER STVS CI SMA G T R\n
EDCS-54500982.translation: [...Sab]inus Erastus, the freedman of Cismagdus, [belonging to the] tribe R[...].
EDCS-54500982.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription mentioning a freedman named Sabinus Erastus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-54500986.translation: Gaius Favonius Regillus had this made.
EDCS-54500986.summary: This is an epitaph or commemorative inscription stating that Gaius Favonius Regillus commissioned the monument, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-54600822.translation: To Gaius Acilius Clement, son of Caius.
EDCS-54600822.summary: This is a dedication or funerary inscription to Gaius Acilius Clement, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-55701998.summary: This is a very brief inscription mentioning the god Silvanus, perhaps associated with a singer, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-55800020.summary: This inscription records the centuria (a military unit) belonging to Lucius Varro, likely from the Roman military context.

EDCS-57400165.text_edition: \n Peculiari annorum L annorum XXXV Iovincato annorum XVI Vattatus pater posuit\n
EDCS-57400165.translation: Peculiaris, aged 50, Iovincatus, aged 35, Vattatus, aged 16, his father, erected this.
EDCS-57400165.summary: This inscription records the erection of a monument by Vattatus, commemorating his sons Peculiaris and Iovincatus, indicating a dedication by a father to his children.

EDCS-57400166.text_edition: \n Hilarius Cucis vernaculus NN VSI CSCESH SI pater posuit\n
EDCS-57400166.translation: Hilarius, the houseborn slave of Cucis, his father, erected this.
EDCS-57400166.summary: This inscription records the erection of a monument by Hilarius, identified as the houseborn slave and son of Cucis, likely a funerary or commemorative dedication.

EDCS-59200149.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Aulus votum solvit libens laetus\n
EDCS-59200149.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Aulus willingly and gladly paid his vow.
EDCS-59200149.summary: This inscription records Aulus fulfilling a vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, a common dedication to the chief Roman deity.

EDCS-59200150.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo C Marcus Ulpius Celsinus VCE A votum solvit laetus libens merito\n
EDCS-59200150.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Marcus Ulpius Celsinus willingly, gladly, and deservedly paid his vow.
EDCS-59200150.summary: This inscription records Marcus Ulpius Celsinus fulfilling a vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, a common dedication to the chief Roman deity.

EDCS-59200151.translation: To Diana, Crispinia Supera, according to her vow.
EDCS-59200151.summary: This inscription records Crispinia Supera dedicating something to the goddess Diana in fulfillment of a vow.

EDCS-59200153.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription identifying a woman named Germana as a priestess, likely from a religious context.

EDCS-59200157.text_edition: \n Deo Silvano Constantius votum libies sic posuit\n
EDCS-59200157.translation: To the god Silvanus, Constantius willingly set this up as vowed.
EDCS-59200157.summary: This inscription records Constantius dedicating a monument or offering to the god Silvanus, fulfilling a vow.

EDCS-59200158.text_edition: \n Deo Invicto Mithrae Valerius Syrianus beneficiarius votum libens solvit\n
EDCS-59200158.translation: To the Unconquered god Mithras, Valerius Syrianus, a beneficiary, willingly paid his vow.
EDCS-59200158.summary: This inscription records Valerius Syrianus, identified with a military or administrative role (beneficiarius), fulfilling a vow to the god Mithras.

EDCS-59200160.summary: This is a dedication to the god Silvanus Domesticus, likely on a religious monument.

EDCS-59200162.text_edition: \n Silvano Domestico sacrum Ulpius Rufianus signifer votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-59200162.translation: Ulpius Rufianus, standard-bearer, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Silvanus Domesticus.
EDCS-59200162.summary: This inscription records Ulpius Rufianus, a standard-bearer, fulfilling a vow to Silvanus Domesticus.

EDCS-59200163.text_edition: \n Silvano Domestico sacrum Valerius Vitalis patri votum solvit ex viribus\n
EDCS-59200163.translation: Valerius Vitalis fulfilled his vow to Silvanus Domesticus according to his strength.
EDCS-59200163.summary: Valerius Vitalis made a dedication to Silvanus Domesticus, fulfilling a vow from his own resources.

EDCS-59200164.text_edition: \n Silvano Silvestro sic sacrum Claudius Severinus |(centurio) votum solvit s\n
EDCS-59200164.translation: Claudius Severinus, centurion, willingly fulfilled his vow to Silvanus Silvestris.
EDCS-59200164.summary: A centurion named Claudius Severinus made a dedication to Silvanus Silvestris.

EDCS-59200167.text_edition: \n Septimius Iustinus signifer legionis XIIII geminae ex voto posuit\n
EDCS-59200167.translation: Septimius Iustinus, standard-bearer of the Legio XIIII Gemina, dedicated this in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-59200167.summary: This is a votive dedication by Septimius Iustinus, a standard-bearer of the Legio XIIII Gemina.

EDCS-59200168.text_edition: \n Marcianus ndus votum solvit libens merito tinus minus Q R ius abius Paulianus Marcellinus inus Ulpius Fe Marcus Ulpius Neradius Aurelius Primigenius Aurelius Prancatus Ulpe(ius) Fortunius Aurelius Victorinus\n
EDCS-59200168.translation: Marcianus... willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow... [names of donors]...
EDCS-59200168.summary: This fragmented inscription records multiple individuals, including Marcianus and others, fulfilling vows, likely to a deity or for a public benefit.

EDCS-59200173.text_edition: \n Silvano Titus Cassius Cel(er) |(centurio) legionis XIIII geminae Martiae victricis Gordianae votum solvit libens laetus merito\n
EDCS-59200173.translation: Titus Cassius Celer, centurion of the Legio XIIII Gemina Martia Victrix Gordiana, willingly, joyfully, and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Silvanus.
EDCS-59200173.summary: Titus Cassius Celer, a centurion in a specific legion, dedicated this to Silvanus in fulfillment of a vow.

EDCS-59200174.summary: A simple dedication to Silvanus by Val Musa.

EDCS-59200175.text_edition: \n Silvano Domestico Magno sacrum Lucius Ulpius Ursianus votum solvit libens laetus merito\n
EDCS-59200175.translation: Lucius Ulpius Ursianus willingly, joyfully, and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Silvanus Domesticus Magnus.
EDCS-59200175.summary: Lucius Ulpius Ursianus made a votive dedication to Silvanus Domesticus Magnus.

EDCS-59200176.text_edition: \n Marcus Magius Marci filius Sabellus Publilia Verona veteranus legionis XV Apollinaris nnorum LXV hic situs est testamento fieri iussit frater faciundum curavit\n
EDCS-59200176.translation: Marcus Magius Sabellus, son of Marcius, a veteran of the Legio XV Apollinaris, aged 65, is buried here; he ordered this to be made in his will, and his brother saw to its construction.
EDCS-59200176.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Marcus Magius Sabellus, a veteran of the Legio XV Apollinaris, detailing his burial arrangements made by will.

EDCS-59200177.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo sacrum Aurelius Vindicio p f si votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-59200177.translation: Sacred to Jupiter Best and Greatest. Aurelius Vindicio, son of Publius, gladly and deservedly paid his vow.
EDCS-59200177.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Aurelius Vindicio, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-59200180.text_edition: \n Silvano Domestico sacrum Lucius Caninius Vitalis veteranus ex beneficiario consularis votum solvit libens laetus merito\n
EDCS-59200180.translation: Sacred to Silvanus Domestus. Lucius Caninius Vitalis, a veteran and former beneficiary of the consular legate, gladly, joyfully, and deservedly paid his vow.
EDCS-59200180.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Silvanus Domestus by Lucius Caninius Vitalis, a veteran, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-59200183.text_edition: \n Marcus Vinius Longinus veteranus legionis primae adiutricis annorum L hic situs est Marcus filius patri posuit et Mariccae Nunaes sic matri vivae posuit\n
EDCS-59200183.translation: Marcus Vinius Longinus, veteran of the First Legion Adjutrix, aged 50, lies here. His son Marcus placed this for his father and for Maricca Nunaes, his mother, while she was alive.
EDCS-59200183.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Marcus Vinius Longinus, a veteran of Legio I Adjutrix, set up by his son, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-59300001.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Marcus Acutius.
EDCS-59300001.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Marcus Acutius, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-59300004.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Marcus Valerius votum solvit libens\n
EDCS-59300004.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Marcus Valerius paid his vow gladly.
EDCS-59300004.summary: This is a votive inscription to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Marcus Valerius, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-59300005.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Sextus Iulius Quintus votum solvit libens\n
EDCS-59300005.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Sextus Iulius Quintus paid his vow gladly.
EDCS-59300005.summary: This is a votive inscription to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Sextus Iulius Quintus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-59300007.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Culminali pro salute Augusti Marcus Ulpius Super decurio coloniae Karnunti aedilicius una cum Marco Ulpio Maximo filio votum solvit libens laetus merito\n
EDCS-59300007.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest Culminalis, for the safety of the Emperor. Marcus Ulpius Super, decurion of the colony of Carnuntum, formerly aedile, together with his son Marcus Ulpius Maximus, gladly, joyfully, and deservedly paid his vow.
EDCS-59300007.summary: This is a votive inscription to Jupiter Culminalis for the Emperor's safety by Marcus Ulpius Super, a decurion and former aedile from Carnuntum, with his son, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-59300008.text_edition: \n Marti Caius Iulius Ver votum solvit libens laetus\n
EDCS-59300008.translation: To Mars, Caius Iulius Ver paid his vow gladly and joyfully.
EDCS-59300008.summary: This is a votive inscription to Mars by Caius Iulius Ver, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-59300009.text_edition: \n Mercurio sacrum Marcus Ulpius Quintillianus votum solvit libens\n
EDCS-59300009.translation: Sacred to Mercury. Marcus Ulpius Quintillianus paid his vow gladly.
EDCS-59300009.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Mercury by Marcus Ulpius Quintillianus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-59300010.summary: This is a dedication to Mercury by Aurelius Pertenius, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-59300025.text_edition: \n Pro salute Augusti sub Caio Atilio Novo Folnius Mundus strator votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-59300025.translation: For the health of Augustus, Folnius Mundus, the trainer, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-59300025.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by Folnius Mundus for the health of Emperor Augustus, likely during the reign of Augustus or shortly thereafter.

EDCS-59500047.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caius Longinius Severinus annorum LX decurio IIvir iuris dicundi Claudiae Celeiae vivus fecit sibi et Acutiae Matronae coniugi et Longinio Avito filio annorum XXXVII decurioni II viro iuris dicundi Claudia Celeia\n
EDCS-59500047.translation: For the departed spirits: Caius Longinius Severinus, aged 60, decurion and former *duovir iuris dicundi* of Claudia Celeia, made this tombstone for himself while alive, and for his wife Acutia Matrona, and for his son Longinius Avitus, aged 37, decurion and former *duovir iuris dicundi* of Claudia Celeia.
EDCS-59500047.summary: This funerary epitaph records the deceased Caius Longinius Severinus, who erected a tomb for himself and his family, likely in the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-59500048.text_edition: \n Valeriano IIII et Gallieno III Auggustis conssulibus Aurelius Claudius Salunas ex regione Doliche a vico Arpuartura ex re promisso et fidei bonae votum promissum solvit\n
EDCS-59500048.translation: During the consulship of Valerian for the 4th time and Gallienus for the 3rd time, Aurelius Claudius Salunas from the district of Doliche, from the village of Arpuartura, fulfilled his promised vow and his promise of good faith.
EDCS-59500048.summary: This inscription records Aurelius Claudius Salunas fulfilling a vow made in good faith during the consulship of Valerian and Gallienus, in the mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-59500049.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Caius Cassius Marcus pro salute \n
EDCS-59500049.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Caius Cassius Marcus, for the health of...
EDCS-59500049.summary: This is a votive inscription fragment dedicating an offering to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Caius Cassius Marcus for a prayer, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-60600069.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Domitius Antoninus beneficiarius consularis legionis I adiutricis piae fidelis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-60600069.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Domitius Antoninus, a staff officer of a consular legate of the I Adjutrix Pia Fidelis legion, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-60600069.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by Domitius Antoninus, a military official of the I Adjutrix legion, to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-62500015.translation: ... for Marcus Iu[---], for his co[nsuel---], for the aedile(?)...
EDCS-62500015.summary: This is a heavily fragmented inscription that appears to mention a name, a marital relationship, and potentially a public office, dating likely to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-62500018.translation: ... and Qu[---], for his wife, for [---]nate, for [---], son(?) aged 30, are buried here.
EDCS-62500018.summary: This is a fragmented funerary inscription indicating the burial of several individuals, including a son aged 30, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-62900318.text_edition: \n Firmianus RufinA Victorine Valeri Clare Vinator sic Sextilla Kardo Sasura Salviane Silvane\n
EDCS-62900318.translation: Firmianus, Rufina, Victorine, Valeria, Clara, Vina, Sic, Sextilla, Kardo, Sasura, Salviane, Silvane.
EDCS-62900318.summary: This inscription is a list of several personal names, possibly recording members of a family or group, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-63400205.text_edition: \n Nymphis Augustis Aelius Victorinus beneficiarius consularis et Lucilia coniunx votum solverunt libentes merito\n
EDCS-63400205.translation: To the August Nymphs, Aelius Victorinus, staff officer of a consular legate, and his wife Lucilia, willingly and deservedly fulfilled their vow.
EDCS-63400205.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by Aelius Victorinus, a military official, and his wife Lucilia to the Nymphs, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-64100105.summary: This is a fragmentary dedication to a spring, possibly a sacred one, belonging to or named after Perennius, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-64100106.text_edition: \n Isidi sacrum Claudia Tiberi filia Severa pro se et suis ex voto libens merito\n
EDCS-64100106.translation: Sacred to Isis. Claudia Severa, daughter of Tiberius, gladly and deservedly fulfilled her vow for herself and her family.
EDCS-64100106.summary: This is a votive dedication to the goddess Isis, made by Claudia Severa.

EDCS-65100003.summary: A brief inscription stating that Belatullus was the maker of the object.

EDCS-65100004.summary: A brief inscription stating that Augustinus was the maker of the object.

EDCS-65100006.summary: A brief inscription stating that Regulinus was the maker of the object.

EDCS-65100012.summary: This inscription simply records the name and origin of Gaius Valerius Constans.

EDCS-65100025.text_edition: \n Mercurio Primigenius Cosmi votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-65100025.translation: Primigenius Cosmi gladly and deservedly paid his vow to Mercury.
EDCS-65100025.summary: This is a votive dedication to the god Mercury, paid by Primigenius Cosmi.

EDCS-65600127.text_edition: \n Publio Aemilio filio Verecundo annorum LXXV et Publio Aemilio Publi filiovo annorum L filio eius domo Iudaei Publius Aemilius avoni sic et patri parentibus pientissimis posuit\n
EDCS-65600127.translation: Publius Aemilius dedicated this to Publius Aemilius Verecundus, his son, aged 75, and to Publius Aemilius, son of Publius, aged 50, his son, from Judaea; he dedicated it to his most devoted father and parents.
EDCS-65600127.summary: This is a funerary monument set up by Publius Aemilius for his father and son, both named Publius Aemilius, who were from Judaea.

EDCS-67300070.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription with mostly illegible text, likely from a tomb or monument.

EDCS-67400411.text_edition: \n g Crispina coniugi pientissimo vivi fecerunt sibi\n
EDCS-67400411.translation: Crispina made this for her most devoted husband while he was alive, for herself.
EDCS-67400411.summary: This is a dedication made by Crispina for her living husband and for herself.

EDCS-67400412.translation: The century of Marcus PET IV
EDCS-67400412.summary: This inscription likely refers to a military unit, specifically the century of Marcus, possibly part of a larger grouping denoted by 'PET IV'.

EDCS-67400413.translation: Of the century of Catus Potens.
EDCS-67400413.summary: This inscription records a military unit affiliation, likely from a tombstone or dedication, belonging to the century of Catus Potens.

EDCS-67600186.text_edition: \n Libero Patri E--- signum? cum base T VS \n
EDCS-67600186.translation: To Liber Pater, a statue [and?] base.
EDCS-67600186.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the god Liber Pater, mentioning a statue and its base.

EDCS-67600187.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelius Tertius armatura legionis primae adiutricae Antoniniana stipendiorum \n
EDCS-67600187.translation: To the Divine Shades. Aurelius Tertius, soldier of the First Antonine Auxiliary Legion, after [his period of] service.
EDCS-67600187.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Aurelius Tertius, a soldier of the Legio I Adiutrix Antoniniana, likely dating to the Antonine period.

EDCS-67600188.translation: Basileus paid his vow willingly, as deserved.
EDCS-67600188.summary: This inscription records a votive offering made by Basileus, likely to a deity, indicating the completion of a promised act.

EDCS-67600193.text_edition: \n Herenniae Rufinae virginiae obsequentissimae quae vixit nnos XX Gaius Caecilius Saturninus |(centurio) maritus faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-67600193.translation: Gaius Caecilius Saturninus, centurion, had this made for Herennia Rufina, a most obedient virgin who lived 20 years.
EDCS-67600193.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Herennia Rufina, a young woman who died at 20, commissioned by her husband, a centurion.

EDCS-67600194.text_edition: \n filii et heredes patri pientissimo et Occia Verecunda coniugi bene merenti\n
EDCS-67600194.translation: To their most devoted father and to Occia Verecunda, his well-deserving wife, from their sons and heirs.
EDCS-67600194.summary: This inscription is a joint dedication or epitaph for a father and his wife, Occia Verecunda, from their children.

EDCS-67600195.translation: Macer: a blue cloak, one denarius, two asses.
EDCS-67600195.summary: This inscription appears to be a record of a transaction or payment, detailing an item (a blue cloak) and its cost in Roman currency.

EDCS-67800056.text_edition: \n Pro salute et victoriis Imperatoris Caesaris Marci Aurell(i) Antonini Pii Felicis Augusti Lucius Alfenus Avitianus legatus legionis X geminae Antoninianae piae fidelis praeses provinciae Arabiae devotus numini maiestatique eius\n
EDCS-67800056.translation: For the health and victories of the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus. Lucius Alfenus Avitianus, legate of the Tenth Gemina Antoniniana Legion, pious and faithful, governor of the province of Arabia, devoted to his divine majesty.
EDCS-67800056.summary: This is a dedication by Lucius Alfenus Avitianus, governor of Arabia and legate of Legio X Gemina, to Emperor Marcus Aurelius, likely from the late 2nd century AD.

EDCS-68100097.text_edition: \n Cauti Aurelius Euandrus et Flavius Basilicus votum solverunt libentes laeti merito\n
EDCS-68100097.translation: Cauti: Aurelius Euandrus and Flavius Basilicus paid their vow willingly, joyfully, as deserved.
EDCS-68100097.summary: This inscription records a votive offering made by Aurelius Euandrus and Flavius Basilicus to Cautopates, indicating the completion of a promised act.

EDCS-68100098.text_edition: \n Cautopati Aurelius Euandrus et Flavius Basilicus votum solverunt libentes laeti merito\n
EDCS-68100098.translation: Cautopati: Aurelius Euandrus and Flavius Basilicus paid their vow willingly, joyfully, as deserved.
EDCS-68100098.summary: This inscription records a votive offering made by Aurelius Euandrus and Flavius Basilicus to Cautopates, indicating the completion of a promised act.

EDCS-68200015.text_edition: \n Quintus Gavius Quinti filius Publilia Fronto |(centurio) legionis XIII geminae praefectus civitatium Scordiscorum et Breucorum et Iasorum beneficio Imperatoris translatus successionibus in legionem IIII Macedonicam dedicavit Bachylus libertus eius\n
EDCS-68200015.translation: Quintus Gavius Fronto, son of Quintus, a centurion of the Legio XIII Gemina and prefect of the Scordisci, Breuci, and Iasi peoples, transferred through the Emperor's favor and successive commands to the Legio IIII Macedonica, dedicated this; Bachylus, his freedman, made it.
EDCS-68200015.summary: A freedman dedicates a monument to his former master, a military officer, likely in the 2nd century AD.

EDCS-68200016.text_edition: \n Minervae Augustae sacrum Lucius Claudius Moderatus\n
EDCS-68200016.translation: Sacred to Augusta Minerva. Lucius Claudius Moderatus dedicated this.
EDCS-68200016.summary: A private dedication to the goddess Minerva, likely in the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-68200017.text_edition: \n Minervae Augustae sacrum Dexter Quintus votum solverunt libentes merito\n
EDCS-68200017.translation: Sacred to Augusta Minerva. Dexter Quintus fulfilled their vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-68200017.summary: A joint private dedication to Minerva, where two individuals fulfill a vow.

EDCS-68200018.text_edition: \n Isidi et Serapi Augusti sacrum Caius Valerius Priscus Augustalis coloniae Ulpiae Traianae Poetoviensis et Caelenia Fortunata coniux sive Oricclio sic votum solvit libens merito Isidi et Serapi Augusti sacrum Caius Valerius Priscus Augustalis coloniae Ulpiae Traianae Poetoviensis et Caelenia Fortunata coniux sive Oricclio sic votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-68200018.translation: Sacred to the Augustan Isis and Serapis. Caius Valerius Priscus, an Augustalis of the colony Ulpia Traiana Poetoviensis, and his wife Caelenia Fortunata, also known as Oricclio, fulfilled their vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-68200018.summary: A married couple, including a local magistrate, dedicates an offering to the Egyptian deities Isis and Serapis.

EDCS-68200020.text_edition: \n Fortunae Iasonianae Nymphis Salutaribus ceterisque dis deabusque quorum haec tutella est Caius Iulius Victorinus decurio municipii VIIvir flamen voto suscepto pro civitatibus Varciani ENES pecunia privata pro salute fratri suo suorumque dedidit\n
EDCS-68200020.translation: To Iasonian Fortuna, to the Salutary Nymphs, and to all other gods and goddesses who protect this place, Caius Iulius Victorinus, a decurion of the municipality and a VIIvir and flamen, having undertaken a vow for the Varciani people, dedicated this from private funds for the well-being of his brother and his family.
EDCS-68200020.summary: A local magistrate dedicates an offering to various deities for the welfare of his community and family.

EDCS-68200022.text_edition: \n Nymphis Augustis sacrum Caius Valerius Phosphorus augustalis coloniae Ulpiae Traianae Poetoviensis pro salute sua et suorum votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-68200022.translation: Sacred to the Augustan Nymphs. Caius Valerius Phosphorus, an Augustalis of the colony Ulpia Traiana Poetoviensis, fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly for his own well-being and that of his family.
EDCS-68200022.summary: A local magistrate dedicates an offering to the Nymphs for the health of himself and his relatives.

EDCS-68200023.text_edition: \n Nymphis Augustis sacrum Verus Titi Iuli Ianuari conductoris portorii publici Illyrici servos sic ex privatis votum solvit Messius fecit\n
EDCS-68200023.translation: Sacred to the Augustan Nymphs. Verus, a slave of Titius Iulius Ianuarius, a conductor of the public customs duty in Illyricum, fulfilled his vow willingly. Messius made this.
EDCS-68200023.summary: A slave of a tax collector dedicates an offering to the Nymphs, with another individual identified as the maker.

EDCS-68200024.text_edition: \n Nimfeis sic Augustis Ulpia Iustina ex voto posuit libens\n
EDCS-68200024.translation: Ulpia Iustina placed this for the Augustan Nymphs according to her vow, willingly.
EDCS-68200024.summary: A woman named Ulpia Iustina dedicates an object to the Nymphs in fulfillment of a vow.

EDCS-68200026.summary: An incomplete inscription recording a dedication by Dillius to the Nymphs.

EDCS-68200028.text_edition: \n Aurelius Aquilinus decurio DVM et Aurelia Maximilla\n
EDCS-68200028.translation: Aurelius Aquilinus, decurion, and Aurelia Maximilla...
EDCS-68200028.summary: An inscription that appears to record the names of two individuals, likely a couple, possibly with an incomplete dedication.

EDCS-68300264.text_edition: \n Caio Valerio Firmino decurioni coloniae Savariae |(centurioni) cohortis V Lucensium Antoninianae Valeria Kalendina amita nepoti pientissimo reliquias condidit\n
EDCS-68300264.translation: To Gaius Valerius Firminus, decurion of the colony of Savaria (and centurion) of the V Lucensium Antoniniana cohort, Valeria Kalendina, his devoted aunt, erected this tomb for her most pious nephew, preserving his remains.
EDCS-68300264.summary: This funerary monument was erected by Valeria Kalendina for her nephew, Gaius Valerius Firminus, a decurion and centurion, likely in the 3rd century CE.

EDCS-68500330.text_edition: \n Seraphi sic sancto et Nymphis salutaribus Caecilius Saturninus \n
EDCS-68500330.translation: To Seraphis, the holy one, and to the saving Nymphs, Caecilius Saturninus [dedicated this].
EDCS-68500330.summary: This is a dedication to the deities Seraphis and the Nymphs by Caecilius Saturninus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-68600029.summary: A dedication to the god Silvanus by an individual named Cupitus, probably from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-68900027.text_edition: \n Valens Saturionis filius sibi vivus posuit et Verae Quintionis filiae coniugi annorum XXV hic sita est\n
EDCS-68900027.translation: Valens, son of Saturio, erected this for himself while alive, and Vera, daughter of Quintio, his wife, who is 25 years old, is buried here.
EDCS-68900027.summary: This is a self-funded funerary inscription for Valens, who also commemorates his wife Vera, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-69000023.text_edition: \n Manibus Dis Marcus Cocceius Iustus beneficiarius legati legionis I adiutricis stipendiorum XXVI posuit merenti votum piae et in arcam sepoliri sic iusserunt pater filiis Aeliam Iustinam uxorem et Cocceia<m> Marcellam filiam vivus\n
EDCS-69000023.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Marcus Cocceius Iustus, a beneficiary of the legate of the I Adjutrix legion, with 26 years of service, erected this for one deserving of honor. It was ordered that he be buried in the tomb, as was his father for his sons: Aelia Iustina, his wife, and Cocceia Marcella, his daughter, he dedicated this while alive.
EDCS-69000023.summary: This funerary inscription records Marcus Cocceius Iustus, a soldier with 26 years of service, dedicating a tomb for himself, his wife, and daughter, likely in the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-69200415.text_edition: \n LV Lucius Iulius L Aurelius Ur Aurelius Dubitatus Aurelius B(?) Fortunatus Nymphis Augustis Salutaribus collatores pecunia sua fecerunt imperatore domino nostro Gordiano Augusto et Aviola consulibus pridie Nonas Octobres Valerius Vitalinus Valerius Crispinianus Aurelius M Aureliustus Aurelius IM Aurelius LaC Aurelius Cassius S Stats Aurelius V Aurelius Va Cestius no Valerius Cus Kaus Iulius Do Se Vius Aurelius u V Aurelius Victorinus Iulius Euprorius AVR Aurelius Cuspis Aurelius Aquila ] ] Aurelia Marnusia Aurelius Antoninus Aurelius Lucianus\n
EDCS-69200415.translation: Lucius Iulius L. Aurelius Ur., Aurelius Dubitatus, Aurelius B(?) Fortunatus [and others]. To the August and Saving Nymphs, the collators made this with their own money, during the reign of our lord Gordian Augustus and Aviola as consul, on the day before the Nones of October. Valerius Vitalinus, Valerius Crispinianus, Aurelius M. Aureliustus, Aurelius I.M. Aurelius LaC., Aurelius Cassius S. Stats., Aurelius V. Aurelius Va. Cestius, N(o?) Valerius Cus(t?) Kaus, Iulius Do. Se. Vius, Aurelius u. V. Aurelius Victorinus, Iulius Euprorius, AVR. Aurelius Cuspis, Aurelius Aquila, Aurelia Marnusia, Aurelius Antoninus, Aurelius Lucianus.
EDCS-69200415.summary: This inscription records a group of individuals who collectively funded a shrine or monument to the Nymphs during the consulship of Aviola and the reign of Emperor Gordian, likely in the mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-69200417.text_edition: \n annorum LII BAI Sabina coniugi liberta \n
EDCS-69200417.translation: Sabina, freedwoman, [aged] 52 years, [erected this] for her husband.
EDCS-69200417.summary: This is a funerary inscription for an unnamed husband, dedicated by his freedwoman wife Sabina, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-69200421.translation: To Silvanus the holy. VPVT [dedicated this].
EDCS-69200421.summary: A dedication to the god Silvanus by an individual or group identified as VPVT, probably from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-69200427.summary: A dedication to a deity or possibly a person named Superius, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-69200430.translation: To A. Carvillius. May you live.
EDCS-69200430.summary: This is a dedication or a wish for good health to a person named A. Carvillius, probably from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-70300046.translation: Petto, son of Firminus, aged 50, and Cotiuvia, aged 40, and Bugia and Quartia, his daughters, made this; Rustius, son of Petto, who died aged [3?], and his brother Firminus, who died aged 50.
EDCS-70300046.summary: A funerary monument commemorating Petto, Cotiuvia, Bugia, Quartia, Rustius, and Firminus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-70600020.text_edition: \n c Maximus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-70600020.translation: Maximus willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-70600020.summary: A votive inscription recording Maximus fulfilling a vow, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-70600023.text_edition: \n Aesculapio et Apollini et Hygiae sacrum Claudius Verecundus medicus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-70600023.translation: Sacred to Asclepius, Apollo, and Hygieia; Claudius Verecundus, a physician, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-70600023.summary: A votive inscription dedicated by the physician Claudius Verecundus to the gods Asclepius, Apollo, and Hygieia, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-70700017.summary: A dedication by Lucius Annirius Rufus, likely for a tomb or a public building, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-70700025.translation: Lucius Cantius Fidus, freedman of Lucius, made this while alive for himself and for Cantia, daughter of Cantia, and Optata, freedwoman of Europa, aged 6.
EDCS-70700025.summary: A funerary dedication made by Lucius Cantius Fidus for himself and for his family members, including a young child, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-70700027.translation: To the spirits of the departed; [---] Sabinianus [---]ia Fus[---]it [---]
EDCS-70700027.summary: A fragmented funerary inscription dedicated to the departed, mentioning the name Sabinianus, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-70700029.summary: A fragment of an inscription, possibly funerary, mentioning the name Prisca, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-70700037.translation: To the Nymphs and to Fontanus; Gaius Hostilius Expertus.
EDCS-70700037.summary: A dedication by Gaius Hostilius Expertus to the Nymphs and the deity Fontanus, likely associated with a spring or water source, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-70700041.translation: Arius Nauportanus: 4 denarii for 4 amphorae, black hoops, 3 pedes.
EDCS-70700041.summary: A record of a transaction by Arius Nauportanus for the purchase of amphorae, likely a commercial inscription from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-70700042.translation: Varrius [---]arus: 3 and three-quarters pounds for a station.
EDCS-70700042.summary: A fragmented inscription recording a payment of 3 and three-quarters pounds by Varrius for a station, likely related to a public service or building, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-70700043.summary: This is a fragmentary epitaph for Gaius Coelius Statius, possibly recording a weight.

EDCS-70700044.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription, likely an epitaph, mentioning the name Vinicius and perhaps Tuniculas.

EDCS-71100026.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Caius Iulius Vero sic Maximinus Pius Felix Auggustus! pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate III consul proconsul pater patriae et Gaius Iulius Verus Maximus nobilissimus Caesar pontes et stratas vetustate conlapsas restituere iusserunt suis nominibus a Bregetione! milia passuum III\n
EDCS-71100026.translation: Emperor Caesar Gaius Julius Verus Maximinus Pius Felix Augustus, Pontifex Maximus, Tribune for the third time, Consul, Proconsul, Father of the Fatherland, and Gaius Julius Verus Maximus, most noble Caesar, ordered the bridges and roads collapsed by age to be restored, under their names, 3 miles from Bregetio.
EDCS-71100026.summary: This is an imperial inscription from the time of Maximinus Thrax and his son, dedicating the restoration of bridges and roads near Bregetio.

EDCS-71100091.inscription_type: diplomata militaria, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-71100091.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar divi Nervae filius Traianus Optimus Augustus Germanicus Dacicus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate XVIII<I> imperator VIIII consul VI pater patriae equitibus et peditibus qui militaverunt in alis quinque et cohortibus tribus quae appellantur I Batavorum |(milliaria) civium Romanorum pia fidelis et I Thracum veterana et I Cann<a=E>nefatium civium Romanorum et I Aravacorum et Hispanorum et I Bosporanorum et I et II Batavorum |(milliaria) civium Romanorum pia fidelis et V Callaecorum Lucensium quae sunt in Pannonia superiore sub Lucio Minicio Natale quinis et vicenis pluribusve stipendiis emeritis dimissis honesta missione quorum nomina subscripta sunt ipsis liberis posterisque eorum civitatem dedit et conubium cum uxoribus quas tunc habuissent cum civitas iis data aut si qui caelibes essent cum iis quas postea duxissent dumtaxat singuli singulas ante diem III Nonas Iulias Publio Iuventio Celso Lucio Iulio Frugi consulibus alae I Batavorum |(milliariae) civium Romanorum piae fidelis cui praefuit Quintus Iulius Bovius Avitus ex gregale Marco Ulpio Celsi filio Victori Batavo et Proculae filiae eius et Festae filiae eius descriptum et recognitum ex tabula aenea quae fixa est Romae in muro post templum divi Augusti ad Minervam Imperator Caesar divi Nervae filius Nerva Traianus Optimus Augustus Germanicus Dacicus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate XVIII<I> imperator VIIII consul VI pater patriae equitibus et peditibus qui militaverunt in alis quinque et cohortibus tribus quae appellantur I Batavorum |(milliaria) civium Romanorum pia fidelis et I Thracum veterana et I Cann<an>efatium civium Romanorum et I Aravacorum et Hispanorum et I Bosporanorum et I et II Batavorum |(milliaria) civium Romanorum pia fidelis et V Callaecorum Lucensium quae sunt in Pannonia superiore sub Lucio Minicio Natale quinis et vicenis pluribusve stipendiis emeritis dimissis honesta missione quorum nomina subscripta sunt ipsis liberis posterisque eorum civitatem dedit et conubium cum uxoribus quas tunc habuissent cum est civitas iis data aut si qui caelibes essent cum iis quas postea duxissent dumtaxat singuli singulas ante diem III Nonas Iulias Publio Iuventio Celso Lucio Iulio Frugi consulibus alae I Batavorum |(milliariae) civium Romanorum piae fidelis cui praefuit Quintus Iulius Bovius Avitus ex gregale Marco Ulpio Celsi filio Victori Batavo et Proculae filiae eius et Festae filiae eius Luci Pulli Verecundi Publi Cauli Vitalis Publi Atini Crescentis Cai Vettieni Hermetis Luci Pulli Charitonis Auli Cascelli Proculi Cai Tuticani Crescentis \n
EDCS-71100091.translation: Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Nerva, Trajan Optimus Augustus, Germanicus, Dacicus, Pontifex Maximus, Tribune for the 18th time, Imperator for the 9th time, Consul for the 6th time, Father of the Fatherland, granted citizenship and the right to marry to the cavalrymen and foot soldiers who served in the five alae and three cohorts named I Batavorum Milliaria Civium Romanorum Pia Fidelis, I Thracum Veterana, I Cannanefatium Civium Romanorum, I Aravacorum et Hispanorum, I Bosporanorum, I and II Batavorum Milliaria Civium Romanorum Pia Fidelis, and V Callaecorum Lucensium, which are stationed in Pannonia Superior, having completed twenty-five or more years of service and having been honorably discharged, whose names are listed below, to them, their children, and their descendants, with the wives they then had when citizenship was granted, or if they were single, with the wives they would later marry, provided each man took only one wife. This was decreed on the 3rd day before the Nones of July, when Publius Iuventius Celsus and Lucius Julius Frugi were consuls. For the Ala I Batavorum Milliaria Civium Romanorum Pia Fidelis, commanded by Quintus Julius Bovius Avitus, from a common soldier Marcus Ulpius Victor, son of Celsus, a Batavian, and his daughter Procula and his daughter Festa. This decree was copied and verified from a bronze tablet fixed in Rome on the wall behind the Temple of the Deified Augustus, near the Temple of Minerva. Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Nerva, Nerva Trajan Optimus Augustus, Germanicus, Dacicus, Pontifex Maximus, Tribune for the 18th time, Imperator for the 9th time, Consul for the 6th time, Father of the Fatherland, granted citizenship and the right to marry to the cavalrymen and foot soldiers who served in the five alae and three cohorts named I Batavorum Milliaria Civium Romanorum Pia Fidelis, I Thracum Veterana, I Cannanefatium Civium Romanorum, I Aravacorum et Hispanorum, I Bosporanorum, I and II Batavorum Milliaria Civium Romanorum Pia Fidelis, and V Callaecorum Lucensium, which are stationed in Pannonia Superior, having completed twenty-five or more years of service and having been honorably discharged, whose names are listed below, to them, their children, and their descendants, with the wives they then had when citizenship was granted, or if they were single, with the wives they would later marry, provided each man took only one wife. This was decreed on the 3rd day before the Nones of July, when Publius Iuventius Celsus and Lucius Julius Frugi were consuls. For the Ala I Batavorum Milliaria Civium Romanorum Pia Fidelis, commanded by Quintus Julius Bovius Avitus, from a common soldier Marcus Ulpius Victor, son of Celsus, a Batavian, and his daughter Procula and his daughter Festa, Lucius Pullius Verecundus, Publius Caelius Vitalis, Publius Atinius Crescens, Gaius Vettienus Hermetis, Lucius Pullius Charito, Aulus Cascellius Proculus, Gaius Tuticanus Crescens.
EDCS-71100091.summary: This is a grant of citizenship and marriage rights by Emperor Trajan to veteran soldiers of several cavalry and infantry units stationed in Pannonia Superior, dated to 109 AD.

EDCS-71200496.translation: ...may the earth lie lightly on you.
EDCS-71200496.summary: This is a fragment of an epitaph, possibly for a soldier of Legio I Adjutrix, with a common funerary wish.

EDCS-71300218.text_edition: \n viva fecit filia Florentina coniugi et FIBCL Florentino E annorum XXV\n
EDCS-71300218.translation: His daughter Florentina made this for her husband, Florentinus, aged 25.
EDCS-71300218.summary: This is an epitaph erected by a daughter for her deceased husband, who died at the age of 25.

EDCS-71400599.translation: Our Lord Magnentius, Pious, Happy, Augustus... Aquileia, consulship.
EDCS-71400599.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription mentioning the emperor Magnentius, likely dating to his reign (350-353 AD).

EDCS-71500028.summary: This is a very brief epitaph, stating that Marcellina mourns someone.

EDCS-72200103.summary: This is a dedication recording a vow made to the god Silvanus.

EDCS-72200104.summary: This is a very short invocation addressed to the god Silvanus.

EDCS-72200105.translation: To Silvanus, the Holy God, the Traveler.
EDCS-72200105.summary: This is a dedication to the god Silvanus, likely from a traveler, possibly on a road or in a wild area.

EDCS-72200106.translation: I call upon Silvanus the Traveler.
EDCS-72200106.summary: This inscription appears to be a personal invocation to the god Silvanus, possibly made by a traveler.

EDCS-72200168.translation: Telesphorus, Augustalis of the colony of Ulpia Traiana Poetovionensis, for his own health and that of Flavia, ordered to be made.
EDCS-72200168.summary: This is a funerary or dedication text for Telesphorus, an Augustalis from Poetovio, mentioning his own health and that of Flavia, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-72200170.translation: Masculus(?) and his sons Clementis, aged 50, and another, aged 3, had this made.
EDCS-72200170.summary: This fragmented inscription records the construction of a monument by Masculus and his sons, Clementis and another, likely a tomb, for the deceased, probably dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-72200171.translation: Titus Varius Ianuarius and Varia Luteina, his wife, while alive, made this for themselves and for their daughter Varia Ianuaria, aged 25.
EDCS-72200171.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Titus Varius Ianuarius and Varia Luteina, who dedicated their tomb for themselves and their deceased daughter, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-72200176.text_edition: \n Silvanis Augustis sacrum Pompeia Florentina pro se et suis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-72200176.translation: Sacred to the August Silvanes. Pompeia Florentina willingly fulfilled her vow for herself and her family, as was deserved.
EDCS-72200176.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to the plural Silvanes by Pompeia Florentina, possibly for her family's well-being, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-72200177.text_edition: \n Vidaso et Thanae sacrum Quintus Domitius Ingenuus pro se et suis ex voto\n
EDCS-72200177.translation: Sacred to Vidasus and Thana. Quintus Domitius Ingenuus, according to his vow, offered this for himself and his family.
EDCS-72200177.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to the deities Vidasus and Thana by Quintus Domitius Ingenuus for himself and his family, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-72300235.summary: This is a very fragmented inscription, possibly a name or part of a dedication, making its exact purpose unclear.

EDCS-72400166.summary: This is a highly fragmented inscription that seems to record the age of a deceased person, likely a child, with the number 'eleven' being partially visible.

EDCS-72400173.translation: Voconius ... the people of Teshi... LXXV denarii.
EDCS-72400173.summary: This inscription seems to record a payment of 75 denarii by Voconius, possibly related to a transaction or a public office, with a reference to people from Teshi, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-72400183.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription, possibly recording the maker of an object or monument, dating from the Roman period.

EDCS-72400195.summary: A short and fragmented inscription, likely a personal name and a note on age or condition, from the Roman period.

EDCS-72400217.text_edition: \n Caius Antonius Sentinus veteranus legionis XIIII geminae Martiae victricis annorum L heres faciendum curavit h c\n
EDCS-72400217.translation: Gaius Antonius Sentinus, a veteran of the Fourteenth Legion Gemina Martia Victrix, aged 50, his heir arranged for this to be made.
EDCS-72400217.summary: A funerary epitaph for a 50-year-old veteran of the Legio XIV Gemina, erected by his heir, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-72400224.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo sacrum Lucius Septimius Maiorinus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-72400224.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Lucius Septimius Maiorinus gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-72400224.summary: A votive dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Lucius Septimius Maiorinus, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-72400231.text_edition: \n Licinia Publi filia Iusta annorum L testamento fieri iussit et Lucio Valerio Luci liberti Ialisso coniugi annorum LX Salvius Tatiianus arbitratu Titi Licini Valentis et Titi Licini Catonis et Titi Licini CCOF\n
EDCS-72400231.translation: Licinia Iusta, daughter of Publius, aged 50, ordered this to be made by her will, and for her husband Lucius Valerius Ialisso, freedman of Lucius, aged 60, Salvius Tatianus supervising on the authority of Titus Licinius Valens, Titus Licinius Caton, and Titus Licinius [filius] CCOF.
EDCS-72400231.summary: A funerary epitaph for a woman named Licinia Iusta and her husband Lucius Valerius Ialisso, with details of their ages and the executors of the will, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-72400232.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo sacrum Caius Marcius Avitus votum solvit libens laetus merito\n
EDCS-72400232.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Gaius Marcius Avitus gladly, joyfully, and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-72400232.summary: A votive dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Gaius Marcius Avitus, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-72400237.text_edition: \n ivit bis ire EA L SA D permanent IEIN Nymphis est liquor e fons scatit crispo fertur flumine rotanti sese igneo caloris vasto inpetu sic qui natus prosperis et lucis filo splendido mundumque hospes contigit huc destinatus advenit o si liceret iudici ut quies esset libera his sempiternis uterer quis metra nulla nascitur hoc Paphus libens dixerim cui Palatiano nomen est amore magno huc veni gravique abscessum sero\n
EDCS-72400237.translation: He lived twice, he went, he endures, EA L SA D permanent, IEIN Nymphs it is the flowing, the spring gushes with crisp river, it is borne with a turning river, in a fiery rush of vast heat, so he who was born in prosperity and touched the world as a guest with a bright thread of light, arrived here destined, oh if it were permitted to the judge that there be free eternal rest, I would use these everlasting [things], no measure is born, I would gladly say Paphos was born here, whose name is Palatiano, with great love I came here and departed late from a heavy [burden].
EDCS-72400237.summary: A lengthy and highly poetic epigram, possibly a funerary inscription or a dedication to deities associated with springs and nature, expressing a desire for eternal peace and reflecting on life's journey, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-72400239.text_edition: \n Iunoni Reginae et Minervae Apollini et Nymphis \n
EDCS-72400239.translation: To Juno Regina, Minerva, Apollo, and the Nymphs.
EDCS-72400239.summary: A dedication to several deities, including Juno Regina, Minerva, Apollo, and the Nymphs, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-72400241.text_edition: \n Apollini Dianae et Nymphis Lucius Aerius Florentinus speculator legionis XIII gemina? \n
EDCS-72400241.translation: To Apollo, Diana, and the Nymphs, Lucius Aerius Florentinus, a speculator of the Thirteenth Legion Gemina?
EDCS-72400241.summary: A dedication to Apollo, Diana, and the Nymphs by Lucius Aerius Florentinus, a speculator (scout or bodyguard) of the Legio XIII Gemina, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-72400243.text_edition: \n Iustus Lucius Septimius Karus Caius Iulius Ingenuus Caius Iulius Heliodorus PAL Caius Iulius Vitalis Marcus Minicius Surillio Marcus Aurelius Staphylus Iulius Cresce Caius Iulius Florianus Marcus Aurelius Sossianus Marcus Ulpius Tertius Titus Iulius Tutorianus Marcus Antonius Ianuarius Gaius Antonius Valentinus MAI II CL Lucius ius IIIS Lucius Iulius Sabinus Gaius Marc(ius) Marcellus Caius Valerius Dubitatus immunis MINIVVS IIENV rius ARA Certius IIVL MNI Marcus Ulpius ACI Marcus Aurelius IVCO Marcus RAN VRCLI tronus N CRESCISIVS IAIIINV\n
EDCS-72400243.translation: Iustus Lucius Septimius Karus, Gaius Julius Ingenuus, Gaius Julius Heliodorus PAL, Gaius Julius Vitalis, Marcus Minicius Surillio, Marcus Aurelius Staphylus, Iulius Cresce, Gaius Julius Florianus, Marcus Aurelius Sossianus, Marcus Ulpius Tertius, Titus Iulius Tutorianus, Marcus Antonius Ianuarius, Gaius Antonius Valentinus MAI II CL, Lucius Iulius IIIS, Lucius Iulius Sabinus, Gaius Marcius Marcellus, Gaius Valerius Dubitatus immunis MINIVVS IIENV rius ARA Certius IIVL MNI, Marcus Ulpius ACI, Marcus Aurelius IVCO, Marcus RAN VRCLI tronus N CRESCISIVS IAIIINV
EDCS-72400243.summary: A long list of names, possibly recording members of a collegium, military unit, or other group, with some abbreviations possibly indicating rank or status, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-72400244.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus OI Aelia ulla coniugi \n
EDCS-72400244.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For Aelia Ulla, his wife.
EDCS-72400244.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Aelia Ulla, likely set up by her husband, dating from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-72900106.summary: This short funerary inscription is a wish for Verina, probably on a tombstone or a personal item.

EDCS-73000033.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For Felici, who lived 15 days and... X days... who lived... 23 days... Aug.
EDCS-73000033.summary: This fragmentary epitaph records the short lifespans of two individuals, likely children, with the dedication 'Dis Manibus'.

EDCS-74100177.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For Gaius Titius Vero Ticinus, son of Gaius Papirius, a soldier of the VII Praetorian cohort, Octavius' century, with 18 years of service, aged 35 years, 7 months, and 19 days. Titus Trabenna Marcellinus, his fiduciary heir in the name of Titus Sotericus, his patron, made this for him who deserved well.
EDCS-74100177.summary: This is a detailed military funerary epitaph for Gaius Titius Vero Ticinus, a praetorian guardsman, who died at age 35, with his heir and patron commissioning the monument.

EDCS-74200133.summary: This is a very fragmentary epitaph recording the age of a deceased named Ascani.

EDCS-74200135.translation: Marcus, son of Gaius T..., aged 26...
EDCS-74200135.summary: This fragmentary inscription records the name and age of a deceased individual, Marcus.

EDCS-74200138.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Gaius Marcius ordered this to be made by his will for himself and for his wife Modestina and for their son Vindicius, aged...
EDCS-74200138.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Gaius Marcius, who commissioned the monument in his will for himself, his wife Modestina, and their son Vindicius.

EDCS-74200141.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. ... Claudia, from Inicerum, prefect of the I Ala of the Pannonians...
EDCS-74200141.summary: This is a fragment of a funerary inscription for Claudia, who held the rank of prefect of an auxiliary cavalry unit.

EDCS-75000365.translation: So in the tenth year, so in the twentieth year. Licinius, may you always be victorious. Ermes the vase-maker made and signed it.
EDCS-75000365.summary: This inscription, likely a dedication on a vase or athletic equipment, is a wish for victory for Licinius, made by the craftsman Ermes.

EDCS-75100029.translation: Thus Atilies, thus Firmes, thus Karnuntinae.
EDCS-75100029.summary: This inscription lists three names, possibly related to individuals or places, with the repetition of 'sic' suggesting comparison or a sequence.

EDCS-75300249.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Gaius Julius Verus Maximinus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, pontifex maximus, from Brigetio.
EDCS-75300249.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Maximinus from the settlement of Brigetio, likely erected by local officials or inhabitants. The date is inferable to his reign (235-238 AD).

EDCS-75300250.translation: Flavius Maximus, Secundus, Respecta [---]. Caius Septimius Crescens [---] Vercius [---] fifth month as a slave, soldiers, magistrates [---] are first.
EDCS-75300250.summary: This inscription appears to be a fragmented epitaph or record, possibly mentioning individuals like Flavius Maximus and Caius Septimius Crescens, with unclear context regarding their status or achievements.

EDCS-75300252.translation: who served in the sacred retinue; Vitula, daughter; Sabinianus, grandson; Aurelia Lucilla [---] placed.
EDCS-75300252.summary: This fragmented inscription likely records family members, possibly serving in a military or religious capacity, with Vitula, her grandson Sabinianus, and Aurelia Lucilla being mentioned. The context suggests a dedication or memorial.

EDCS-75600108.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Quinto Caetronio Martiniano optio spei legionis X gemina nnorum XXX stipendiorum X Quintus Caetronius Asper librarius legati legionis supra scriptae fratri bene merenti et Caetronio Iuliano signiferi legionis supra? scriptae? avonculo sic vivo \n
EDCS-75600108.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Quintus Caetronius Martinianus, optio of the guard of the X Gemina legion, aged 30, with 10 years of service, to his well-deserving brother. Quintus Caetronius Asper, clerk of the legate of the aforementioned legion, and to his uncle Caetronius Julianus, standard-bearer of the aforementioned legion, while he was alive.
EDCS-75600108.summary: This is a tombstone set up by Quintus Caetronius Asper for his brother Quintus Caetronius Martinianus, an optio in the X Gemina legion, and for his uncle Caetronius Julianus, a standard-bearer.

EDCS-76200041.translation: Sammus, son of Magiovinidus, aged 40, lies here. He remained in Germania. Briga, daughter of Vincio, erected this for herself and her husband while alive.
EDCS-76200041.summary: This tombstone records Sammus, son of Magiovinidus, who died in Germania, and was placed by his wife Briga, daughter of Vincio, for herself and her husband.

EDCS-78200047.summary: This is a fragmented inscription recording the name and age of a deceased individual named Ianuaria, likely from a tombstone.

EDCS-78200049.summary: This inscription is very fragmentary and possibly indicates a public land grant or concession, with the abbreviation L() having an unclear meaning.

EDCS-78300209.summary: This is a dedication to the goddess Nemesis Augusta, with the name likely being Septima, possibly erected by an individual or a group.

EDCS-78300211.summary: This inscription is highly fragmented and mentions personal names, possibly Attonius Pis[---], with the initial letters being illegible.

EDCS-79600110.translation: [---] Adnamat [---] / erected this memorial tablet. [---] lies here.
EDCS-79600110.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription indicating a memorial tablet erected by someone named Adnamat, with the text recording that a person is buried there.

EDCS-80300028.translation: Of black olives. Made by Marcus Mulvius Sec[1].
EDCS-80300028.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription that likely records a commodity (black olives) and a name, possibly related to a sale or ownership marker, from the Roman period.

EDCS-80300060.translation: Latinus made it. Made it. Latinus made it. Made it.
EDCS-80300060.summary: This inscription, a repetitive statement of creation, likely marks an object made by a craftsman named Latinus, dating to the Roman period.

EDCS-80300061.translation: Latinus made it. Made it. Legion XIIII.
EDCS-80300061.summary: This inscription indicates that an object was made by Latinus and is associated with Legion XIIII, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-80300062.translation: Latinus made it. Made it. Legion XIIII.
EDCS-80300062.summary: This inscription indicates that an object was made by Latinus and is associated with Legion XIIII, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-81300028.translation: Legion. XIIII. Legion. XIIII. Latinus made it. Made it. Latinus made it. Made it.
EDCS-81300028.summary: This inscription, with repeated phrases, associates an object made by Latinus with Legion XIIII, suggesting it was military equipment or property, dating to the Roman period.

EDCS-81700015.summary: This inscription records a quantity of wool (cara lana, potentially a specific type or quality) weighing nine pounds, likely related to trade or inventory in the Roman period.

EDCS-82300098.translation: Emperor Caesar Severus Pius, grandson of the deified Severus, son of the deified Antoninus, Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Pius Felix Augustus, supreme pontiff, holding tribunician power for the 8th time, consul for the 3rd time, proconsul, father of the country, restored bridges and roads collapsed from age, from Vindobona, 33 miles.
EDCS-82300098.summary: This imperial dedication, dating to the reign of Severus Alexander (c. 222-235 AD), records his restoration of bridges and roads over a distance of 33 miles from Vindobona, likely commemorating a public works project.
